## Copilot

Your `.vbproj` block you pasted is fine **now** (it generates into `$(IntermediateOutputPath)` a.k.a. `obj\...`), so the *remaining* reason you’re still getting:

> Duplicate 'Compile' items were included … duplicate items were: 'BuildInfo.g.vb'

is that you almost certainly still have an **old `BuildInfo.g.vb` sitting in your project directory** (same folder as `AMiOffice.vbproj`, or somewhere under the project tree). The SDK includes `**\*.vb` from the project directory by default, so that stray file gets compiled implicitly, and then your `Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)"` compiles the generated one too.

### Do this to fix it definitively

## 1) Delete the stray `BuildInfo.g.vb` from the project folder
In Solution Explorer:
- Click **Show All Files**
- Look for `BuildInfo.g.vb` anywhere under the project (especially next to the `.vbproj`)
- If it exists there:
  - **Delete it** from disk (best), or at least **Exclude From Project**

Also check the filesystem directly in File Explorer in your project folder for `BuildInfo.g.vb`.

## 2) Ensure `BuildInfo.g.vb` is NOT included as a normal project file
If you see `BuildInfo.g.vb` listed under your project in Solution Explorer (not under `obj`), remove it:
- right-click → **Exclude From Project**, then delete the file.

You want the only generated file to live under:
- `obj\Debug\net10.0-windows\BuildInfo.g.vb` (or Release)

## 3) Clean and rebuild
- **Build → Clean Solution**
- Delete the `bin` and `obj` folders manually (this helps when stale files linger)
- **Build → Rebuild Solution**

## 4) Remove your manual `BuildInfo.vb` (important)
Since you’re generating a `Friend Module BuildInfo` now, you should **not** also have a manual `BuildInfo.vb` that defines `Module BuildInfo`. Delete/rename the manual one, otherwise you’ll get the ambiguity error again (BC31429).

---

### If you want an extra guardrail (optional)
Add this to your `.vbproj` to *force* `BuildInfo.g.vb` in the project directory to never compile if it somehow appears again:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
</ItemGroup>
```

(Only needed if you keep accidentally generating/copying that file into the project folder.)

---

If you tell me **where** the `BuildInfo.g.vb` file is located on disk when you search (project root vs obj\...), I can tell you exactly which one is being double-included.

## @testsubject117

buildinfo.g.vb exists directly under AMiOffice in solution explorer. If I right click and choose exclude from project, nothing happens

## Copilot

That file being **directly under the project** is exactly what triggers the duplicate compile-item problem. Excluding it “does nothing” because in SDK-style projects, `*.vb` files are included by the default glob even if they aren’t individually listed—so it will keep coming back as long as the file exists on disk under the project folder.

Do this instead:

## 1) Delete `BuildInfo.g.vb` from disk (not just from the project)
1. In Solution Explorer, right-click `BuildInfo.g.vb` → **Open Containing Folder**
2. Close Visual Studio (or at least unload the project)
3. Delete `BuildInfo.g.vb` in File Explorer
4. Reopen Visual Studio

That removes the file so the implicit `Compile Include="**\*.vb"` can’t pick it up.

## 2) Ensure your generated file is only in `obj\`
With your current `.vbproj`:

```xml
<BuildInfoFile>$(IntermediateOutputPath)BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
```

the file that should be generated is:
- `obj\Debug\net10.0-windows\BuildInfo.g.vb` (or Release)

You should *not* have any `BuildInfo.g.vb` in the project root.

## 3) Clean out stale build outputs
After deleting the root file:

- Delete folders `bin\` and `obj\` (from File Explorer)
- Then **Build → Rebuild**

## 4) Prevent it from ever compiling if it reappears (recommended safety)
Add this to the `.vbproj` (anywhere before `</Project>`):

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
</ItemGroup>
```

This tells the SDK: “even if a `BuildInfo.g.vb` shows up under the project directory, do not compile it.”

## 5) Also: remove the manual duplicate module
Make sure you don’t have both:
- `BuildInfo.vb` (manual `Module BuildInfo`) **and**
- the generated `BuildInfo.g.vb` (generated `Module BuildInfo`)

You should keep only the generated one. If you want to keep a manual file, rename it to something else (e.g. `BuildInfoManual`) and don’t define `Module BuildInfo` inside it.

---

If you want, paste the first ~5 lines of the **root** `BuildInfo.g.vb` (the one under AMiOffice). I’ll confirm whether it’s the old generated file and whether anything else is writing it there.

## @testsubject117

I closed visual studio, deleted the file, opened visual studio and chose excluded from project then chose rebuild. The file is appearing in solution explorer again under AMiOffice, and appears to have been recreated in file explorer

## Copilot

Something in your build is still writing **`BuildInfo.g.vb` into the project folder** (not `obj\`). That’s why it keeps reappearing and why “Exclude from project” can’t stick.

With the `.vbproj` you pasted, the `WriteLinesToFile` *should* write to:

- `$(IntermediateOutputPath)BuildInfo.g.vb` → `obj\...\BuildInfo.g.vb`

So if it’s being recreated in the project root, one of these is true:

1) You have **another generation step** (a pre-build event, post-build event, custom target, script, etc.) writing `BuildInfo.g.vb` to the project directory, **or**
2) `$(IntermediateOutputPath)` is somehow ending up as the project directory (unlikely in normal SDK projects), **or**
3) There’s a second `.targets`/`.props` file in the solution overriding `BuildInfoFile`.

### The fastest, guaranteed fix (even if something keeps recreating it)
Add a rule to your `.vbproj` that **removes the root file from compilation**, and (optionally) deletes it during build.

Add this near the bottom of your `.vbproj`, before `</Project>`:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <!-- If BuildInfo.g.vb gets created in the project directory, do not compile it -->
  <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
</ItemGroup>

<Target Name="DeleteRootBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
  <!-- Safety: delete the stray generated file in the project root if it exists -->
  <Delete Files="$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\BuildInfo.g.vb" />
</Target>
```

This does two things:
- Prevents the duplicate compile error (root file won’t be compiled)
- Keeps the project tree clean by deleting the stray file each build

### Also: confirm your “real” generated file still compiles
Keep your existing block:

```xml
<BuildInfoFile>$(IntermediateOutputPath)BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
...
<Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
```

That ensures the obj-generated one still compiles.

---

## Next: identify what is recreating the root file
To pinpoint it, check these in Visual Studio:

1) **Project Properties → Build Events**
   - Pre-build event command line
   - Post-build event command line  
   (Look for anything mentioning `BuildInfo.g.vb`)

2) Search your entire solution folder for `BuildInfo.g.vb` references:
   - “Find in Files” for: `BuildInfo.g.vb`
   - Also search for: `WriteLinesToFile` and `GenerateBuildInfo`

3) Check for these files in your repo/solution folder:
   - `Directory.Build.props`
   - `Directory.Build.targets`
   - any `.targets` imported by the project

Those can override paths or add extra build targets.

---

### One quick question
When the file reappears, is it **exactly** at:
- `<project folder>\BuildInfo.g.vb`
or is it in a subfolder?

And can you paste the first ~10 lines of the *recreated root* `BuildInfo.g.vb`? That will tell us whether it’s your MSBuild target writing it, or some other generator.

## @testsubject117

it is exactly at project folder\buildinfo.g.vb and now the file is not showing in solution explorer but I'm getting errors BC30451 Buildinfo is declared. It may be inaccessible due to it's protection level

## Copilot

That new error means the opposite problem now: **your project is compiling, but the generated `BuildInfo` module is not being compiled/visible**, so references like `BuildInfo.VersionText` can’t be resolved.

Given what you described (root `BuildInfo.g.vb` exists on disk but not in Solution Explorer), what likely happened is:

- The root `BuildInfo.g.vb` is being **removed/excluded**, and
- The *intended* generated file under `obj\...\BuildInfo.g.vb` is **not actually being included**, or the target isn’t running when the compile items are evaluated.

Let’s put you on a stable, known-good configuration.

## Do this: simplify to one generated file in `obj\` and ensure it compiles

### 1) Update your `.vbproj` to this exact pattern
Replace your BuildInfo-related bits with the following (remove any other BuildInfo targets/includes you added earlier):

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildInfoFile>$(IntermediateOutputPath)BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
</PropertyGroup>

<Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss 'UTC'"))</BuildTimestamp>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <WriteLinesToFile
    File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
    Overwrite="true"
    Lines=
"Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = &quot;$(BuildTimestamp)&quot;
    Friend Const VersionText As String = &quot;$(Version)&quot;
End Module
" />

  <!-- IMPORTANT: add the generated file to compilation AFTER it is created -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Include=&quot;$(BuildInfoFile)&quot; Visible=&quot;false&quot; />
  </ItemGroup>
</Target>

<ItemGroup>
  <!-- If a stray BuildInfo.g.vb appears in the project root, never compile it -->
  <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
</ItemGroup>
```

Key change vs what you had: the `<Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />` is moved **inside the Target**, so it’s added after the file is generated. This avoids timing issues where the compiler item list is evaluated before the file exists.

### 2) Delete any manual `BuildInfo.vb`
Make sure you do **not** have a manual `Friend Module BuildInfo` anymore. Only the generated one should exist.

### 3) Clean hard
- Close Visual Studio
- Delete `bin` and `obj` folders
- Delete `<project folder>\BuildInfo.g.vb` if it exists
- Reopen VS
- Rebuild

## Why you got “BuildInfo is declared…inaccessible”
Once `BuildInfo` isn’t being compiled, VB treats `BuildInfo` as an unresolved identifier (BC30451). The “protection level” wording can also appear when there’s a namespace/type confusion, but in your case it’s almost certainly “module not in compilation”.

---

## Quick confirmation question
In `FormMainMenu.vb`, are you referencing it exactly as `BuildInfo.VersionText` (capital I), or `Buildinfo.VersionText`? VB is case-insensitive, but I want to ensure you’re not referencing a different name than the module.

If you paste the exact line(s) where the BC30451 occurs, I’ll tell you whether it’s “module not compiled” vs “namespace conflict” and adjust accordingly.

## @testsubject117

here is AMiOffice.vbproj code, please review then regenerate the entire file with the needed code so I can copy and paste the entire file

## Copilot

Paste-ready `.vbproj` below. This version:

- Generates `BuildInfo.g.vb` **only into `obj\...`** (IntermediateOutputPath)
- Adds the generated file to `Compile` **inside the target** (so it’s included even though it doesn’t exist at evaluation time)
- Explicitly **removes** any stray `BuildInfo.g.vb` sitting in the project root from compilation
- Keeps your existing settings and resource entries

Before you rebuild:
1) Delete **any manual `BuildInfo.vb`** that defines `Friend Module BuildInfo`
2) Delete `<project folder>\BuildInfo.g.vb` if it exists
3) Delete `bin\` and `obj\`, then rebuild

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>
    <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
    <MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>

    <AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
    <FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>

    <GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>

    <!-- Optional: informational version for display/logging -->
    <InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- If a stray BuildInfo.g.vb appears in the project root, never compile it -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Generate build info at build time into obj\... and compile it -->
  <PropertyGroup>
    <BuildInfoFile>$(IntermediateOutputPath)BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <!-- UTC is stable across machines/timezones -->
      <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss 'UTC'"))</BuildTimestamp>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines=
"Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = &quot;$(BuildTimestamp)&quot;
    Friend Const VersionText As String = &quot;$(Version)&quot;
End Module
" />

    <!-- Add generated file to compilation AFTER it is created -->
    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Include=&quot;$(BuildInfoFile)&quot; Visible=&quot;false&quot; />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Remove="ESC Logo.jpg" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Include="ESC Logo.jpg">
      <CopyToOutputDirectory>Never</CopyToOutputDirectory>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Import Include="System.Data" />
    <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
    <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Update="My Project\Application.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Application.myapp</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Resources.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Resources.resx</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Update="My Project\Resources.resx">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My.Resources</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>VbMyResourcesResXFileCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Resources.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Update="My Project\Application.myapp">
      <Generator>MyApplicationCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Application.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>
```

After this, your code usage stays simple:

```vbnet
Dim v = BuildInfo.VersionText
Dim built = BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp
```

If you still see a root-level `BuildInfo.g.vb` being recreated, tell me **what created it** (pre-build/post-build events, or an external script). The project above will ignore it for compilation, but it’s still worth removing the source of that extra file generation.

## @testsubject117

that threw all kinds of errors

## Copilot

Understood. The full-file replacement likely broke because your project depends on other implicit/legacy settings that weren’t in the snippet (or because `net10.0-windows`/WinForms VB has some quirks and the generated compile include inside the target interacted badly).

Let’s recover safely: **don’t replace the whole `.vbproj`**. Instead, revert it and apply a minimal, proven patch.

## Step 0: Revert
In Visual Studio: right‑click the project → **Edit Project File** and undo your last change (or restore from source control / backup). Build once to confirm you’re back to the “known state” (even if it has the BuildInfo error).

## Step 1: Use the simplest working method on SDK-style projects
For SDK-style projects, you do **not** need to explicitly include the generated file if it’s in the project directory—BUT you *also* don’t want it there. So:

### A) Generate into `obj\` and do **NOT** add `<Compile Include=...>`
Instead, we’ll add the generated file via `Compile` item **Update** (safe) *or* rely on an analyzer? No—best minimal approach is:

1) Generate into `obj\`
2) Add it to compilation with `@(Compile)` using `ItemGroup` **outside** the target (but avoid duplicates)
3) Ensure there is no root `BuildInfo.g.vb`

However, you hit “duplicate compile items” earlier because a root file existed.

So here’s the **minimal change** that normally works and won’t “throw all kinds of errors”:

### Minimal patch to add to your existing `.vbproj`
Add **only this** near the bottom (before `</Project>`), without deleting anything else:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildInfoFile>$(IntermediateOutputPath)BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
</PropertyGroup>

<ItemGroup>
  <!-- Never compile a stray root BuildInfo.g.vb -->
  <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
  <!-- Compile the generated one under obj -->
  <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
</ItemGroup>

<Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss 'UTC'"))</BuildTimestamp>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <WriteLinesToFile
    File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
    Overwrite="true"
    Lines=
"Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = &quot;$(BuildTimestamp)&quot;
    Friend Const VersionText As String = &quot;$(Version)&quot;
End Module
" />
</Target>
```

## Step 2: Delete the *root* file that keeps getting recreated
You said `<project folder>\BuildInfo.g.vb` keeps coming back. That will cause confusion (and sometimes duplicates).

After applying the minimal patch:
1) Close VS
2) Delete `<project folder>\BuildInfo.g.vb`
3) Delete `bin\` and `obj\`
4) Reopen VS and rebuild

If the root file still reappears, it’s coming from **some other generator** (pre/post build event, external script, etc.). In that case we can add a *single* line to delete it during build—but only after we confirm what’s generating it.

---

## I need the exact error list to proceed correctly
When you say “all kinds of errors”, please paste:
1) The **first 10–20 lines** of the build output (the topmost errors), and
2) The **first error code** that appears (e.g., BCxxxx / MSBxxxx) and the file it points to.

Also: are you currently targeting **`net10.0-windows`** exactly, or was that a typo and it’s `net8.0-windows` / `net9.0-windows`? (That matters a lot.)

## @testsubject117

never mind about all kinds of errors. For now let's focus on the BuildInfo.g.vb being recreated again after I did what you said (excluded from project, deleted it from file explorer, and upon restarting VB and rebuilding, it's there again)

## Copilot

If `BuildInfo.g.vb` is reappearing in the **project root** after a rebuild, then **something in your build process is explicitly writing it there**. “Exclude from project” can’t stop that because it’s a disk write, not a VS setting.

At this point the goal is twofold:

1) **Identify what is generating** `<project>\BuildInfo.g.vb`
2) **Stop it** (or at least force it into `obj\`)

## 1) Find the generator (fast checks)

### A) Check Build Events (most common)
In Visual Studio:
- Right‑click project → **Properties**
- **Build Events** tab
- Look at:
  - **Pre-build event command line**
  - **Post-build event command line**

If you see anything referencing `BuildInfo.g.vb` or writing a `.vb` file, that’s it.

### B) Search the solution folder for the filename
In Visual Studio: **Find in Files** (Ctrl+Shift+F)
Search for:

- `BuildInfo.g.vb`
- `WriteLinesToFile`
- `GenerateBuildInfo`
- `BuildInfoFile`

Scope: **Entire Solution** (and include `.props`, `.targets`, `.cmd`, `.ps1`).

If it appears in:
- `Directory.Build.targets`
- `Directory.Build.props`
- any imported `.targets`

…those will run automatically and can recreate the file.

### C) Check if the file is coming from your own `.vbproj`
Open the *root* `BuildInfo.g.vb` that gets recreated and look at its contents.

- If it contains your exact template text (`Friend Module BuildInfo`, `BuildTimestamp`, etc.), then **your MSBuild target is still writing to the root** (meaning `BuildInfoFile` is resolving to the project directory somewhere).
- If it has different content (designer-ish comments, tool headers, etc.), it’s being generated by something else.

## 2) Hard-stop: prevent root BuildInfo.g.vb from compiling (so it can’t break your build)
Even before we find the source, add this to your `.vbproj` (anywhere before `</Project>`):

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
</ItemGroup>
```

This doesn’t stop creation, but it **stops it from affecting compilation**.

## 3) Hard-stop: delete it automatically during build (so it stops reappearing)
Add this too:

```xml
<Target Name="DeleteRootBuildInfoG" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
  <Delete Files="$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\BuildInfo.g.vb" />
</Target>
```

That guarantees it’s gone by the time compilation runs (even if something created it earlier).

## The one piece of info that will tell us the real cause
Paste the first ~20 lines of the **recreated** `<project>\BuildInfo.g.vb` (the one in the project folder).  
From those lines I can tell whether it’s your MSBuild `WriteLinesToFile` generating it, or a different tool/script.

Also tell me:
- Do you have `Directory.Build.props` or `Directory.Build.targets` anywhere in the solution folder or parent folders? (Even one directory above the solution counts.)

## Copilot

Yes—I saw it. Your `AMiOffice.vbproj` contains:

- `<BuildInfoFile>$(IntermediateOutputPath)BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>`
- a `<Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" ...>` that uses `WriteLinesToFile File="$(BuildInfoFile)"`

So **based on that XML**, *your* target should be generating the file under `obj\...`, not in the project root.

That’s exactly why the project-root `AMiOffice\BuildInfo.g.vb` reappearing is a red flag: one of these must be happening:

1) **You previously generated `BuildInfo.g.vb` into the project folder** and it’s still being recreated by some *other* build step (pre/post build event, script, Directory.Build.targets, etc.), **or**
2) Something is overriding `BuildInfoFile` / `IntermediateOutputPath` so that `$(IntermediateOutputPath)` effectively points at the project directory (unusual but possible via imported props/targets), **or**
3) There is **another target** (in a `Directory.Build.targets` or imported `.targets`) that writes `BuildInfo.g.vb` to `$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\BuildInfo.g.vb`.

### How we prove which one in 30 seconds
Open the **project-root** `BuildInfo.g.vb` that keeps coming back and check whether it matches your template.

- If it contains exactly:
  - `Friend Module BuildInfo`
  - `Friend Const BuildTimestamp`
  - `Friend Const VersionText`
  
then it’s almost certainly being created by MSBuild using your/related target, but with the path resolving to the project folder.

- If it’s different content, it’s being created by something else.

### What I need from you
1) Paste the first ~20 lines of the reappearing `BuildInfo.g.vb` (root one).
2) In Visual Studio, open **Project Properties → Build Events** and paste the **Pre-build** and **Post-build** command lines (even if blank).
3) Tell me if you have `Directory.Build.props` or `Directory.Build.targets` in your solution folder (or any parent folder).

### Meanwhile (safe stopgap)
Even if the file keeps being created, you can stop it from breaking builds by adding this to the `.vbproj`:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
</ItemGroup>
```

That doesn’t solve the “why is it created”, but it prevents it from being compiled and causing the duplicate compile item issues.

## @testsubject117

when I right click the project and choose properties I don't see a build events tab. I see application, references, compile, package, code analysis, debug, resources and settings

## Copilot

That’s expected for **SDK-style .NET projects** in recent Visual Studio: the old **Build Events** UI often isn’t shown. In SDK-style projects, pre/post build steps are typically done in the `.vbproj` via MSBuild targets, or via imported `Directory.Build.targets`.

So we’ll find what’s recreating the root `BuildInfo.g.vb` using MSBuild-based checks instead.

## 1) Confirm which file is being generated and by what
Open the **project-root** file that keeps reappearing:

- `AMiOffice\BuildInfo.g.vb`

Paste the first ~20 lines here.

That will tell me immediately whether it’s being produced by your `WriteLinesToFile` target (it will match your template exactly) or something else.

## 2) Search for any other MSBuild that writes that file
In Visual Studio: **Ctrl+Shift+F** (Find in Files), search the *solution directory* for:

- `BuildInfo.g.vb`
- `WriteLinesToFile`
- `GenerateBuildInfo`
- `BuildInfoFile`
- `MSBuildProjectDirectory)\BuildInfo.g.vb` (or just `MSBuildProjectDirectory`)

If you find matches in:
- `Directory.Build.targets`
- `Directory.Build.props`
- `.targets` / `.props` files
- another `.vbproj`

…that’s almost certainly the thing recreating it.

### Where to look on disk
Check these paths (File Explorer) in your solution folder and **parent folders** too:
- `Directory.Build.props`
- `Directory.Build.targets`

Those apply automatically to all projects underneath them.

## 3) Immediate containment: stop compiling the root file
Even before we find the generator, add this to your `.vbproj` (before `</Project>`):

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
</ItemGroup>
```

This prevents the root copy from participating in compilation (so it can’t cause “duplicate Compile items”).

## 4) If you want it gone entirely: auto-delete it every build
Add:

```xml
<Target Name="DeleteRootBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
  <Delete Files="$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\BuildInfo.g.vb" />
</Target>
```

That guarantees it won’t survive the build, even if something creates it earlier.

---

### One more key check (path resolution)
In your `.vbproj`, what is the exact value of `BuildInfoFile` right now? You set:

```xml
<BuildInfoFile>$(IntermediateOutputPath)BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
```

If `IntermediateOutputPath` is being overridden to the project directory by some imported props/targets, that would explain the behavior. The content of the root `BuildInfo.g.vb` will be the tell.

So: paste the first ~20 lines of the recreated root `BuildInfo.g.vb`, and we’ll pinpoint the source.

## @testsubject117

Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = "2026-04-16 22:09:17 UTC"
    Friend Const VersionText As String = "1.0.0.0"
End Module


## Copilot

That root `BuildInfo.g.vb` is **definitely** being generated by *your* `WriteLinesToFile` target (it matches the template exactly). So the question becomes: why is `$(BuildInfoFile)` resolving to the **project folder** instead of `obj\...`?

In SDK-style projects, the most common reason is:

- you have set **`IntermediateOutputPath`** (or **`BaseIntermediateOutputPath`**) somewhere (often in a `Directory.Build.props`), and it’s being set to `.\` (project dir) or otherwise ending up as the project folder.

### What to do next (in order)

## 1) Check for `Directory.Build.props` / `Directory.Build.targets` (most likely)
In File Explorer, look in:
- the **solution folder**
- the **parent folder** above the solution
- (and one more parent if needed)

for either of these files:
- `Directory.Build.props`
- `Directory.Build.targets`

If found, open it and search for:
- `IntermediateOutputPath`
- `BaseIntermediateOutputPath`
- `IntermediateOutputPath`
- `obj`
- `OutputPath`

If you see something like:

```xml
<BaseIntermediateOutputPath>.</BaseIntermediateOutputPath>
```

or anything pointing intermediates to the project directory, that’s the culprit.

## 2) Quick “force it” fix inside `AMiOffice.vbproj`
Even if something upstream is overriding intermediate paths, you can force your generated file into a safe, explicit location using `$(BaseIntermediateOutputPath)` + `$(Configuration)` + `$(TargetFramework)` (and ensure it ends with a slash).

Add/replace your BuildInfoFile property with this:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

This bypasses `IntermediateOutputPath` completely and will always land under your project’s obj folder.

Then **delete the root** `BuildInfo.g.vb` and rebuild; it should now be created under `obj\Debug\net10.0-windows\BuildInfo.g.vb`.

## 3) Containment: also prevent compiling a root copy (safety)
Keep this too:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
</ItemGroup>
```

So even if something goes wrong again, the root file won’t get compiled and won’t trigger the duplicate compile-items error.

---

### I need one detail to finish this cleanly
Do you have a `Directory.Build.props` file anywhere above the project? If yes, paste its contents (or at least any lines mentioning intermediate/output paths). That will let us fix the *actual* reason the intermediates are being redirected.

But the “force it” `BuildInfoFile` path above will get you unblocked immediately.

## @testsubject117

I'm confused. I'm using VB .net but you keep mentioning sdk

## Copilot

VB.NET projects can be **SDK-style** too. “SDK-style” is about the **project file format** (`.vbproj`) and how MSBuild includes files, not about the programming language.

Your project is SDK-style because the very first line of your `.vbproj` is:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
```

That single `Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk"` is what I mean by “SDK-style project”. It’s why:

- VB source files under the project folder are **implicitly included** (you saw the “implicit items” duplicate-Compile error).
- The old **Build Events** UI tab often doesn’t appear (which you also saw).
- Build customization is typically done via MSBuild targets (like your `GenerateBuildInfo` + `WriteLinesToFile`).

So: you’re absolutely using **VB.NET**, and you’re also using an **SDK-style .NET project**. Both are true.

### Why this matters to your BuildInfo problem
Your `WriteLinesToFile` target is generating `BuildInfo.g.vb` using:

- `File="$(BuildInfoFile)"`

and we expected `BuildInfoFile` to point into `obj\...` via `$(IntermediateOutputPath)`.

But since the file is showing up in the **project root**, something is causing that path to resolve to the project directory.

### Next step (simple)
To remove the confusion entirely, we can stop relying on `$(IntermediateOutputPath)` and generate explicitly into `obj\...`:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

That works the same in VB.NET; it’s just MSBuild.

If you paste any `Directory.Build.props` / `Directory.Build.targets` you have (if they exist), I can also explain exactly *why* `IntermediateOutputPath` is behaving unexpectedly on your machine.

Replace it.

You already have a `<PropertyGroup>` in your `.vbproj` that defines `BuildInfoFile` (currently using `$(IntermediateOutputPath)`). You want to **replace that one line** so MSBuild stops resolving to the project root.

### Exactly what to do

1) Right‑click the project → **Edit Project File** (`AMiOffice.vbproj`)

2) Find this block (or similar) that you added earlier:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildInfoFile>$(IntermediateOutputPath)BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

3) Replace it with this:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

That’s all—same place, just a different value.

### Then do this cleanup once

- `obj\<Configuration>\<TargetFramework>\BuildInfo.g.vb`

…and should **stop** appearing in the project root.

That message is coming from the **VB code editor / Properties window**, not from the `.vbproj` XML editor.

You can’t paste a `<PropertyGroup>...</PropertyGroup>` block into a VB source file or into the project “Properties” UI—`PropertyGroup` is an MSBuild XML element, not VB code.

1) In **Solution Explorer**, **right‑click the project** (AMiOffice)  
2) Click **Edit Project File**

3) In the `.vbproj` XML that opens, find your existing BuildInfo block, e.g.:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildInfoFile>$(IntermediateOutputPath)BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

4) Replace **just the value** with:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

5) Save the `.vbproj`.

### Then clean once
- Close Visual Studio
- Delete `<project folder>\BuildInfo.g.vb`
- Delete `<project folder>\bin` and `<project folder>\obj`

## @testsubject117

Duplicate 'Compile' items were included. The .NET SDK includes 'Compile' items from your project directory by default. You can either remove these items from your project file, or set the 'EnableDefaultCompileItems' property to 'false' if you want to explicitly include them in your project file. For more information, see https://aka.ms/sdkimplicititems. The duplicate items were: 'C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\obj\Release\net10.0-windows\BuildInfo.g.vb'

## Copilot

You’re very close now: the file is being generated into `obj\Release\...` (good), but it’s being **included twice** in compilation:

- once by **implicit compile globs**, and
- once by your explicit `<Compile Include="...BuildInfo.g.vb" />`.

In SDK-style projects, `obj\**\*.vb` is *normally* excluded from compile globs, but if it’s being picked up implicitly in your setup, the simplest fix is: **stop explicitly including it** and instead tell MSBuild to compile it via `Compile` **Update** only if it’s already in the item list (or disable default compile items). Easiest and most reliable is:

## Fix: Remove the explicit `<Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />`
In your `.vbproj`, find and delete this block (or line):

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
</ItemGroup>
```

Keep the `GenerateBuildInfo` target that creates the file.

### Why this works
If your environment is already picking up that file through the default include mechanism, adding an explicit `Include` creates a duplicate.

---

## If removing that include causes `BuildInfo` to disappear (possible)
Then your default compile items *aren’t* including it, and we need a different approach. In that case, do this instead (more explicit, no duplicates):

### Option B (deterministic): disable implicit compile items and include what you want
Add this in the main `<PropertyGroup>`:

```xml
<EnableDefaultCompileItems>false</EnableDefaultCompileItems>
```

Then you must explicitly include your `*.vb` files (not fun, usually not recommended unless you really want full control). So I’d try the “remove explicit include” fix first.

---

## Also do this cleanup once
Because you’ve been bouncing between Debug/Release and paths:

1) Close Visual Studio
2) Delete `bin` and `obj`
3) Reopen and rebuild

---

### One question so I can give the exact final `.vbproj` snippet
In your `.vbproj` right now, do you have **any** of these lines?

- `<EnableDefaultItems>false</EnableDefaultItems>`
- `<EnableDefaultCompileItems>false</EnableDefaultCompileItems>`
- Anything that changes `DefaultItemExcludes` / `DefaultItemIncludes`

If you paste just the **BuildInfo-related section** of your `.vbproj` (the PropertyGroup + ItemGroup + Target), I’ll tell you exactly which single line to remove and what the final block should be.

## Copilot

Your `.vbproj` has **duplicated blocks** (multiple `BuildInfoFile` properties, multiple `Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)"`, multiple `Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb"`, and **two `Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo"`**). That’s exactly why you’re seeing duplicate compile-item errors and weird behavior.

### What to do
Replace everything from the first extra `PropertyGroup` you added onward with **one clean BuildInfo section**.

Below is a regenerated **entire** `AMiOffice.vbproj` file based on the top (good) part of what you pasted, plus a single correct BuildInfo generator. Copy/paste this whole file.

Key points in this version:
- `BuildInfo.g.vb` is generated into `obj\<Configuration>\<TargetFramework>\BuildInfo.g.vb` (never project root)
- It’s included in compile **once**
- Any stray root `BuildInfo.g.vb` is removed from compile (harmless safety)
- Only **one** `GenerateBuildInfo` target exists

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>
    <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
    <MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>

    <AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
    <FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>

    <GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>

    <!-- Optional informational version -->
    <InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>

    <!-- Where the generated VB file will go (explicit path avoids IntermediateOutputPath overrides) -->
    <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <!-- Never compile a stray BuildInfo.g.vb that ends up in the project root -->
    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />

    <!-- Compile the generated one under obj -->
    <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <!-- UTC timestamp is stable across machines/timezones -->
      <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss 'UTC'"))</BuildTimestamp>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines=
"Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = &quot;$(BuildTimestamp)&quot;
    Friend Const VersionText As String = &quot;$(Version)&quot;
End Module
" />
  </Target>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Remove="ESC Logo.jpg" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Include="ESC Logo.jpg">
      <CopyToOutputDirectory>Never</CopyToOutputDirectory>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Import Include="System.Data" />
    <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
    <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Update="My Project\Application.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Application.myapp</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Resources.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Resources.resx</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Update="My Project\Resources.resx">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My.Resources</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>VbMyResourcesResXFileCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Resources.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Update="My Project\Application.myapp">
      <Generator>MyApplicationCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Application.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>
```

### After pasting this file
1) Delete any manual `BuildInfo.vb` that defines `Friend Module BuildInfo` (only one should exist: the generated one)
2) Close VS
3) Delete `bin` and `obj`
4) Reopen and rebuild

If you still get “duplicate Compile items … obj\Release\…\BuildInfo.g.vb”, then something in your environment is also implicitly including `obj\**\*.vb` (unusual). If that happens, tell me the exact error text again and I’ll switch to the “add the compile item inside the target and remove it from default items” pattern.

## @testsubject117

hold that thought, I can't do anything right now after you said after pasting this file. When I paste the new code into the file, after deleting all the old code, I get the MSB4004 The "PropertyGroup" property is reserved, and cannot be modified.

## Copilot

You’re not editing the `.vbproj` file as **XML**—you’re pasting it into a place that treats it like a **property value** (typically the Visual Studio **Property Pages** UI, or sometimes the wrong editor surface). That specific error (MSB4004 “The `PropertyGroup` property is reserved…”) happens when MSBuild parses `<PropertyGroup>` as if you were trying to set a property named `PropertyGroup`, which only occurs when the XML is not in the correct project-file structure (or you’re not actually editing the project file).

Do this exact sequence to guarantee you’re editing the real `AMiOffice.vbproj`:

## 1) Open the project file the right way
### Method (works even when VS hides “Edit Project File”)
1. In Solution Explorer: **right-click the AMiOffice project**
2. Click **Unload Project**
3. Right-click the (unloaded) project again
4. Click **Edit AMiOffice.vbproj**

You should now see a plain text XML file whose first line is:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
```

If you *don’t* see that, you are not in the right file.

## 2) Paste the full XML exactly, including the outer `<Project ...>` line
Make sure the file begins with `<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">` and ends with `</Project>`.

Also ensure you did not accidentally delete/mess up the first line or add extra characters before `<Project...>` (no stray quotes, no BOM issues usually, etc.).

## 3) Save, then reload
1. Save the `.vbproj`
2. Close the editor tab
3. Right-click the project → **Reload Project**

---

### Why you’re seeing MSB4004 even after pasting “correct XML”
MSBuild throws MSB4004 when it encounters something like this *inside a property value context*:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <SomeProperty>
    <PropertyGroup> ... </PropertyGroup>
  </SomeProperty>
</PropertyGroup>
```

or when the file is not a valid MSBuild project root (missing `<Project>` root element).

So the most likely causes are:
- you pasted into the **wrong UI** (not the `.vbproj` text editor), or
- the `.vbproj` file no longer has the correct `<Project ...>` root element after edits.

---

## Quick verification
After you open `Edit AMiOffice.vbproj`, paste me the **first 5 lines** of what you see *before* you edit anything. I’ll tell you immediately whether you’re in the right file and whether the root element is intact.

## Copilot

If the project opens “empty”, that usually means the `.vbproj` got into an invalid state and Visual Studio can’t load it, so it shows an unloaded/blank project.

Get back to a working state by fixing the `.vbproj` **outside Visual Studio**.

## 1) Close Visual Studio completely

## 2) Open the project file in a plain text editor
Use Notepad (or VS Code) and open this file directly:

- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj`

## 3) Replace the entire contents with this known-good `.vbproj`
Copy/paste this whole file, save it:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>
    <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
    <MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>

    <AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
    <FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>

    <GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
    <InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>

    <!-- Generate here (explicit) to avoid IntermediateOutputPath overrides -->
    <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Remove="ESC Logo.jpg" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Include="ESC Logo.jpg">
      <CopyToOutputDirectory>Never</CopyToOutputDirectory>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Import Include="System.Data" />
    <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
    <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Update="My Project\Application.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Application.myapp</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Resources.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Resources.resx</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Update="My Project\Resources.resx">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My.Resources</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>VbMyResourcesResXFileCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Resources.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Update="My Project\Application.myapp">
      <Generator>MyApplicationCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Application.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- IMPORTANT: do NOT compile a stray root BuildInfo.g.vb -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Generate BuildInfo.g.vb and add it to compilation -->
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss 'UTC'"))</BuildTimestamp>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines=
"Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = &quot;$(BuildTimestamp)&quot;
    Friend Const VersionText As String = &quot;$(Version)&quot;
End Module
" />

    <!-- Add AFTER generation so it can't be missing -->
    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>
```

## 4) Delete stale build output (prevents old junk from reappearing)
Delete these folders if they exist:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\bin`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\obj`

Also delete (if present):
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\BuildInfo.g.vb`

## 5) Reopen Visual Studio and open the `.sln` again
The project should load normally (not empty).

Some tooling (VS previews, “Open Folder”, or external extensions) can generate alternate “solution-like” files. But for reliable MSBuild/WinForms VB work, use `.sln` or open the `.vbproj`.

<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>
    <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
    <MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>

    <AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
    <FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>

    <GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
    <InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>

    <!-- Generate here (explicit) to avoid IntermediateOutputPath overrides -->
    <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

## Copilot

That header looks fine. The two things that were breaking you earlier were:

1) **Duplicate `GenerateBuildInfo` targets / duplicate `BuildInfoFile` property blocks**, and  
2) **Explicit `<Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />` causing “Duplicate Compile items … obj\…BuildInfo.g.vb”**.

To get you back to a stable state *and* stop the file from appearing in the project root, use this **single, clean BuildInfo section** (and make sure there is only ONE of each of these in the entire file).

## Put this in your `.vbproj` (once)
Place it **after** your main `<PropertyGroup>` (the one you pasted) and **before** the final `</Project>`.

### 1) Add this ItemGroup (once)
```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <!-- Never compile a stray BuildInfo.g.vb that appears in the project folder -->
  <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
</ItemGroup>
```

### 2) Add this Target (once)
**Important:** do **NOT** add any `<Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />` anywhere. That’s what caused the duplicate compile items error you pasted earlier.

```xml
<Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss 'UTC'"))</BuildTimestamp>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <WriteLinesToFile
    File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
    Overwrite="true"
    Lines=
"Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = &quot;$(BuildTimestamp)&quot;
    Friend Const VersionText As String = &quot;$(Version)&quot;
End Module
" />
</Target>
```

## Then do a one-time cleanup
1) Close VS
2) Delete `<project>\BuildInfo.g.vb` if it exists
3) Delete `<project>\obj` and `<project>\bin`
4) Rebuild

### If BuildInfo becomes “missing” after removing Compile Include
Then your default compile items are *not* picking up the generated file (which is expected, because it’s under `obj`). In that case we’ll do the correct deterministic approach:

- add the compile item **inside the target**, **but** first remove it from default items so it can’t be duplicated.

If that happens, paste the exact compiler error (BC30451 etc.) and I’ll give you the exact 3-line MSBuild fix.

## One critical check right now
In your current `.vbproj`, do you still have *any* line like this anywhere?

```xml
<Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
```

If yes: delete it. That line is the direct cause of the “Duplicate 'Compile' items… obj\Release\net10.0-windows\BuildInfo.g.vb” error.

## @testsubject117

  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Update="My Project\Application.myapp">
      <Generator>MyApplicationCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Application.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- IMPORTANT: do NOT compile a stray root BuildInfo.g.vb -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Generate BuildInfo.g.vb and add it to compilation -->
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss 'UTC'"))</BuildTimestamp>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines=
"Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = &quot;$(BuildTimestamp)&quot;
    Friend Const VersionText As String = &quot;$(Version)&quot;
End Module
" />

    <!-- Add AFTER generation so it can't be missing -->
    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>

## Copilot

Delete this part:

```xml
    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
    </ItemGroup>
```

That line is what’s triggering your **Duplicate 'Compile' items** error.

However, once you remove it, **BuildInfo will likely become missing** again (BC30451), because files under `obj\...` are *not reliably compiled by default*.

So the correct deterministic fix is: **keep compiling the generated file, but make it impossible for it to be included twice** by explicitly excluding it from the SDK’s default item includes.

Use this final version of the BuildInfo section (copy/paste to replace your current BuildInfo section entirely):

```xml
  <!-- IMPORTANT: do NOT compile a stray root BuildInfo.g.vb -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Prevent the SDK default include globs from also picking up the generated file -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Generate BuildInfo.g.vb and add it to compilation -->
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss 'UTC'"))</BuildTimestamp>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines=
"Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = &quot;$(BuildTimestamp)&quot;
    Friend Const VersionText As String = &quot;$(Version)&quot;
End Module
" />

    <!-- Add AFTER generation so it can't be missing, and avoid duplicates -->
    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>
```

Why this works:

- The second `ItemGroup` (`<Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />`) ensures that **even if** the SDK’s implicit compile items or a glob happens to include that `obj\...\BuildInfo.g.vb`, it gets removed first.
- Then we add it back exactly once inside the target.

### After you edit the `.vbproj`
1) Close Visual Studio
2) Delete:
   - `<project>\BuildInfo.g.vb` (root, if present)
   - `<project>\bin`
   - `<project>\obj`
3) Reopen VS and rebuild.

If you still see a duplicate compile error, paste the new error text (it will list which two paths are colliding), and I’ll adjust the exclusion to match exactly what’s being double-included.

## @testsubject117

my file has a reference at the very bottom to <Compile Include="$BuildInfoFile)" /> , here's the last 13 lines, is this how it should be?: Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = &quot;$(BuildTimestamp)&quot;
    Friend Const VersionText As String = &quot;$(Version)&quot;
End Module
" />

    <!-- Add AFTER generation so it can't be missing -->
    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>

## Copilot

Yes — **that exact line is correct** *as long as it appears only inside the `GenerateBuildInfo` target* and you also have the “remove” line to prevent duplicates.

So the end of your `.vbproj` can look like that, **but you must also have this elsewhere (once) in the file** to prevent the “Duplicate Compile items” error:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
</ItemGroup>
```

### What “correct” looks like (minimum)
Somewhere above your `<Target ...>` (order doesn’t matter much), you should have:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
</PropertyGroup>

<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
  <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
</ItemGroup>
```

And then your target ending exactly like you pasted:

```xml
    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>
```

### Two things to double-check
1) Make sure it is **exactly** `$(BuildInfoFile)` (with parentheses).  
   Not `$BuildInfoFile` and not `"$BuildInfoFile)"` (those will break).

2) Make sure you **do not** have any other `<Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />` elsewhere outside the target (even one extra will trigger duplicates again).

If you paste the single `<PropertyGroup>` that defines `BuildInfoFile` plus any `<ItemGroup>` lines mentioning `BuildInfo.g.vb` or `BuildInfoFile`, I’ll confirm you’ve got exactly one of each and in the right form.

## Copilot

Copy/paste this entire `AMiOffice.vbproj` into Notepad to replace what you have. This version removes the accidental duplicates, generates `BuildInfo.g.vb` under `obj\...` (never the project root), and avoids the “Duplicate Compile items” error by explicitly removing the generated file from any default includes before adding it once.

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>
    <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
    <MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>

    <AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
    <FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>

    <GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
    <InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>

    <!-- Explicit path so we don't depend on IntermediateOutputPath (which can be overridden) -->
    <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Remove="ESC Logo.jpg" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Include="ESC Logo.jpg">
      <CopyToOutputDirectory>Never</CopyToOutputDirectory>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Import Include="System.Data" />
    <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
    <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Update="My Project\Application.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Application.myapp</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Resources.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Resources.resx</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Update="My Project\Resources.resx">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My.Resources</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>VbMyResourcesResXFileCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Resources.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Update="My Project\Application.myapp">
      <Generator>MyApplicationCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Application.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Safety: never compile a stray BuildInfo.g.vb that appears in the project root -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Prevent any default globs from also including the generated file (avoids duplicate Compile items) -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Generate BuildInfo.g.vb and add it to compilation exactly once -->
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss 'UTC'"))</BuildTimestamp>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines=
"Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = &quot;$(BuildTimestamp)&quot;
    Friend Const VersionText As String = &quot;$(Version)&quot;
End Module
" />

    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>
```

After you paste/save:

1) Delete these if they exist:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\BuildInfo.g.vb`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\bin\`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\obj\`

2) Rebuild.

If you still get a duplicate Compile-items error, paste the exact error line (it will list the duplicate paths). That will tell us what other include rule is pulling in `obj\...\BuildInfo.g.vb` so we can exclude it precisely.

## Copilot

You’re extremely close. The only thing you’re missing (to prevent the **Duplicate `Compile` items** error) is this line:

```xml
<Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
```

Add it **once** (best right before the `<Target ...>`), so MSBuild removes the generated file from any default includes, then your target adds it back exactly once.

Here is your corrected full file (copy/paste to replace in Notepad):

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>
    <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
    <MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>

    <AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
    <FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>

    <GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
    <InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>

    <!-- Explicit path so we don't depend on IntermediateOutputPath (which can be overridden) -->
    <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Remove="ESC Logo.jpg" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Include="ESC Logo.jpg">
      <CopyToOutputDirectory>Never</CopyToOutputDirectory>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Import Include="System.Data" />
    <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
    <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Update="My Project\Application.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Application.myapp</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Resources.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Resources.resx</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Update="My Project\Resources.resx">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My.Resources</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>VbMyResourcesResXFileCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Resources.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Update="My Project\Application.myapp">
      <Generator>MyApplicationCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Application.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- IMPORTANT: do NOT compile a stray root BuildInfo.g.vb -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Prevent any default globs from also including the generated file (avoids duplicate Compile items) -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Generate BuildInfo.g.vb and add it to compilation -->
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss 'UTC'"))</BuildTimestamp>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines=
"Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = &quot;$(BuildTimestamp)&quot;
    Friend Const VersionText As String = &quot;$(Version)&quot;
End Module
" />

    <!-- Add AFTER generation so it can't be missing -->
    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>
```

## Copilot

Good—closing/reopening the solution points to a VS state/cache hiccup, not the project file.

And thanks for the correction on **VB/VS 2026 using `.slnx`** in your setup. My earlier advice to prefer `.sln` was based on older VS behavior; in your environment `.slnx` is evidently the normal solution format.

## What you should have now (recap)
Your `.vbproj` should include all three of these pieces (exactly once each):

1) **BuildInfoFile property** (explicit path into `obj\...`):
```xml
<BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
```

2) **Remove root stray file**:
```xml
<Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
```

3) **Prevent duplicate compile items + include generated one once**:
```xml
<Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
...
<Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
```

That combination is what prevents:
- `BuildInfo.g.vb` reappearing in the project root, and
- “Duplicate ‘Compile’ items … obj\...\BuildInfo.g.vb”.

## One quick suggestion (optional, but helps avoid future “blank” loads)
If this happens again, deleting the repo’s `.vs` folder usually clears it:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\.vs\`

## Verify it’s working
Build once in Debug and once in Release and confirm the generated file ends up here:
- `AMiOffice\obj\Debug\net10.0-windows\BuildInfo.g.vb`
- `AMiOffice\obj\Release\net10.0-windows\BuildInfo.g.vb`

If you paste the next build output/error (if any), I’ll help you fine-tune it, but at this point your MSBuild wiring is in the “correct/robust” shape.

2) You confirmed your VS/VB 2026 uses **`.slnx`** solution files (normal for you).
3) Your `.vbproj` was corrected so that:

   - The generated file won’t cause **Duplicate `Compile` items** (by removing it first, then including it once)

- In a `<PropertyGroup>`:
```xml
<BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
```

- In an `<ItemGroup>` (safety):
```xml
<Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
```

```xml
  <PropertyGroup>
    <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss 'UTC'"))</BuildTimestamp>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <WriteLinesToFile File="$(BuildInfoFile)" Overwrite="true" Lines= "...VB code..." />

</Target>
```

## Copilot

This `.vbproj` looks correct and consistent now. You haven’t left any of the earlier “by mistake” duplicates in here (no repeated `BuildInfoFile`, no duplicate `GenerateBuildInfo` target, no repeated `Compile Remove` blocks).

### What’s good
- **Generated file path is deterministic** (always under `obj\<Configuration>\<TargetFramework>\BuildInfo.g.vb`):
  ```xml
  <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
  ```
- You prevent accidental compilation of a root file:
  ```xml
  <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
  ```
- You prevent “Duplicate Compile items” by removing the generated file from any default globs *before* adding it:
  ```xml
  <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
  ```
- You generate the file before compile and then include it:
  ```xml
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile"> ... </Target>
  ```

### One small optional improvement (recommended)
Right now the file is generated, but the directory might not exist on a totally clean build (usually it does, but to make it bulletproof you can add a `MakeDir`).

Add this just before `WriteLinesToFile` inside the target:

```xml
<MakeDir Directories="$([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName('$(BuildInfoFile)'))" />
```

So the target becomes:

```xml
<Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss 'UTC'"))</BuildTimestamp>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <MakeDir Directories="$([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName('$(BuildInfoFile)'))" />

  <WriteLinesToFile ... />
  ...
</Target>
```

Not required if it’s already working, but it prevents edge-case failures.

### Next step
What do you want to do with `BuildInfo` now?
- Show it in an **About** dialog
- Put it in the **window title bar**
- Log it at startup
- Include **Git commit hash** too (if you use Git)

Tell me which, and where in your app you want it displayed, and I’ll give you the exact VB.NET code.

## @testsubject117

doesn't this already show in the about dialogue and on the windows title bar?

## Copilot

No—**not automatically**.

What you have now does only this:

- It **generates** a VB module `BuildInfo` at build time (in `obj\...\BuildInfo.g.vb`) containing:
  - `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp`
  - `BuildInfo.VersionText`

Nothing in WinForms will display those values in the **title bar** or the **About** dialog unless you explicitly set it, or unless your About dialog is already wired to show some other assembly attributes.

## What *does* show automatically (without extra code)
### Window title bar
By default, a WinForms form title bar shows the form’s `Text` property (whatever you set in the designer). It will not pick up `BuildInfo.*` or `Version` automatically.

### VB “About…” dialog
If you’re using VB’s built-in About box (`My Project\Application.myapp` setting “About box”), it typically shows things like:
- **Product name**
- **Version** (from assembly version / application version)

It will **not** automatically include your custom `BuildTimestamp`.

Also, your `.vbproj` currently sets:
- `<AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>`
- `<FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>`
- `<Version>1.0.0.0</Version>`
- `<InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>`

So your About box may show “1.0.0.0”, but that’s separate from the BuildInfo timestamp module.

## How to show it (you choose where)

### Option A: add to the main form title bar
In your main form’s `Load` event:

```vb
Me.Text = $"{Me.Text}  v{BuildInfo.VersionText}  ({BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp})"
```

### Option B: add to your About box
If you have an About form, add a label and set it on load:

```vb
lblBuild.Text = $"Version {BuildInfo.VersionText}{Environment.NewLine}Built {BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp}"
```

---

If you tell me:
1) what your startup form is called (e.g., `MainForm`, `Form1`, etc.), and  
2) whether you’re using the VB built-in About box or a custom one,  

…I’ll give you the exact snippet and where to paste it (including the right event handler).

## @testsubject117

what file do I place option a in

## Copilot

Put Option A in your **startup form’s code-behind** (the form that opens first).

### Most common location
1) In **Solution Explorer** open your startup form (often `Form1.vb` or `MainForm.vb`).
2) Open the **code view** (not the Designer).
3) Add this handler inside the form class:

```vbnet
Private Sub Form1_Load(sender As Object, e As EventArgs) Handles MyBase.Load
    Me.Text = $"{Me.Text}  v{BuildInfo.VersionText}  ({BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp})"
End Sub
```

Replace `Form1_Load` name if you want (the name doesn’t matter), but the key part is:
- `Handles MyBase.Load`

### If you don’t know which form is the startup form
Your `.vbproj` shows:

```xml
<StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>
```

So the actual startup form is configured in **My Project**:
- `My Project\Application.myapp`

To find it:
1) Expand **My Project**
2) Open **Application.myapp**
3) Look for **Startup form**

Whatever it says (e.g., `frmMain`)—that is the `.vb` file to put the code in.

### If your form doesn’t already have a Load handler
Visual Basic will create it automatically when you paste the code above into the form’s `.vb` file.

If you tell me the name shown in **Startup form**, I’ll write the exact code with the correct form class name.
