## Copilot

Put Option A in **`FormMainMenu.vb`** (the code-behind for your startup form).

Add (or update) a `Load` handler inside the `FormMainMenu` class:

```vbnet
Public Class FormMainMenu

    Private Sub FormMainMenu_Load(sender As Object, e As EventArgs) Handles MyBase.Load
        Me.Text = $"{Me.Text}  v{BuildInfo.VersionText}  ({BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp})"
    End Sub

End Class
```

Notes:
- This goes in **FormMainMenu.vb** (not the Designer file).
- If you already have a `FormMainMenu_Load` method, just add the `Me.Text = ...` line inside it.
- Make sure `BuildInfo` exists (it will after you build once, since it’s generated into `obj\...`).

If you want the title bar to show only the version (and not repeat whatever designer text is already there), tell me what you want the exact title string to look like.

## @testsubject117

I do not see FormMainMenu_Load within FormMainMenu.vb. here is a complete copy: Option Strict Off
Option Explicit On

Imports System
Imports System.Drawing
Imports System.Windows.Forms

Partial Public Class FormMainMenu

    Private _buildInfo As String = ""

    Protected Overrides Sub OnLoad(e As EventArgs)
        MyBase.OnLoad(e)

        Dim v = BuildInfo.VersionText
        Dim built = BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp

        Me.KeyPreview = True

        BuildMenu()

        Dim mainAsm As System.Reflection.Assembly = System.Reflection.Assembly.GetExecutingAssembly()

        Dim mainVer As String = ""
        Try
            mainVer = mainAsm.GetName().Version.ToString()
        Catch
            mainVer = "(unknown)"
        End Try

        Dim mainBuildDate As String = ""
        Try
            mainBuildDate = System.IO.File.GetLastWriteTime(Application.ExecutablePath).ToString("yyyy-MM-dd")
        Catch ex As Exception
            mainBuildDate = "(unknown)"
            ' Optional while debugging:
            ' MessageBox.Show("Build date read failed: " & ex.Message)
        End Try
        _buildInfo = "   v" & mainVer & "   (" & mainBuildDate & ")"

        lblMainMenu.Text = "MAIN MENU"
        Me.Text = "Active Magnetic Inspection Main Menu Application   v" & mainVer & "   (" & mainBuildDate & ")"
        UpdateHeaderClock()
        tmrClock.Interval = 1000
        tmrClock.Start()

        ' Force the version/date text to start as far left as possible in lblDateTime
        lblDateTime.TextAlign = ContentAlignment.MiddleLeft
        lblDateTime.Padding = New Padding(0, 10, 0, 0)  ' remove right padding; keep top padding
        lblDateTime.Margin = New Padding(0)

        ' IMPORTANT: run resize after layout is finalized
        Me.BeginInvoke(New Action(Sub()
                                      ResizeButtonsToPanel(flpLeft)
                                      ResizeButtonsToPanel(flpRight)
                                  End Sub))
    End Sub

    Private Sub flpLeft_SizeChanged(sender As Object, e As EventArgs) Handles flpLeft.SizeChanged
        ResizeButtonsToPanel(flpLeft)
    End Sub

    Private Sub flpRight_SizeChanged(sender As Object, e As EventArgs) Handles flpRight.SizeChanged
        ResizeButtonsToPanel(flpRight)
    End Sub
    Private Sub tmrClock_Tick(sender As Object, e As EventArgs) Handles tmrClock.Tick
        UpdateHeaderClock()
    End Sub
    Private Sub UpdateHeaderClock()
        Dim now As DateTime = DateTime.Now

        lblDateTime.Text = _buildInfo & "          " &
                       now.ToString("dddd") & "  " &
                       now.ToString("MM-dd-yyyy") & "          " &
                       now.ToString("hh:mm:ss tt")
    End Sub
    Private Sub BuildMenu()
        If flpLeft Is Nothing OrElse flpRight Is Nothing Then Return

        flpLeft.Controls.Clear()
        flpRight.Controls.Clear()
        ' Left column
        AddMenuButton(flpLeft, "A", "Shop Card Generator")
        AddMenuButton(flpLeft, "B", "Invoice Generator")
        AddMenuButton(flpLeft, "C", "Checks and Cash Receipts")
        AddMenuButton(flpLeft, "D", "View Sales Journal")
        AddMenuButton(flpLeft, "E", "View Log Book")
        AddMenuButton(flpLeft, "F", "Price List Program")
        AddMenuButton(flpLeft, "G", "Print Records / Void Invoices")
        AddMenuButton(flpLeft, "H", "Quick Message Flashing")
        AddMenuButton(flpLeft, "I", "Backup Price List & Rolodex")
        AddMenuButton(flpLeft, "J", "Print Out Customers Actual Names")
        AddMenuButton(flpLeft, "K", "Cash Disbursements")
        AddMenuButton(flpLeft, "L", "Business Expenses Account")
        AddMenuButton(flpLeft, "M", "Quotation Form Generator")
        AddMenuButton(flpLeft, "N", "Rolodex")

        ' Right column (removed: S, U, V, W, +, 5)
        AddMenuButton(flpRight, "O", "Copy Spec Index")
        AddMenuButton(flpRight, "P", "Entire Ledger Viewing")
        AddMenuButton(flpRight, "Q", "Word Processor")
        AddMenuButton(flpRight, "R", "Find Word Processor Text")
        AddMenuButton(flpRight, "T", "Change Date or Time")
        AddMenuButton(flpRight, "X", "Typewriter Mode")
        AddMenuButton(flpRight, "Y", "Ed Dean's Personal Backup")
        AddMenuButton(flpRight, "Z", "Personal Calendar")

        AddMenuButton(flpRight, "1", "Mileage Tracking")
        AddMenuButton(flpRight, "2", "Product Purchasing")
        AddMenuButton(flpRight, "3", "Miscellaneous Menu")
        AddMenuButton(flpRight, "4", "Add Entries to Log Book")
        AddMenuButton(flpRight, "6", "Cadmium Cards")
        AddMenuButton(flpRight, "7", "Emergency PAYROLL System")
        AddMenuButton(flpRight, "?", "About AMiOffice Menu System")
    End Sub

    Private Function MakeHeaderLabel(text As String) As Label
        Dim lbl As New Label()
        lbl.AutoSize = False
        lbl.Height = 32
        lbl.TextAlign = ContentAlignment.MiddleLeft
        lbl.Font = New Font(Me.Font, FontStyle.Bold)
        lbl.Text = text
        lbl.Margin = New Padding(3, 3, 3, 8)
        lbl.Width = 1000 ' will be resized later
        Return lbl
    End Function

    Private Sub AddMenuButton(panel As FlowLayoutPanel, key As String, text As String)
        Dim btn As New Button()

        btn.AutoSize = False
        btn.Height = 36
        btn.Font = New Font("Segoe UI", 12.0F, FontStyle.Bold, GraphicsUnit.Point, CByte(0))
        btn.TextAlign = ContentAlignment.MiddleLeft
        btn.Text = "(" & key & ") " & text
        btn.Tag = key
        btn.Margin = New Padding(3, 3, 3, 6)

        ' Force black buttons with white text
        btn.UseVisualStyleBackColor = False
        btn.BackColor = Color.Black
        btn.ForeColor = Color.White
        btn.FlatStyle = FlatStyle.Flat
        btn.FlatAppearance.BorderColor = Color.DimGray
        btn.FlatAppearance.BorderSize = 1

        ' Optional: nicer hover / click feedback
        btn.FlatAppearance.MouseOverBackColor = Color.FromArgb(32, 32, 32)
        btn.FlatAppearance.MouseDownBackColor = Color.FromArgb(64, 64, 64)

        btn.AutoEllipsis = True

        AddHandler btn.Click, Sub(sender, args) HandleMenuKey(CStr(btn.Tag))

        panel.Controls.Add(btn)
    End Sub

    Protected Overrides Sub OnResize(e As EventArgs)
        MyBase.OnResize(e)
        ResizeButtonsToPanel(flpLeft)
        ResizeButtonsToPanel(flpRight)
    End Sub

    Private Sub ResizeButtonsToPanel(panel As FlowLayoutPanel)
        If panel Is Nothing Then Return

        Dim targetWidth As Integer =
        panel.ClientSize.Width -
        panel.Padding.Left - panel.Padding.Right -
        SystemInformation.VerticalScrollBarWidth - 6

        If targetWidth < 150 Then targetWidth = 150

        For Each c As Control In panel.Controls
            Dim btn = TryCast(c, Button)
            Dim lbl = TryCast(c, Label)

            If btn IsNot Nothing Then
                btn.Width = targetWidth
            ElseIf lbl IsNot Nothing Then
                lbl.Width = targetWidth
            End If
        Next
    End Sub

    Private Sub FormMainMenu_KeyPress(sender As Object, e As KeyPressEventArgs) Handles Me.KeyPress
        Dim ch As String = e.KeyChar.ToString()
        If ch = vbCr OrElse ch = vbLf Then Return
        HandleMenuKey(ch)
    End Sub

    Private Sub HandleMenuKey(ch As String)
        If String.IsNullOrEmpty(ch) Then Return

        Dim up As String = ch
        If up.Length = 1 AndAlso Char.IsLetter(up(0)) Then
            up = up.ToUpperInvariant()
        End If

        Select Case up
            Case "A" : NotYet("Shop Card Generator")
            Case "B" : NotYet("Invoice Generator")
            Case "C" : NotYet("Checks and Cash Receipts (LEDGER)")
            Case "D" : NotYet("View Sales Journal (SALES)")
            Case "E" : NotYet("View Log Book (LOGBOOK)")
            Case "F" : NotYet("Price List Program (plist)")
            Case "G" : NotYet("Print/Void Invoices (BOOT)")
            Case "H" : NotYet("Quick Message Flashing")

            Case "I"
                Try
                    Cursor = Cursors.WaitCursor
                    MigrationService.EnsureFoldersAndMigrateOnce()
                    BackupService.RunBackupPriceListAndRolodex()
                    MessageBox.Show("Backup complete." & Environment.NewLine & AppPaths.BackupDir,
                                    "Backup", MessageBoxButtons.OK, MessageBoxIcon.Information)
                Catch ex As Exception
                    MessageBox.Show("Backup failed: " & ex.Message,
                                    "Backup", MessageBoxButtons.OK, MessageBoxIcon.Error)
                Finally
                    Cursor = Cursors.Default
                End Try

            Case "J" : NotYet("Print Out Customers Actual Names (spool real names)")
            Case "K" : NotYet("Cash Disbursements (BILL)")
            Case "L" : NotYet("Business Expenses Account (password)")
            Case "M" : NotYet("Quotation Form Generator (QUOTE)")
            Case "N" : NotYet("Rolodex (PHONE)")

            Case "O" : NotYet("Copy Spec Index")
            Case "P" : NotYet("Entire Ledger Viewing (ENTIRE)")
            Case "Q" : NotYet("Word Processor")
            Case "R" : NotYet("Find Word Processor Text")

            Case "T" : NotYet("Change Date or Time")
            Case "X" : NotYet("Typewriter Mode")
            Case "Y" : NotYet("Ed Dean's Personal Backup")
            Case "Z" : NotYet("Personal Calendar")

            Case "1" : NotYet("Mileage Tracking")
            Case "2" : NotYet("Product Purchasing")
            Case "3" : NotYet("Miscellaneous Menu")
            Case "4" : NotYet("Add Entries to Log Book")
            Case "6" : NotYet("Cadmium Cards")
            Case "7" : NotYet("Emergency PAYROLL System")

            Case "?"
                Using f As New FormAbout()
                    f.ShowDialog(Me)
                End Using

            Case Else
                ' ignore unknown keys
        End Select
    End Sub

    Private Sub NotYet(feature As String)
        MessageBox.Show("Not implemented yet: " & feature, "Port status", MessageBoxButtons.OK, MessageBoxIcon.Information)
    End Sub

End Class

## Copilot

You don’t need a `FormMainMenu_Load` method because your form already uses the better pattern: **overriding `OnLoad`**. That *is* the right place to set the title bar.

Right now, your `OnLoad` **reads** the generated values:

```vb
Dim v = BuildInfo.VersionText
Dim built = BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp
```

…but then you ignore them and instead build the title from:

- `mainVer = Assembly.GetExecutingAssembly().GetName().Version`
- `mainBuildDate = File.GetLastWriteTime(Application.ExecutablePath)`

So your title bar/About box is **not using BuildInfo yet** (even though BuildInfo is generated and available).

## Minimal change: actually use BuildInfo in the title and header
Replace these two lines:

```vb
_buildInfo = "   v" & mainVer & "   (" & mainBuildDate & ")"
Me.Text = "Active Magnetic Inspection Main Menu Application   v" & mainVer & "   (" & mainBuildDate & ")"
```

with these:

```vb
_buildInfo = "   v" & v & "   (" & built & ")"
Me.Text = "Active Magnetic Inspection Main Menu Application   v" & v & "   (" & built & ")"
```

That’s it.

### Optional cleanup (recommended)
After you do that, you can delete the whole block that computes `mainAsm/mainVer/mainBuildDate`, because it becomes unused.

## If you want it to look nicer
Your `built` string is `"yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss UTC"`. If you want only the date on the title bar, you can do:

```vb
Dim builtDateOnly = built.Substring(0, 10) ' yyyy-MM-dd
_buildInfo = "   v" & v & "   (" & builtDateOnly & ")"
Me.Text = "Active Magnetic Inspection Main Menu Application   v" & v & "   (" & builtDateOnly & ")"
```

## About dialog
You open `FormAbout()` on `Case "?"`. If you paste `FormAbout.vb` (or just its `OnLoad`/constructor), I can show you exactly where to display `BuildInfo.VersionText` and `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp` there too.

## @testsubject117

After you do that, you can delete the whole block that computes mainAsm/mainVer/mainBuildDate, because it becomes unused - Where is this located at?

## Copilot

It’s in the `FormMainMenu.vb` file you pasted, inside your overridden `OnLoad` method—right after `BuildMenu()`.

Specifically, it starts at this line:

```vbnet
Dim mainAsm As System.Reflection.Assembly = System.Reflection.Assembly.GetExecutingAssembly()
```

…and it ends at this line:

```vbnet
_buildInfo = "   v" & mainVer & "   (" & mainBuildDate & ")"
```

So the whole block you can delete (once you switch to using `v` and `built`) is:

```vbnet
Dim mainAsm As System.Reflection.Assembly = System.Reflection.Assembly.GetExecutingAssembly()

Dim mainVer As String = ""
Try
    mainVer = mainAsm.GetName().Version.ToString()
Catch
    mainVer = "(unknown)"
End Try

Dim mainBuildDate As String = ""
Try
    mainBuildDate = System.IO.File.GetLastWriteTime(Application.ExecutablePath).ToString("yyyy-MM-dd")
Catch ex As Exception
    mainBuildDate = "(unknown)"
    ' Optional while debugging:
    ' MessageBox.Show("Build date read failed: " & ex.Message)
End Try

_buildInfo = "   v" & mainVer & "   (" & mainBuildDate & ")"
```

After deleting that, make sure you still set `_buildInfo` using BuildInfo, e.g.:

```vbnet
_buildInfo = "   v" & v & "   (" & built & ")"
```

And update the title similarly.

If you want, paste your `OnLoad` method after you edit it and I’ll sanity-check it compiles cleanly (no unused variables / missing `_buildInfo` assignment).

## Copilot

You’ve updated it *almost* correctly, but you left one old line in place that will break the build:

You still have:

```vbnet
Me.Text = "Active Magnetic Inspection Main Menu Application   v" & mainVer & "   (" & mainBuildDate & ")"
```

`mainVer` and `mainBuildDate` no longer exist (since you deleted that block), and you’re also setting `Me.Text` **twice**.

## Fix
Delete the second `Me.Text = ... mainVer ... mainBuildDate ...` line entirely.

Your `OnLoad` should look like this (only the relevant section shown):

```vbnet
Protected Overrides Sub OnLoad(e As EventArgs)
    MyBase.OnLoad(e)

    Dim v = BuildInfo.VersionText
    Dim built = BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp

    Me.KeyPreview = True

    BuildMenu()

    _buildInfo = "   v" & v & "   (" & built & ")"
    Me.Text = "Active Magnetic Inspection Main Menu Application   v" & v & "   (" & built & ")"

    lblMainMenu.Text = "MAIN MENU"
    UpdateHeaderClock()
    tmrClock.Interval = 1000
    tmrClock.Start()

    ' ...rest unchanged...
End Sub
```

That will:
- put version/build timestamp into `_buildInfo` (used in your clock header)
- set the window title bar once, using the generated BuildInfo values

If you want the title bar to show only the date (not time), tell me and I’ll adjust the format.

## @testsubject117

how do I publish to a self contained executable instead of a setup.exe

## Copilot

To publish a **self-contained .exe** (no installer / no `setup.exe`), use **Publish** with a **Folder** target and set it to **Self-contained**.

## Visual Studio (GUI)
1) Right‑click the project **AMiOffice** → **Publish**
2) If it asks for a target, choose **Folder** (not “Installer”, not ClickOnce, not MSIX).
3) In the publish settings:
   - **Deployment mode**: **Self-contained**
   - **Target runtime**: pick one, e.g.  
     - `win-x64` (most common)  
     - `win-x86` (older 32-bit machines)  
     - `win-arm64` (ARM devices)
   - **Configuration**: `Release`

4) Click **Publish**.

Result will be in something like:
- `AMiOffice\bin\Release\net10.0-windows\win-x64\publish\`

### Important note about “single EXE”
Self-contained does **not automatically mean one file**. By default you often get an `.exe` plus a bunch of `.dll` files.

If you want **one single .exe file**, enable single-file publish.

---

## Command line (most reliable)
From the project folder:

### Self-contained publish (folder output)
```bat
dotnet publish -c Release -r win-x64 --self-contained true
```

### Self-contained + single EXE
```bat
dotnet publish -c Release -r win-x64 --self-contained true ^
  /p:PublishSingleFile=true
```

### Self-contained + single EXE + no console window (WinForms usually already)
```bat
dotnet publish -c Release -r win-x64 --self-contained true ^
  /p:PublishSingleFile=true /p:IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract=true
```

Output:
- `bin\Release\net10.0-windows\win-x64\publish\AMiOffice.exe`

---

## If you still see `setup.exe`
That usually means you’re using **ClickOnce** or an installer publishing profile. Switch the publish target to **Folder**.

## Quick questions (so I can give exact settings)
1) Are you targeting users on **64-bit Windows** only, or do you need **32-bit** too?
2) Do you want **one single `.exe`** file, or is an `.exe` + supporting `.dll`s in a folder OK?

## @testsubject117

when I right click amioffice and choose publish it asks from a web site, from a unc path or file share or from a cd, dvd, or usb drive

## Copilot

You’re in the **ClickOnce “Publish Wizard”** (that’s why it’s asking website / UNC / CD). That path produces a deployment with **setup.exe**.

To publish a plain self-contained executable, you need a **Folder publish profile** (not ClickOnce), or use `dotnet publish`.

## Option 1 (fastest, no VS UI): publish from command line
From a Command Prompt:

```bat
cd /d C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice
dotnet publish -c Release -r win-x64 --self-contained true /p:PublishSingleFile=true
```

Your output will be here:
- `bin\Release\net10.0-windows\win-x64\publish\`

You can distribute the `.exe` from that folder.

If you want 32-bit instead:
```bat
dotnet publish -c Release -r win-x86 --self-contained true /p:PublishSingleFile=true
```

## Option 2: Switch Visual Studio away from ClickOnce
In Visual Studio:

1) Right-click project → **Properties**
2) Look for a tab/page called **Publish** (or **ClickOnce / Publish**)
3) If you see ClickOnce options, you’re in the ClickOnce pipeline.

What you want is the newer “Publish” experience (Folder profile). Depending on VS edition/settings, it may be under:
- **Build → Publish Selection…**
- or **Project → Publish…** (not the wizard)

If your VS only shows the ClickOnce wizard, use **Option 1** (CLI) — it’s the same underlying .NET publish and is the most reliable.

## One important clarification (so you don’t get surprised)
Even with `/p:PublishSingleFile=true`, self-contained WinForms apps sometimes still produce additional files or extract native bits at runtime. Usually you’ll still have a single `AMiOffice.exe` you can hand out, but it may unpack internally on first run.

---

### Tell me this and I’ll give you the exact best command
Do you need to support **win-x64 only**, or also **win-x86**?

## @testsubject117

Win-x64 only

## Copilot

Use this command to publish a **self-contained win-x64 executable** (no ClickOnce, no setup.exe):

```bat
cd /d C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice
dotnet publish -c Release -r win-x64 --self-contained true /p:PublishSingleFile=true /p:IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract=true
```

Your output will be in:

- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\bin\Release\net10.0-windows\win-x64\publish\`

The file you distribute is:

- `AMiOffice.exe`

If you want it to be as small as possible (optional), try adding trimming **only after you confirm everything works**, because trimming can break WinForms apps:

```bat
dotnet publish -c Release -r win-x64 --self-contained true /p:PublishSingleFile=true /p:IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract=true /p:PublishTrimmed=true
```

(Again: trimming is optional and risky; test thoroughly if you enable it.)

## @testsubject117

how can I change the gui to move away from click once

## Copilot

You can’t “convert” that ClickOnce wizard into the newer Folder-publish UI with a single toggle inside the wizard—what you’re seeing is the **ClickOnce publish pipeline**. To move away from ClickOnce in Visual Studio, do one (or both) of these:

## 1) Turn off ClickOnce in the project properties
1) Right‑click **AMiOffice** → **Properties**
2) Find the **Publish** tab/page (it often literally says **Publish** and has ClickOnce settings).
3) **Uncheck**:
   - **Publish using ClickOnce** (wording varies), or
   - Any “**ClickOnce** / **Install** / **Update**” enable checkbox.

If you don’t see a checkbox, you can still disable it by editing the project file.

### Disable ClickOnce by editing the `.vbproj`
Open `AMiOffice.vbproj` and remove (or set false) any ClickOnce properties if they exist, such as:
- `<PublishUrl>...`
- `<Install>true</Install>`
- `<InstallFrom>Web</InstallFrom>`
- `<UpdateEnabled>true</UpdateEnabled>`
- `<GenerateManifests>true</GenerateManifests>`
- `<SignManifests>true</SignManifests>`
- `<ApplicationRevision>...`
- `<ApplicationVersion>...`

(Those are the usual markers that the project is configured for ClickOnce.)

## 2) Use the “Publish to Folder” workflow instead of the ClickOnce wizard
Depending on your VS version/layout, look for one of these:
- **Build → Publish…**
- **Build → Publish Selection…**
- **Project → Publish…**

What you want is a profile that targets **Folder** (sometimes called “Folder / Local”).

If Visual Studio still insists on the ClickOnce wizard for this project type, then the supported workaround is: **use `dotnet publish`** (what you already did) and optionally create a `.pubxml` profile for folder publish.

## 3) (Best long-term) Create a Folder publish profile (.pubxml) so you can publish from VS
You can add a publish profile file manually:

1) Create this folder if it doesn’t exist:
- `AMiOffice\Properties\PublishProfiles\`

2) Create a file like:
- `AMiOffice\Properties\PublishProfiles\Folder-win-x64.pubxml`

with contents like:

```xml
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <Configuration>Release</Configuration>
    <RuntimeIdentifier>win-x64</RuntimeIdentifier>
    <SelfContained>true</SelfContained>
    <PublishSingleFile>true</PublishSingleFile>
    <IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract>true</IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract>
    <PublishDir>bin\Release\net10.0-windows\win-x64\publish\</PublishDir>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

Then VS usually starts showing that profile in Publish UI (even if ClickOnce exists).

---

What exact Visual Studio are you using (Help → About), and is this a **WinForms VB** project targeting **net10.0-windows** (as in your `.vbproj`)? If you tell me the VS version string, I’ll map the exact menu path to get the Folder publish UI in *your* build.

## Copilot

In **VB/VS 2026**, the dialog you’re getting (website / UNC / CD) is the **ClickOnce Publish Wizard**. For a WinForms `net10.0-windows` app, the simplest way to “move away” from ClickOnce is:

1) **disable/remove ClickOnce settings**, and  
2) **use a Folder publish profile** (so VS stops steering you into the wizard).

### 1) Disable ClickOnce in the project
In Visual Studio:
- Right‑click **AMiOffice** → **Properties**
- Find **Publish** (it will show ClickOnce-related options)
- If there is a checkbox like **“Publish using ClickOnce”** / **“Enable ClickOnce security settings”** / **“The application is available online/offline”**:
  - turn it **off**, or set “available” options to **off**.

If the UI doesn’t offer a single “off” switch, do it via the project file:

**AMiOffice.vbproj** → search for and remove any of these (if present):
- `<PublishUrl>...`
- `<Install>true</Install>`
- `<InstallFrom>Web</InstallFrom>`
- `<UpdateEnabled>true</UpdateEnabled>`
- `<GenerateManifests>true</GenerateManifests>`
- `<SignManifests>true</SignManifests>`
- `<ApplicationRevision>...`
- `<ApplicationVersion>...`
- any property containing `ClickOnce`, `Manifest`, `PublishUrl`, `Install`, `Update`

(If none of these exist, then you’re not “configured” for ClickOnce in the project file—VS is just offering the wizard because you clicked the ClickOnce-style Publish command.)

### 2) Add a Folder publish profile (so you can publish without the wizard)
Create this file:

**`AMiOffice\Properties\PublishProfiles\Folder-win-x64.pubxml`**

with these contents:

```xml
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <Configuration>Release</Configuration>
    <RuntimeIdentifier>win-x64</RuntimeIdentifier>
    <SelfContained>true</SelfContained>
    <PublishSingleFile>true</PublishSingleFile>
    <IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract>true</IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract>
    <PublishDir>bin\Release\net10.0-windows\win-x64\publish\</PublishDir>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

Then in VS:
- **Build** menu → look for **Publish** / **Publish Selection** / **Publish Profiles**
- pick **Folder-win-x64** and publish.

### 3) If VS still only shows the ClickOnce wizard
Don’t fight the UI—publish from inside VS using the same profile via the terminal:

```bat
dotnet publish -c Release /p:PublishProfile=Folder-win-x64
```

That uses the `.pubxml` and produces the folder output with a self-contained `AMiOffice.exe`, no `setup.exe`.

## Copilot

That means your project isn’t using the old **ClickOnce “Publish” property page** at all—so there’s nothing in *Project Properties* to “turn off”. The “website / UNC / CD” prompt you see is simply the **Publish Wizard** entry point, and it’s ClickOnce-oriented.

To move away from ClickOnce **in the VS GUI**, you need to use the **new publish profile (Folder) workflow**, which is *separate* from Project Properties.

## Do this (GUI, no ClickOnce)

### A) Create a Folder publish profile file (one-time)
1) In Solution Explorer, right click the **project** → **Add** → **New Folder**
2) Create: `Properties`
3) Inside that, create folder: `PublishProfiles`
4) Add a new file named: `Folder-win-x64.pubxml`

Paste this into it:

```xml
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <Configuration>Release</Configuration>
    <RuntimeIdentifier>win-x64</RuntimeIdentifier>
    <SelfContained>true</SelfContained>
    <PublishSingleFile>true</PublishSingleFile>
    <IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract>true</IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract>
    <PublishDir>bin\Release\net10.0-windows\win-x64\publish\</PublishDir>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

### B) Publish using the profile (GUI)
Now instead of right-click → Publish Wizard, use:

- **Build → Publish…** (or **Build → Publish Selection…**)

Then pick the profile **Folder-win-x64**.

> If you don’t see “Build → Publish…”, use the command palette/search:
- press `Ctrl+Q` and type **Publish**  
- choose the one that mentions **profiles** (not the wizard).

## If VS still keeps forcing the wizard
Then VS 2026 in your SKU/config simply isn’t exposing the profile-based publish UI for this project type. In that case, the correct workflow is:

### “Still one command, not ClickOnce”
Open **View → Terminal** (or Developer Command Prompt) and run:

```bat
dotnet publish -c Release -r win-x64 --self-contained true /p:PublishSingleFile=true /p:IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract=true
```

That is the official non-ClickOnce publish path and produces no `setup.exe`.

## Copilot

If the **Publish (wizard)** worked before and then you hit the “blank pages” problem, what likely happened is Visual Studio’s cached web/UI state got corrupted. That wizard uses a VS-hosted UI that’s prone to going blank when cache/state breaks.

You have two practical ways forward:

## A) Keep using the wizard (fix the “blank page” problem)
Do these in order:

1) **Close Visual Studio**
2) Delete the solution’s VS cache folder:
   - `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\.vs\`  (delete the whole `.vs` folder)
3) Also delete build artifacts (optional but helps):
   - `AMiOffice\bin\`
   - `AMiOffice\obj\`
4) Reopen the solution (`.slnx`) and try **Publish** again.

If it still opens blank, also try:
- Run: `devenv /resetuserdata` (this resets VS user data; more disruptive)
- Or at least: `devenv /safemode` to test if an extension is breaking it

## B) Avoid the wizard entirely (recommended)
Even if you fix the wizard, it’s still ClickOnce-oriented and will keep trying to generate `setup.exe`.

For a **self-contained exe**, the most reliable path is the command you already used:

```bat
dotnet publish -c Release -r win-x64 --self-contained true /p:PublishSingleFile=true /p:IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract=true
```

This completely sidesteps the wizard UI (so blank pages won’t matter).

---

When it goes blank, is it:
1) the **Solution Explorer / designer** that’s blank, or
2) specifically the **Publish Wizard window/page** that’s blank?

If you tell me which one, I’ll give you the exact targeted fix (there are two different cache locations depending on which UI is failing).

## Copilot

You can’t really “remove ClickOnce” from that **Publish Wizard**—that wizard *is* the ClickOnce/installer-style flow. The way to get to a **non‑ClickOnce** publish in VS is to stop using that wizard entry point and use a **Folder publish profile** (or the CLI).

Since you specifically want “remove ClickOnce” (i.e., no `setup.exe`), here are the two clean options:

## Option 1 (recommended): keep it in Visual Studio, but use a Folder publish profile
1) In your project folder, create:
   - `Properties\PublishProfiles\Folder-win-x64.pubxml`

2) Put this in the file:

```xml
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <Configuration>Release</Configuration>
    <RuntimeIdentifier>win-x64</RuntimeIdentifier>
    <SelfContained>true</SelfContained>
    <PublishSingleFile>true</PublishSingleFile>
    <IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract>true</IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract>
    <PublishDir>bin\Release\net10.0-windows\win-x64\publish\</PublishDir>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

3) Then in VS, don’t right‑click → Publish Wizard. Instead:
- Use **Build → Publish…** (or use `Ctrl+Q` and type **Publish** and pick the one that mentions **profiles**)

If your VS 2026 install doesn’t show a profile-based Publish command, you can still “publish from VS” via the Terminal with the same profile:

```bat
dotnet publish -c Release /p:PublishProfile=Folder-win-x64
```

That produces the self-contained `AMiOffice.exe` and **no setup.exe**.

## Option 2: If you just want one command, always
Run this from VS Terminal (View → Terminal):

```bat
dotnet publish -c Release -r win-x64 --self-contained true /p:PublishSingleFile=true /p:IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract=true
```

## Key point
- **Right‑click → Publish** (website/UNC/CD wizard) = ClickOnce/installer path → tends to generate `setup.exe`.
- **dotnet publish / Folder profile** = app publish path → gives you the executable you want.

If you tell me what you see when you press `Ctrl+Q` and type `publish` (the list of commands that appear), I can tell you exactly which one to use to avoid the ClickOnce wizard in your VS 2026.

## @testsubject117

even if I don't right click the amioffice project and choose properties, and use build, publish selection, it is still the same wizard

## Copilot

Then in your VS 2026 install, the only wired-up “Publish” UI for this project is the **ClickOnce Publish Wizard**. There isn’t a separate GUI switch you can flip to make that wizard become “folder publish” (no `setup.exe`). In other words: **you can’t remove ClickOnce from that wizard**—you have to bypass it.

The closest “GUI-like” workflow that avoids ClickOnce is to publish via a **publish profile** using `dotnet publish` (you can run it from Visual Studio’s Terminal, so you never leave VS).

## Do this (one-time setup): create a non-ClickOnce publish profile
Create this file:

**`AMiOffice\Properties\PublishProfiles\Folder-win-x64.pubxml`**

```xml
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <Configuration>Release</Configuration>
    <RuntimeIdentifier>win-x64</RuntimeIdentifier>
    <SelfContained>true</SelfContained>
    <PublishSingleFile>true</PublishSingleFile>
    <IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract>true</IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract>
    <PublishDir>bin\Release\net10.0-windows\win-x64\publish\</PublishDir>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

## Then publish (repeatable, no wizard)
Open **View → Terminal** in VS and run:

```bat
dotnet publish -c Release /p:PublishProfile=Folder-win-x64
```

Output:
- `AMiOffice\bin\Release\net10.0-windows\win-x64\publish\AMiOffice.exe`

No `setup.exe`.

## Make it “one click” inside VS
If you want it to be truly one-click, the usual solution is adding a small script and running it:

### Add `publish.bat` next to your `.slnx` (or in the project folder)
```bat
@echo off
cd /d "%~dp0AMiOffice"
dotnet publish -c Release /p:PublishProfile=Folder-win-x64
pause
```

Then you double-click `publish.bat` (or run it from Terminal). That’s effectively your “publish button” without ClickOnce.

---

When you say you want “self contained executable instead of setup.exe”, do you mean:
1) **single file** `AMiOffice.exe` only, or
2) **a folder** containing `AMiOffice.exe` plus some DLLs is acceptable?

(The command/profile above aims for single-file, self-contained.)

What likely happened when you removed/re-added the project is that Visual Studio **lost your old publish profile/settings**, and now the only publish entry point it can find is the ClickOnce wizard.

### What to check (quick, concrete)
Open your project folder in File Explorer and look for this folder:

- `AMiOffice\Properties\PublishProfiles\`

If “PublishProfiles” is missing, or it’s empty, that explains the change.

If it exists, see if you have one or more `*.pubxml` files (those are the non-ClickOnce publish profiles).

### If you DO find old `.pubxml` files
Then VS should be able to publish without ClickOnce—your UI just isn’t picking them up.

Tell me what files are in:
- `AMiOffice\Properties\PublishProfiles\`

and I’ll tell you exactly what to do next.

### If you DO NOT find `.pubxml` files
Then recreate the profile (this is the “restore what you used to have” step):

1) Create:
- `AMiOffice\Properties\PublishProfiles\Folder-win-x64.pubxml`

2) Put this in it:

```xml
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <Configuration>Release</Configuration>
    <RuntimeIdentifier>win-x64</RuntimeIdentifier>
    <SelfContained>true</SelfContained>
    <PublishSingleFile>true</PublishSingleFile>
    <IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract>true</IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract>

  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

3) Publish using the profile (works even if VS GUI routes to ClickOnce):
```bat
dotnet publish -c Release /p:PublishProfile=Folder-win-x64
```

Re-adding can drop:
- the `.pubxml` files (if they weren’t in the repo / got deleted),
- or the **solution-level reference** to the publish profile,
- leaving VS with only the ClickOnce wizard option.

The right-click/Build-menu “Publish” entry you’re hitting is mapped to ClickOnce for VB WinForms projects in your configuration. Even when a `.pubxml` exists, VS may not surface the profile UI, but **`dotnet publish /p:PublishProfile=...` always works**.

## @testsubject117

it generates and entire folder full of .exe, .dll, .config, .json and folders like cd, de, es, fe, it etc.

## Copilot

That’s normal for a .NET desktop app publish. Two separate things are getting mixed together:

1) **“Self-contained”** = includes the .NET runtime so the target machine doesn’t need to install .NET.
2) **“Single-file”** = tries to bundle into one `.exe`.

You can be self-contained and still get a folder of files (especially for WinForms/WPF + localization satellite assemblies).

### Why you’re seeing `cd, de, es, fr, it, ...` folders
Those are **satellite resource assemblies** (localized resources) coming from .NET/WinForms dependencies. Single-file doesn’t always eliminate them depending on settings and what gets bundled/extracted.

## If your goal is “no installer” (no setup.exe)
You already succeeded. Just ship the **publish folder** (zip it) and run the main `.exe`.

## If your goal is “ONE single .exe file”
You can try, but with WinForms it may still extract or emit extra files.

### 1) Confirm your publish profile actually requests single-file
Open `Properties\PublishProfiles\Folder-win-x64.pubxml` and make sure it contains:

```xml
<PublishSingleFile>true</PublishSingleFile>
<SelfContained>true</SelfContained>
<RuntimeIdentifier>win-x64</RuntimeIdentifier>
```

### 2) Add these properties to reduce the “folder explosion”
Add/ensure these in the same `.pubxml`:

```xml
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <Configuration>Release</Configuration>
    <RuntimeIdentifier>win-x64</RuntimeIdentifier>
    <SelfContained>true</SelfContained>

    <PublishSingleFile>true</PublishSingleFile>
    <IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract>true</IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract>

    <!-- tries to embed managed assemblies into the single file -->
    <IncludeAllContentForSelfExtract>true</IncludeAllContentForSelfExtract>

    <!-- optional: reduces extra files but can have side-effects; test carefully -->
    <InvariantGlobalization>true</InvariantGlobalization>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

Then republish:

```bat
dotnet publish "C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj" -c Release /p:PublishProfile=Folder-win-x64
```

### Important reality check
Even with all of the above, you may still see:
- `.json` / `.config`
- localization folders
- or the app may produce a single EXE but **extract resources at runtime** to a temp folder

So the most dependable distribution method for WinForms is:
- **zip the publish folder** and distribute that.

## Quick question (so I can tailor the exact `.pubxml`)
Do you *need* the app to work in multiple languages (those `de/es/fr/it` resources), or is English-only OK? If English-only is OK, we can be more aggressive about trimming globalization/resources (with the caveat that it can break some formatting/culture behaviors).

## @testsubject117

only english. and it still publishes to a folder full of files. that's fine, I'll zip the folder and transfer it to computers that way

## Copilot

Zipping the publish folder and copying it is a solid approach, and it’s very common for WinForms deployments.

Do this:

1) Publish (your existing command/profile is fine).
2) Zip **the entire** publish output folder (not just the EXE), e.g.
   - `...\bin\Release\net10.0-windows\win-x64\publish\`
3) On the target PC, unzip to something like:
   - `C:\AMiOffice\` (or a user folder)
4) Run `AMiOffice.exe`.

A couple of practical tips when distributing the zip:

- **Keep all files together** in the same folder structure—don’t move just the `.exe` out by itself.
- If Windows shows “blocked” / SmartScreen warnings after copying:
  - Right-click the **zip** → Properties → **Unblock** (if present) *before* extracting, or
  - After extracting, right-click `AMiOffice.exe` → Properties → **Unblock**.
- If you want an easy “installer-like” experience without setup.exe, you can ship a shortcut (`.lnk`) or a small `Run_AMiOffice.bat` that starts the exe.

If you paste your `Folder-win-x64.pubxml`, I can suggest a couple of safe “English-only” tweaks (like reducing satellite resource folders) — but since you’re OK with zipping the folder, you’re already at a good stopping point.

## @testsubject117

when I publish should the build number increase?

## Copilot

Only if **your assembly version is configured to change on each build/publish**.

### What usually happens (most SDK-style .NET projects)
- **Publishing does not automatically increment the assembly version/build number.**
- You’ll keep seeing the same version unless you explicitly set versioning rules.

### What you’re showing in your app
You’re currently displaying:

- `BuildInfo.VersionText` (whatever you generate into that file)
- `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp` (timestamp)

So:
- The **timestamp should change every publish/build** (if `BuildInfo` is regenerated each time).
- The **version number will only change** if you bump it via project settings or your versioning mechanism.

### Where the version comes from (common cases)
Check your `AMiOffice.vbproj` for entries like:

- `<Version>1.2.3</Version>`
- `<AssemblyVersion>1.2.3.4</AssemblyVersion>`
- `<FileVersion>1.2.3.4</FileVersion>`
- `<InformationalVersion>...</InformationalVersion>`

If those are fixed values, the “build number” won’t increase unless you edit them (or use a script/CI to do it).

### If you want it to increase automatically
Tell me which “build number” you mean:

1) **The 4-part Windows file version** (1.0.0.123), or  
2) **A semantic version** (1.0.123), or  
3) **Just the timestamp is enough** (what you already have)

…and whether this is **manual publishing on your PC** or **GitHub Actions/CI**. Then I’ll recommend the simplest versioning setup for your workflow.

## @testsubject117

yes, increase automatically - in the about dialogue that we previously created as well as the window title. I understand the version may not increment, but each time I do a new build, I'm seeing the about dialogue and the windows title dialogue stay the same

## Copilot

If **both** the About dialog and the window title stay the same after a new build/publish, then one of these is happening:

1) `BuildInfo` is **not being regenerated** on build, or  
2) you’re running an **old copy** of the app (old publish folder / old exe), or  
3) the displayed values come from **assembly version** which isn’t changing.

Since you already switched `FormMainMenu` to use `BuildInfo.VersionText` + `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp`, the **timestamp should change every build** *if BuildInfo is generated at build time*.

## Step 1: Prove whether you’re running the fresh build
After you publish, run the EXE from the publish folder and confirm its path:
- `...\bin\Release\net10.0-windows\win-x64\publish\AMiOffice.exe`

Also do a quick “hard refresh” build:
```bat
dotnet clean "C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj"
dotnet publish "C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj" -c Release /p:PublishProfile=Folder-win-x64
```

If the timestamp still doesn’t change, then BuildInfo isn’t regenerating.

## Step 2: Make BuildInfo update automatically every build (recommended fix)
The most reliable method is: **generate BuildInfo at build time from MSBuild**.

### A) Add a real source file you compile (instead of relying on something in `obj\`)
Create: `BuildInfo.vb` in your project (in the AMiOffice project, not in obj).

Example:

```vbnet
' BuildInfo.vb
Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Public Module BuildInfo
    Public ReadOnly Property VersionText As String
        Get
            Return My.Application.Info.Version.ToString()
        End Get
    End Property

    Public ReadOnly Property BuildTimestamp As String
        Get
            ' This will be compiled in; you can replace it with MSBuild generation for true build-time stamp.
            Return "(not set)"
        End Get
    End Property
End Module
```

That alone won’t stamp a changing timestamp, so the next step is what makes it automatic.

### B) Generate the timestamp constant at build time
In your **AMiOffice.vbproj**, add a target that overwrites `BuildInfo.g.vb` on every build and includes it in compilation.

If you paste your `AMiOffice.vbproj`, I’ll give you an exact drop-in block. (VB projects can differ slightly.)

## Step 3: Quick “tell me what you have” questions (so I give the exact fix)
1) Where is your current `BuildInfo` coming from?
   - Do you have a `BuildInfo.vb` file in the project, **or** is it only something generated under `obj\...`?
2) Paste your `AMiOffice.vbproj` (or at least the parts containing `<Version>`, `<AssemblyVersion>`, and anything mentioning `BuildInfo`).

With those two, I can give you an exact MSBuild snippet that makes **BuildTimestamp change every build** and optionally makes a **build counter increment** automatically too.

(You’re correct: versions don’t auto-increment by default—but timestamps absolutely can, and they should change every build once generation is wired correctly.)

## @testsubject117

so the current build version displays a time stamp?

## Copilot

Not automatically.

There are **two different things** here:

1) **“Build version”** (e.g., `1.0.0.0` or `2.3.1`)  
   - This comes from your project’s version settings (`<Version>`, `<AssemblyVersion>`, etc.) or from VB’s `My.Application.Info.Version`.
   - It **does not include a timestamp** unless you *deliberately* put a timestamp into `InformationalVersion` (and even then it’s not the numeric version).

2) **A “build timestamp”** (e.g., `2026-04-17 14:22:10 UTC`)  
   - This is a separate value you must generate yourself (like your `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp`).
   - It **can** change every build/publish, but only if `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp` is being regenerated on build.

### In your code
You display:

```vb
Dim v = BuildInfo.VersionText       ' version string
Dim built = BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp ' timestamp string
```

So:
- `VersionText` = version (normally stable unless you bump it)
- `BuildTimestamp` = timestamp (should change every build **if** you regenerate it)

### Why yours might not be changing
If `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp` is hard-coded (or generated once and not regenerated), it will stay the same forever.

### Quick check
Search your solution for `BuildTimestamp`:
- If you find something like `Public Const BuildTimestamp = "..."`, that string will only change when the file is regenerated.

If you paste the `BuildInfo` module/class you currently have (the file contents), I can tell you immediately whether it’s hard-coded and why it isn’t updating—and how to make it update every build.

## @testsubject117

ok, so to clarify, I want a version number with build numbers that increment next to it, like Ver 1.0 build 0.1 etc

## Copilot

You want **two numbers**:

- a human “product version” like **1.0**
- plus an **auto-incrementing build number** like **Build 42** (or 0.1 / 0.2, etc.)

The clean way to do this in SDK-style `net10.0-windows` is:

1) keep `<Version>` as your manual “1.0”
2) generate a build number automatically at build/publish time
3) expose both through `BuildInfo` and display them in the title/About

### Recommendation (simple + robust)
Use a **date-based build number** (always increases and no state to maintain), e.g.
- `Build 202604171530` (YYYYMMDDHHmm)
or
- `Build 20260417.1530`

This avoids the hardest part: a persistent counter that survives rebuilds/cleans.

If you *really* want “0.1, 0.2, 0.3” as a counter, you need a persisted file/counter (or Git commit count, or CI run number). I can do that too—just slightly more moving parts.

---

## Option A (recommended): auto build number from timestamp (always increases)
### 1) Add this MSBuild target to `AMiOffice.vbproj`
Open `AMiOffice.vbproj` and add this near the bottom (before `</Project>`):

```xml
<Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <!-- Manual product version -->
    <ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>

    <!-- Auto build stamp -->
    <BuildStamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyyMMdd.HHmm"))</BuildStamp>

    <BuildInfoFile>$(IntermediateOutputPath)BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <WriteLinesToFile
    File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
    Overwrite="true"
    Lines="
Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Public Module BuildInfo
    Public Const ProductVersion As String = &quot;$(ProductVersion)&quot;
    Public Const BuildNumber As String = &quot;$(BuildStamp)&quot;

    Public ReadOnly Property VersionText As String
        Get
            Return ProductVersion
        End Get
    End Property

    Public ReadOnly Property BuildText As String
        Get
            Return &quot;Build &quot; &amp; BuildNumber
        End Get
    End Property
End Module
" />
</Target>
```

This generates `BuildInfo.g.vb` into `obj\...` each build and compiles it.

### 2) In your UI
Change your display to:

```vbnet
Dim v = BuildInfo.VersionText
Dim b = BuildInfo.BuildText
Me.Text = $"AMiOffice  Ver {v}  {b}"
```

And same for your About dialog.

Result example:
- `AMiOffice Ver 1.0 Build 20260417.1530`

---

## Option B: true incrementing counter (0.1, 0.2, 0.3…)
To do that locally (not CI), you need to store the counter somewhere (e.g., a `buildcounter.txt` in the repo) and increment it during build. That’s doable, but I need to know:
- do you want the counter to increment on **every Build** or **only on Publish**?

---

### Quick choice
Which do you want?

1) **Always-increasing timestamp build** (recommended, zero maintenance), or  
2) **Persistent counter** that goes 0.1, 0.2, 0.3… (increments every build or every publish)?

## @testsubject117

does option a replace entries in the amioffice.vbproj or add entries

## Copilot

**Option A adds entries** to `AMiOffice.vbproj`; it does **not** replace anything by default.

- You paste the `<Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" ...>` block **near the bottom of the `.vbproj`**, usually right before the closing `</Project>`.
- It can coexist with any existing `<PropertyGroup>` settings like `<Version>`, `<AssemblyVersion>`, etc.

### The only time you’d “replace” something
If you *already* have a `BuildInfo.vb` file in your project that defines `Public Module BuildInfo`, then generating another `BuildInfo` module will cause a **duplicate definition** compile error.

In that case, you either:
- delete/rename the existing `BuildInfo.vb`, **or**
- change the generated module name (e.g., `Public Module AutoBuildInfo`) and update your code accordingly.

### Quick check before you add it
Search your solution for `Module BuildInfo` or `Class BuildInfo`.

- **If you find none:** you can add Option A as-is.
- **If you find one:** tell me what that file contains and I’ll adjust the MSBuild-generated name so you don’t conflict.

If you paste your current `BuildInfo` file (or confirm it exists and where), I’ll tell you exactly which route to take.

Once you paste it, I will:
1) review it for any existing versioning / ClickOnce / BuildInfo generation,
2) produce a **complete, drop-in replacement** `AMiOffice.vbproj` that:
   - keeps all your current references/settings,
   - adds automatic build stamping (Ver `1.0` + Build `yyyyMMdd.HHmm` or a counter—your choice),
   - avoids duplicate `BuildInfo` definitions.

Before you paste: also tell me which behavior you want to trigger the increment/stamp:
- **on every Build** (`dotnet build` / VS Build), or
- **only on Publish** (`dotnet publish` / your publish profile).

  </PropertyGroup>

Option Explicit On

Public Module BuildInfo
    Public Const ProductVersion As String = &quot;$(ProductVersion)&quot;
    Public Const BuildNumber As String = &quot;$(BuildStamp)&quot;

    Public ReadOnly Property VersionText As String
        Get
            Return ProductVersion

## Copilot

That snippet is **not** your full `AMiOffice.vbproj`—it’s only the MSBuild `<Target>` you pasted earlier.

### What I can do right now
You can add this target into your existing `.vbproj` as-is, but you must ensure **the generated file is actually included in compilation** (SDK-style projects usually include `obj\**\*.vb` automatically *only if* it’s marked as a Compile item; to be safe, we explicitly include it).

Here is the **correct, complete target** you should insert (this *replaces* the target you pasted), and it will work reliably:

```xml
<Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <!-- Set your manual "Ver X.Y" here -->
    <ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>

    <!-- Auto build stamp (UTC) -->
    <BuildStamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyyMMdd.HHmm"))</BuildStamp>

    <BuildInfoFile>$(IntermediateOutputPath)BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Generate the file -->
  <WriteLinesToFile
    File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
    Overwrite="true"
    Lines="
Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Public Module BuildInfo
    Public Const ProductVersion As String = &quot;$(ProductVersion)&quot;
    Public Const BuildNumber As String = &quot;$(BuildStamp)&quot;

    Public ReadOnly Property VersionText As String
        Get
            Return ProductVersion
        End Get
    End Property

    Public ReadOnly Property BuildText As String
        Get
            Return &quot;Build &quot; &amp; BuildNumber
        End Get
    End Property

    Public ReadOnly Property VersionAndBuildText As String
        Get
            Return &quot;Ver &quot; &amp; VersionText &amp; &quot; &quot; &amp; BuildText
        End Get
    End Property
End Module
" />

  <!-- Ensure it's compiled -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Target>
```

### What I still need to “generate code to completely replace” your `.vbproj`
To produce a full replacement `.vbproj` (without accidentally breaking references, resources, application framework settings, etc.), I need the **entire** contents of your existing `AMiOffice.vbproj`.

Please paste the whole file (everything from `<Project ...>` to `</Project>`). If it’s long, that’s fine.

### One important warning before you apply it
Make sure you **do not already have** a `BuildInfo.vb` in your project defining `Public Module BuildInfo`. If you do, you’ll get a “type/module BuildInfo is already defined” compile error. If you’re not sure, tell me and I’ll adjust the generated module name to avoid collision (e.g., `AutoBuildInfo`).

      <Generator>VbMyResourcesResXFileCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Resources.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </EmbeddedResource>

    <None Update="My Project\Application.myapp">
      <Generator>MyApplicationCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Application.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
    <None Update="My Project\Settings.settings">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>SettingsSingleFileGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Settings.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>

    <WriteLinesToFile File="$(BuildInfoFile)" Overwrite="true" Lines="Option Strict On&#xD;&#xA;Option Explicit On&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;Friend Module BuildInfo&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = &quot;$(BuildTimestamp)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const VersionText As String = &quot;$(Version)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;End Module&#xD;&#xA;" />

    <!-- Add AFTER generation so it can't be missing -->
    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>

## Copilot

Here’s a cleaned-up, **complete replacement** for your `AMiOffice.vbproj` that will:

- keep your current settings,
- generate a **stable product version** (e.g. `1.0`) that you control in one place,
- generate an **auto build number that changes every build** (timestamp-based, always increasing),
- generate `BuildInfo.g.vb` every build and compile it,
- avoid duplicate-compile problems.

It also keeps your existing `BuildTimestamp` idea, but adds a **BuildNumber** and a combined **DisplayVersion** string you can use in both the About dialog and window title.

### Replace your entire `AMiOffice.vbproj` with this

```xml name=AMiOffice.vbproj
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>
    <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
    <MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>

    <!--
      Manual product version (what you want to show as "Ver 1.0").
      Change ONLY this when you want a new major/minor.
    -->
    <ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>

    <!-- Keep assembly/file versions stable unless you want to manage them too -->
    <AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
    <FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>

    <!-- Keep NuGet/package/app version stable too; we'll show build separately -->
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>

    <GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
    <InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>

    <!-- Explicit path so we don't depend on IntermediateOutputPath -->
    <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>

    <Copyright>Copyright Kirk Saffell 2026</Copyright>
    <Title>AMiOfficeApplication</Title>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Remove="ESC Logo.jpg" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Include="ESC Logo.jpg">
      <CopyToOutputDirectory>Never</CopyToOutputDirectory>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Import Include="System.Data" />
    <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
    <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Update="My Project\Application.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Application.myapp</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Resources.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Resources.resx</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Settings.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTimeSharedInput>True</DesignTimeSharedInput>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Settings.settings</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Update="My Project\Resources.resx">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My.Resources</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>VbMyResourcesResXFileCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Resources.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Update="My Project\Application.myapp">
      <Generator>MyApplicationCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Application.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
    <None Update="My Project\Settings.settings">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>SettingsSingleFileGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Settings.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- IMPORTANT: do NOT compile a stray root BuildInfo.g.vb -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Prevent any default globs from also including the generated file (avoids duplicate Compile items) -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Generate BuildInfo.g.vb and add it to compilation -->
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <!-- UTC timestamp shown in About / title if you want -->
      <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss 'UTC'"))</BuildTimestamp>

      <!-- Auto build number (increasing, compact). Example: 20260417.1530 -->
      <BuildNumber>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyyMMdd.HHmm"))</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <MakeDir Directories="$([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName('$(BuildInfoFile)'))" />

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="Option Strict On&#xD;&#xA;Option Explicit On&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;Friend Module BuildInfo&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const ProductVersion As String = &quot;$(ProductVersion)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const BuildNumber As String = &quot;$(BuildNumber)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = &quot;$(BuildTimestamp)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend ReadOnly Property DisplayVersion As String&#xD;&#xA;        Get&#xD;&#xA;            Return &quot;Ver &quot; &amp; ProductVersion &amp; &quot; Build &quot; &amp; BuildNumber&#xD;&#xA;        End Get&#xD;&#xA;    End Property&#xD;&#xA;End Module&#xD;&#xA;" />

    <!-- Add AFTER generation so it can't be missing -->
    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>
```

## How to use it in your title/About
Use the combined string:

- `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion`

Example:

```vbnet
Me.Text = "AMiOffice - " & BuildInfo.DisplayVersion
```

And in About dialog label:
```vbnet
lblVersion.Text = BuildInfo.DisplayVersion & " (" & BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp & ")"
```

## Note about “0.1, 0.2…” specifically
The above uses a **timestamp build number** (e.g. `20260417.1530`) which always increases and needs no storage. If you truly want **0.1 / 0.2 / 0.3** as a counter, we can do it, but it requires storing/incrementing a counter file (or using Git commit count / CI run number).

If you confirm “timestamp build number is fine”, you’re done after replacing the `.vbproj` and switching your UI to `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion`.

## @testsubject117

I replaced the contents of amioffice.vbproj and now get bc30456 'VersionText' is not a member of 'BuildInfo'.

## Copilot

Your UI code is still referencing `BuildInfo.VersionText`, but in the replacement `.vbproj` I generated `BuildInfo` with:

- `ProductVersion`
- `BuildNumber`
- `BuildTimestamp`
- `DisplayVersion`

…and **no `VersionText`** property/const. That’s why you get:

> BC30456 `'VersionText' is not a member of 'BuildInfo'`

### Fix option 1 (smallest change): add `VersionText` back into the generated BuildInfo
Edit your `AMiOffice.vbproj` target and replace the `WriteLinesToFile` `Lines=` content with this version (adds `VersionText` and keeps everything else):

```xml
<WriteLinesToFile
  File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
  Overwrite="true"
  Lines="Option Strict On&#xD;&#xA;Option Explicit On&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;Friend Module BuildInfo&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const ProductVersion As String = &quot;$(ProductVersion)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const VersionText As String = &quot;$(ProductVersion)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const BuildNumber As String = &quot;$(BuildNumber)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = &quot;$(BuildTimestamp)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend ReadOnly Property DisplayVersion As String&#xD;&#xA;        Get&#xD;&#xA;            Return &quot;Ver &quot; &amp; VersionText &amp; &quot; Build &quot; &amp; BuildNumber&#xD;&#xA;        End Get&#xD;&#xA;    End Property&#xD;&#xA;End Module&#xD;&#xA;" />
```

Now your existing references to `BuildInfo.VersionText` will compile again.

### Fix option 2 (change your code): switch to `DisplayVersion`
Wherever you have:

```vbnet
BuildInfo.VersionText
```

Change to:

```vbnet
BuildInfo.DisplayVersion
```

(And keep using `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp` if you want the time shown separately.)

### After changing: force regeneration
Because this file is generated into `obj\...`, do a clean rebuild so it regenerates:

- In VS: **Build → Clean Solution**, then **Build → Rebuild Solution**
or CLI:
```bat
dotnet clean
dotnet build
```

If you want, paste the exact line(s) in your code where `VersionText` is referenced (and whether you want it to show `1.0` only or `1.0.0.0`), and I’ll tell you the best mapping.

## @testsubject117

what files contain BuildInfo.VersionText

## Copilot

I can’t directly search your repo from here because I don’t have an accessible `owner/repo` to query (and `testsubject117/AMiOffice` isn’t accessible via my tools right now). But you can find every reference in seconds locally in Visual Studio.

## In Visual Studio (best)
1) Press **Ctrl+Shift+F** (Find in Files)
2) Find what: `BuildInfo.VersionText`
3) Look in: **Entire Solution**
4) Click **Find All**

That will list every file and line.

## From command line (fast)
From your solution folder:

**PowerShell**
```powershell
Get-ChildItem -Recurse -File | Select-String -Pattern "BuildInfo\.VersionText" | ForEach-Object {
  "{0}:{1}: {2}" -f $_.Path, $_.LineNumber, $_.Line.Trim()
}
```

**CMD**
```bat
findstr /S /N /I "BuildInfo.VersionText" *.*
```

## Where it usually is in a WinForms VB app
Most commonly you’ll find it in:
- your main form (e.g., `FormMainMenu.vb`, `MainForm.vb`)
- the About dialog (e.g., `AboutBox*.vb`)
- any helper module that sets the window title

## Note
You won’t typically see it in the generated file itself (`obj\...\BuildInfo.g.vb`) unless you wrote it there; it’s normally referenced by your UI code.

If you paste the “Find All” results list (or just the filenames), I can tell you which ones to change and what to change them to (`BuildInfo.DisplayVersion` vs re-adding `VersionText`).

## @testsubject117

ok, I made the changes but now the window has an extra v at the beginning it says vVersion 1.0 Build

## Copilot

That extra **`v`** is coming from your *UI string*, not from MSBuild.

You likely have something like:

```vbnet
Me.Text = "v" & BuildInfo.DisplayVersion
```

or:

```vbnet
Me.Text = "vVersion " & BuildInfo.VersionText & " Build " & BuildInfo.BuildNumber
```

…and now that `DisplayVersion` already contains `"Ver ... Build ..."`, you’re effectively prefixing it with another “v” (or mixing “v” + “Version”).

## Fix it (pick one format)

### Option 1: Use `DisplayVersion` directly (recommended)
Set the title like:

```vbnet
Me.Text = "AMiOffice - " & BuildInfo.DisplayVersion
```

And make sure you remove any `"v"` / `"Version "` prefixes you previously added.

### Option 2: If you want it to say “Version” instead of “Ver”
Change the generated string in your `.vbproj`:

In the `WriteLinesToFile` content, change:

```vbnet
Return "Ver " & VersionText & " Build " & BuildNumber
```

to:

```vbnet
Return "Version " & VersionText & " Build " & BuildNumber
```

Then **Clean + Rebuild** so `BuildInfo.g.vb` regenerates.

## Quick pinpoint
Search your code for these strings and remove/adjust:
- `"v"`
- `"Ver "`
- `"Version "`

In VS: **Ctrl+Shift+F** → search for `"Version "` and `"v" & BuildInfo` / `Me.Text = "v"`.

If you paste the exact line where you set the window title, I’ll rewrite it to match exactly the title text you want.

## @testsubject117

that line currently looks like this: Me.Text = "Active Magnetic Inspection Main Menu Application   v" & v & "   (" & built & ")" do I completely replace it with the line you suggested?: Me.Text = "AMiOffice - " & BuildInfo.DisplayVersion

## Copilot

Yes—because your existing line is *manually* adding `"v"` and also appending a timestamp in parentheses. If you switch to `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion`, you should stop composing `v`/`built` yourself (unless you want the timestamp too).

Pick one of these replacements depending on what you want to show:

## A) Version + build only (cleanest)
```vbnet
Me.Text = "Active Magnetic Inspection Main Menu Application   " & BuildInfo.DisplayVersion
```

## B) Version + build + timestamp (closest to what you had)
```vbnet
Me.Text = "Active Magnetic Inspection Main Menu Application   " & BuildInfo.DisplayVersion & "   (" & BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp & ")"
```

### What *not* to do
Don’t keep the `" v" & v` part if `DisplayVersion` already contains `"Ver 1.0 Build 20260417.1530"`—that’s what caused the extra “v” / duplicated wording.

After changing it, do **Clean + Rebuild** once to ensure the generated `BuildInfo.g.vb` is up to date.

## @testsubject117

I replaced that line and I still see the extra v

## Copilot

If you replaced that line and you **still** see the extra `v`, then one of these is true:

1) there is **another place** setting the window title after your line runs, or  
2) you’re running an **older build/publish** than the one you just changed, or  
3) your generated `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion` itself still starts with something like `"vVersion ..."` (unlikely, but possible if the generated text was changed).

Do these checks in this order—they’ll pinpoint it quickly.

## 1) Verify what `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion` actually is at runtime
Temporarily add:

```vbnet
MessageBox.Show(BuildInfo.DisplayVersion, "DisplayVersion")
```

Run the app and see exactly what it shows.

- If the message box shows `Ver 1.0 Build ...` (no extra v), then your extra `v` is coming from **some other code setting the title**.
- If it shows `vVersion ...` or `vVer ...`, then we fix the generated string in the `.vbproj`.

## 2) Find every place in code that sets the title
In Visual Studio: **Ctrl+Shift+F** and search for:

- `Me.Text =`
- `.Text = "Active Magnetic`
- `Main Menu Application`
- `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion`
- `BuildInfo.VersionText`
- `v"`

You’re looking for another assignment like:
```vbnet
Me.Text = ...
```
or
```vbnet
FormMainMenu.Text = ...
```

Common culprits:
- `Form_Shown`
- `Form_Activated`
- `Timer_Tick`
- after login / after selecting a menu item

## 3) Confirm you’re running the updated binary
If you’re launching from a published folder, you might be running an older copy.

Add this temporarily:

```vbnet
MessageBox.Show(Application.ExecutablePath, "EXE Path")
```

That tells you exactly which `AMiOffice.exe` you’re running.

## 4) Force regeneration of BuildInfo.g.vb
Because it’s generated into `obj\...`, do:

- **Build → Clean Solution**
- **Build → Rebuild Solution**

(or CLI: `dotnet clean` then `dotnet build`)

## If you paste two things, I’ll tell you exactly which case it is
1) What the message box shows for `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion`
2) The first ~30 characters of the window title as it appears (exactly)

That will let me say “it’s coming from another title assignment” vs “it’s in DisplayVersion” immediately.

## @testsubject117

FormMainMenu.vb has Code	_buildInfo = "   v" & v & "   (" & built & ")"

## Copilot

That’s the source of the extra **`v`**. You fixed the `Me.Text = ...` line, but you still have another string (`_buildInfo`) that *still* prepends `"   v"` and is likely being appended to the title somewhere else.

### Fix it by switching `_buildInfo` to use the new generated string
Replace that line with one of these:

## Option A (version/build only)
```vbnet
_buildInfo = "   " & BuildInfo.DisplayVersion
```

## Option B (version/build + timestamp, matching your old style)
```vbnet
_buildInfo = "   " & BuildInfo.DisplayVersion & "   (" & BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp & ")"
```

Then, wherever you build the final title (often something like `Me.Text = BaseTitle & _buildInfo`), it won’t add an extra `v`.

### Also remove old variables if you’re still computing them
If you still have:
```vbnet
Dim v = BuildInfo.VersionText
Dim built = BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp
```
You can either keep them (harmless), or simplify to:
```vbnet
Dim built = BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp
```
(or remove both if unused).

### Do a Clean + Rebuild
Because BuildInfo is generated, after changing code do:
- **Build → Clean Solution**
- **Build → Rebuild Solution**

If you paste the code block around where `_buildInfo` is used to set `Me.Text`, I can give you the exact final version so there’s only one place composing the title.
