- `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion` for the **version + build**
- `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp` only if you want the timestamp shown too
- avoid manually adding `"v"` anywhere else (since `DisplayVersion` already formats it)

If you want, paste your final desired window title text (exactly), and I’ll suggest the cleanest single place in `FormMainMenu` to assemble it so it doesn’t drift again.

## @testsubject117

what do you mean final desired window title text?

## Copilot

I mean: **exactly what you want the title bar to display, as a complete example string**—including spacing, punctuation, whether you want the timestamp, and whether you want “Ver” vs “Version”.

For example, pick one (or describe your own):

1) `AMiOffice - Ver 1.0 Build 20260417.1530`  
2) `Active Magnetic Inspection - Ver 1.0 Build 20260417.1530 (2026-04-17 15:30:12 UTC)`  
3) `AMiOffice v1.0 (Build 42)`  
4) `Active Magnetic Inspection Main Menu Application`

Once you tell me the exact format you prefer, I can tell you the **one best place** to build it (so you don’t have title logic split between `Me.Text = ...` and `_buildInfo = ...` in different handlers).

## @testsubject117

I like the AMiOffice v1.0 (Build 42) but does that require lots of extra steps and changes?

## Copilot

No—**not lots of extra steps**, but “Build 42” specifically does require *one* extra concept: a **persistent counter** (because a pure timestamp can’t naturally become 41 → 42 → 43 unless you store it somewhere or derive it from something like Git/CI).

You have 3 practical ways to get `Build 42`, in order of simplicity:

## Option 1 (easiest, no persistence): keep timestamp but format like your example
You’ll get:
- `AMiOffice v1.0 (Build 20260417.1530)`

**No extra steps**, just change the string format.

## Option 2 (recommended if you publish via GitHub Actions): use the CI run number
If you publish in CI, you can use the workflow run number as “Build 42”.
**Very clean**, but only works when built in CI.

## Option 3 (local builds, true Build 42): store a counter file and increment on Publish
This is still not “lots”, but it’s more moving parts than timestamps:
- add a text file like `build-counter.txt` (checked into your repo)
- MSBuild reads it, increments it, writes it back, and generates `BuildInfo.g.vb`

**One important caveat:** auto-incrementing a file on build can cause constant git changes; so I usually do it **only on Publish**, not every Build.

---

### Quick question (determines the best solution)
Do you want the number to increment when you:
1) press **Build** in Visual Studio, **every time**, or
2) run your `dotnet publish ...` (your `amoffpub`) **only when publishing**?

If you answer that, I’ll give you the exact minimal edits to your `.vbproj` to display exactly:

`AMiOffice v1.0 (Build 42)`

(without introducing annoying side effects).

## @testsubject117

only when I publish

## Copilot

Perfect—incrementing **only on publish** is the least annoying (no constant git diffs during normal builds).

Below is a simple, reliable approach that requires **one small file** in your project plus a small change to your `.vbproj`.

## 1) Create a counter file (once)
Create this file in your project root (same folder as `AMiOffice.vbproj`):

```text name=build-counter.txt
0
```

Commit it (so it persists across machines).

## 2) Update your `.vbproj` (replace your current GenerateBuildInfo target)
In `AMiOffice.vbproj`, replace the entire `<Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" ...>` with this **publish-only** pair of targets.

This does two things:
- on **Publish**: increments `build-counter.txt`
- on **Build and Publish**: generates `BuildInfo.g.vb` (so builds still compile), but the number only increments on publish

```xml name=AMiOffice.vbproj
  <!-- Increment build number ONLY when publishing -->
  <Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Ensure the file exists -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <!-- Read current number -->
    <ReadLinesFromFile File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Write incremented number back -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="$(BuildNumber)" />
  </Target>

  <!-- Generate BuildInfo for builds and publishes -->
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>

      <!-- Your manual product version -->
      <ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>

      <!-- If BuildNumber wasn't set by Publish (normal Build), read current file without increment -->
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
      <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss 'UTC'"))</BuildTimestamp>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Make sure counter exists -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <!-- If BuildNumber is empty (normal build), read current counter -->
    <ReadLinesFromFile Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''" File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines2" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''">
      <BuildNumber>%(BuildCounterLines2.Identity)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <MakeDir Directories="$([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName('$(BuildInfoFile)'))" />

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="Option Strict On&#xD;&#xA;Option Explicit On&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;Friend Module BuildInfo&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const ProductVersion As String = &quot;$(ProductVersion)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const BuildNumber As String = &quot;$(BuildNumber)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = &quot;$(BuildTimestamp)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend ReadOnly Property DisplayVersion As String&#xD;&#xA;        Get&#xD;&#xA;            Return &quot;AMiOffice v&quot; &amp; ProductVersion &amp; &quot; (Build &quot; &amp; BuildNumber &amp; &quot;)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;        End Get&#xD;&#xA;    End Property&#xD;&#xA;End Module&#xD;&#xA;" />

    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>
```

### What you’ll see
- Normal **Build**: build number stays whatever is in `build-counter.txt`
- **Publish**: increments (0 → 1 → 2 → 3 …) and the published EXE will show the new number

## 3) Use it in your window title/About
Set title to:

```vbnet
Me.Text = BuildInfo.DisplayVersion
```

(or prefix with your longer app name if you want)

## Important note
Every time you publish, `build-counter.txt` will change—so you’ll want to **commit that change** (or at least be aware it will show as a modified file in git).

If you want, tell me whether you’re using git and whether you publish from VS or `dotnet publish`, and I’ll tailor the target to avoid any “file is read-only” / permissions / line-ending gotchas.

## @testsubject117

Commit it (so it persists across machines). what does this mean?

## Copilot

“Commit it” means: **add that file to your Git repository and make a Git commit**, so the file (and its current build number) is saved in source control and will come along when you copy/cloned the repo to another computer.

If you *don’t* commit it, then:

- it will exist only on the computer where you created it, and
- another computer (or a fresh clone) won’t have the counter, so the build number would reset or be missing.

### What you do (typical Git workflow)
From your repo folder:

```bat
git status
git add build-counter.txt
git commit -m "Add publish build counter"
```

After that, if you work on another machine and do:

```bat
git clone ...
```

…it will include `build-counter.txt` and your build number will continue from the same place.

### If you’re *not* using Git
Then “commit it” doesn’t apply. In that case you’d need to manually copy `build-counter.txt` along with your project folder whenever you move the project to a new machine.

If you tell me whether you’re using GitHub / git for this project (and whether you ever build on more than one PC), I can recommend the best build-number approach (counter file vs timestamp vs CI run number).

## @testsubject117

not using git

## Copilot

Then you don’t need to “commit” anything—there’s no source control to save it into.

What it means for the build counter approach:

- The file `build-counter.txt` will live in your project folder.
- Each time you **Publish**, MSBuild will update that file (0 → 1 → 2 → 3…).
- As long as you keep building/publishing from the **same project folder on the same PC**, the number will keep increasing.

### If you move the project to another computer
You must copy `build-counter.txt` along with the project, or the counter will reset.

Two easy ways to handle that:

1) **Copy the whole project folder** (recommended), ensuring `build-counter.txt` goes with it.
2) If you already moved the project and forgot the file, just create `build-counter.txt` again and put the last number you used inside (e.g., `42`).

### Alternative (no file to carry around)
If you don’t want any counter file at all, use the timestamp build number instead. It always increases and works on any machine without keeping state, but it looks like:
- `AMiOffice v1.0 (Build 20260417.1530)`

If you tell me whether you ever plan to build/publish on more than one PC, I’ll tell you which option will be least hassle.

## @testsubject117

only publishing on one pc, prefer to use build and not time stamp

## Copilot

Then the **publish-only counter file** approach is exactly what you want, and it’s not much work:

- add `build-counter.txt` once
- MSBuild increments it **only when you publish**
- your title/About shows `AMiOffice v1.0 (Build 42)` (no timestamp required)

## 1) Create the counter file
In the same folder as `AMiOffice.vbproj`, create:

```text name=build-counter.txt
0
```

## 2) Update your `AMiOffice.vbproj`
You already have a `GenerateBuildInfo` target. Replace your current target with the following **two targets** (one increments on Publish; one generates BuildInfo for compilation):

> Paste these just before `</Project>` and remove your old `GenerateBuildInfo` target to avoid duplicates.

```xml name=AMiOffice.vbproj
  <!-- Increment build number ONLY when publishing -->
  <Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Ensure the file exists -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <!-- Read current number -->
    <ReadLinesFromFile File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <!-- Increment -->
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Write back -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="$(BuildNumber)" />
  </Target>

  <!-- Generate BuildInfo.g.vb for BOTH normal builds and publishes -->
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <!-- manual version you want to show as v1.0 -->
      <ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>

      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
      <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Ensure the file exists -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <!-- If BuildNumber wasn't set by Publish, just read the current counter -->
    <ReadLinesFromFile Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''" File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines2" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''">
      <BuildNumber>%(BuildCounterLines2.Identity)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <MakeDir Directories="$([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName('$(BuildInfoFile)'))" />

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="Option Strict On&#xD;&#xA;Option Explicit On&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;Friend Module BuildInfo&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const ProductVersion As String = &quot;$(ProductVersion)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const BuildNumber As String = &quot;$(BuildNumber)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend ReadOnly Property DisplayVersion As String&#xD;&#xA;        Get&#xD;&#xA;            Return &quot;AMiOffice v&quot; &amp; ProductVersion &amp; &quot; (Build &quot; &amp; BuildNumber &amp; &quot;)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;        End Get&#xD;&#xA;    End Property&#xD;&#xA;End Module&#xD;&#xA;" />

    <ItemGroup>
      <!-- Avoid duplicates, then include generated file -->
      <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>
```

## 3) Set your window title / About text
Use:

```vbnet
Me.Text = BuildInfo.DisplayVersion
```

(or `"Active Magnetic Inspection - " & BuildInfo.DisplayVersion`)

## How it behaves
- Click **Build**: build number stays the same (reads `build-counter.txt`)
- Click **Publish**: build number increments by 1, and the published app shows the new number

If you tell me how you publish (Visual Studio “Publish…” button vs `dotnet publish` command), I can confirm the `BeforeTargets="Publish"` hook matches your exact publish method.

please generate a new AMiOffice.vbproj file here with the replaced contents so I can paste in

## Copilot

```xml name=AMiOffice.vbproj
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>
    <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
    <MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>

    <AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
    <FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>

    <GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
    <InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>

    <!-- Explicit path so we don't depend on IntermediateOutputPath (which can be overridden) -->
    <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>

    <Copyright>Copyright Kirk Saffell 2026</Copyright>
    <Title>AMiOfficeApplication</Title>

    <!-- Manual product version shown in UI as: AMiOffice v1.0 (Build N) -->
    <ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Remove="ESC Logo.jpg" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Include="ESC Logo.jpg">
      <CopyToOutputDirectory>Never</CopyToOutputDirectory>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Import Include="System.Data" />
    <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
    <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Update="My Project\Application.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Application.myapp</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Resources.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Resources.resx</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Settings.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTimeSharedInput>True</DesignTimeSharedInput>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Settings.settings</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Update="My Project\Resources.resx">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My.Resources</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>VbMyResourcesResXFileCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Resources.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Update="My Project\Application.myapp">
      <Generator>MyApplicationCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Application.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
    <None Update="My Project\Settings.settings">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>SettingsSingleFileGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Settings.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- IMPORTANT: do NOT compile a stray root BuildInfo.g.vb -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Prevent any default globs from also including the generated file (avoids duplicate Compile items) -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Increment build number ONLY when publishing -->
  <Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Ensure the file exists -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <!-- Read current number -->
    <ReadLinesFromFile File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <!-- Increment -->
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Write back -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="$(BuildNumber)" />
  </Target>

  <!-- Generate BuildInfo.g.vb for BOTH normal builds and publishes -->
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Ensure the file exists -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <!-- If BuildNumber wasn't set by Publish, just read the current counter -->
    <ReadLinesFromFile Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''" File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines2" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''">
      <BuildNumber>%(BuildCounterLines2.Identity)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <MakeDir Directories="$([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName('$(BuildInfoFile)'))" />

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="Option Strict On&#xD;&#xA;Option Explicit On&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;Friend Module BuildInfo&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const ProductVersion As String = &quot;$(ProductVersion)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const BuildNumber As String = &quot;$(BuildNumber)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend ReadOnly Property DisplayVersion As String&#xD;&#xA;        Get&#xD;&#xA;            Return &quot;AMiOffice v&quot; &amp; ProductVersion &amp; &quot; (Build &quot; &amp; BuildNumber &amp; &quot;)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;        End Get&#xD;&#xA;    End Property&#xD;&#xA;End Module&#xD;&#xA;" />

    <ItemGroup>
      <!-- Avoid duplicates, then include generated file -->
      <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>
```

## @testsubject117

now I am unable to load the project. I get: the project file could not be loaded. unexpected end of file has occurred. the following element are not closed. Target, project line 118, position 2. c:\users\capnkirk\source\repos\AMiOFfice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj

## Copilot

That error means the XML in `AMiOffice.vbproj` is **truncated or missing a closing tag** (usually `</Target>` or `</Project>`), or Visual Studio pasted only part of it.

In the file I generated, there should be **exactly**:
- one `</Target>` for `IncrementBuildCounter`
- one `</Target>` for `GenerateBuildInfo`
- one final `</Project>`

If you got “Unexpected end of file… Target, Project line 118”, it almost certainly means the file ends before one of those closing tags.

## Fastest fix
1) Open `AMiOffice.vbproj` in **Notepad** (not VS).
2) Scroll to the very bottom.
3) Make sure the last ~10 lines look like this:

```xml
    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>
```

If `</Target>` or `</Project>` is missing, add it and save.

## Safer: paste this corrected full file
Below is a full, known-good `AMiOffice.vbproj`. Delete everything in your `.vbproj` and paste **all** of this (ensure your editor does not cut it off):

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>
    <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
    <MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>

    <AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
    <FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>

    <GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
    <InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>

    <!-- Explicit path so we don't depend on IntermediateOutputPath (which can be overridden) -->
    <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>

    <Copyright>Copyright Kirk Saffell 2026</Copyright>
    <Title>AMiOfficeApplication</Title>

    <!-- Manual product version shown in UI as: AMiOffice v1.0 (Build N) -->
    <ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Remove="ESC Logo.jpg" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Include="ESC Logo.jpg">
      <CopyToOutputDirectory>Never</CopyToOutputDirectory>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Import Include="System.Data" />
    <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
    <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Update="My Project\Application.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Application.myapp</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Resources.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Resources.resx</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Settings.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTimeSharedInput>True</DesignTimeSharedInput>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Settings.settings</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Update="My Project\Resources.resx">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My.Resources</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>VbMyResourcesResXFileCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Resources.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Update="My Project\Application.myapp">
      <Generator>MyApplicationCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Application.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
    <None Update="My Project\Settings.settings">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>SettingsSingleFileGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Settings.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- IMPORTANT: do NOT compile a stray root BuildInfo.g.vb -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Prevent any default globs from also including the generated file (avoids duplicate Compile items) -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Increment build number ONLY when publishing -->
  <Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <ReadLinesFromFile File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="$(BuildNumber)" />
  </Target>

  <!-- Generate BuildInfo.g.vb for BOTH normal builds and publishes -->
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <ReadLinesFromFile Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''" File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines2" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''">
      <BuildNumber>%(BuildCounterLines2.Identity)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <MakeDir Directories="$([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName('$(BuildInfoFile)'))" />

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="Option Strict On&#xD;&#xA;Option Explicit On&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;Friend Module BuildInfo&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const ProductVersion As String = &quot;$(ProductVersion)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const BuildNumber As String = &quot;$(BuildNumber)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend ReadOnly Property DisplayVersion As String&#xD;&#xA;        Get&#xD;&#xA;            Return &quot;AMiOffice v&quot; &amp; ProductVersion &amp; &quot; (Build &quot; &amp; BuildNumber &amp; &quot;)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;        End Get&#xD;&#xA;    End Property&#xD;&#xA;End Module&#xD;&#xA;" />

    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>
```

## If it still fails
Copy/paste the **last 30 lines** of your current `.vbproj` here—especially around line ~118—and I’ll tell you exactly which tag is missing.

## Copilot

Your file is actually **well‑formed XML** (all `Target` and `Project` tags are closed) — so the “unexpected end of file… Target, Project line 118” error is almost certainly coming from **a truncated/partial file on disk** or a **hidden character/encoding issue** after you pasted.

That said, there are two practical fixes that eliminate the common causes:

1) ensure there’s a newline at end of file and nothing after `</Project>`
2) avoid multi-line MSBuild task attributes that sometimes get mangled by copy/paste (especially if the editor inserts smart quotes or invalid characters)

Below is a **sanitized, paste-safe** replacement `AMiOffice.vbproj` where I:
- keep all your content,
- keep build-counter publish increment,
- keep BuildInfo generation,
- but reformat the XML to be less fragile (no indentation/tab oddities, and the big `WriteLinesToFile` is still one attribute but the XML is otherwise clean).

### Replace your entire `AMiOffice.vbproj` with this

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>
    <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
    <MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>

    <AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
    <FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>

    <GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
    <InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>

    <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>

    <Copyright>Copyright Kirk Saffell 2026</Copyright>
    <Title>AMiOfficeApplication</Title>
    <ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Remove="ESC Logo.jpg" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Include="ESC Logo.jpg">
      <CopyToOutputDirectory>Never</CopyToOutputDirectory>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Import Include="System.Data" />
    <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
    <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Update="My Project\Application.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Application.myapp</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>
    <Compile Update="My Project\Resources.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Resources.resx</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>
    <Compile Update="My Project\Settings.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTimeSharedInput>True</DesignTimeSharedInput>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Settings.settings</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Update="My Project\Resources.resx">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My.Resources</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>VbMyResourcesResXFileCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Resources.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Update="My Project\Application.myapp">
      <Generator>MyApplicationCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Application.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
    <None Update="My Project\Settings.settings">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>SettingsSingleFileGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Settings.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')" File="$(BuildCounterFile)" Overwrite="true" Lines="0" />

    <ReadLinesFromFile File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile File="$(BuildCounterFile)" Overwrite="true" Lines="$(BuildNumber)" />
  </Target>

  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')" File="$(BuildCounterFile)" Overwrite="true" Lines="0" />

    <ReadLinesFromFile Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''" File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines2" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''">
      <BuildNumber>%(BuildCounterLines2.Identity)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <MakeDir Directories="$([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName('$(BuildInfoFile)'))" />

    <WriteLinesToFile File="$(BuildInfoFile)" Overwrite="true" Lines="Option Strict On&#xD;&#xA;Option Explicit On&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;Friend Module BuildInfo&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const ProductVersion As String = &quot;$(ProductVersion)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const BuildNumber As String = &quot;$(BuildNumber)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend ReadOnly Property DisplayVersion As String&#xD;&#xA;        Get&#xD;&#xA;            Return &quot;AMiOffice v&quot; &amp; ProductVersion &amp; &quot; (Build &quot; &amp; BuildNumber &amp; &quot;)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;        End Get&#xD;&#xA;    End Property&#xD;&#xA;End Module&#xD;&#xA;" />

    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>
</Project>
```

## If Visual Studio STILL says “Unexpected end of file”
That means the file on disk is not what you pasted here. Do this:

1) Open `AMiOffice.vbproj` in **Notepad**
2) Click **Save As…**
3) Encoding: choose **UTF-8** (or “UTF-8 with BOM”)
4) Save
5) Re-open the solution

Also confirm the file does **not** end mid-line (sometimes copy/paste truncates). The last line must be exactly `</Project>`.

If you paste the **exact text of line 110–130** from the file on disk (Notepad, not VS), I can point to the exact character/line that’s broken.

## @testsubject117

get the same error

## Copilot

Then the `.vbproj` on disk is still ending prematurely (or contains an invalid hidden character). Since the XML you pasted here is fine, this is almost certainly a **file/encoding/truncation** problem, not an MSBuild logic problem.

Do this exact recovery sequence (it fixes this class of error reliably):

## 1) Restore to a minimal loadable project file (to get the project to open)
1. Close Visual Studio.
2. Open `C:\Users\capnkirk\source\repos\AMiOFfice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj` in **Notepad**.
3. Replace the entire contents with this **minimal** vbproj and save:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

4. Re-open Visual Studio and load the solution.
   - If this still fails, the issue is not your XML; it’s file access/VS cache/something else (rare).

## 2) If minimal works: the problem is in how the full file is being saved
Now we fix the save/encoding and put your full file back.

### Save with a known-good encoding
In Notepad:
- **File → Save As…**
- Encoding: choose **UTF-8**
- Save (overwrite the same file)

(If you’re using an older Notepad that doesn’t show encoding, use Notepad++: Encoding → UTF-8)

## 3) Put the full vbproj back, but validate it before VS opens it
Before reopening VS, validate the XML quickly:

### Option A (PowerShell)
Run this in PowerShell:

```powershell
[xml](Get-Content "C:\Users\capnkirk\source\repos\AMiOFfice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj" -Raw) | Out-Null
"XML OK"
```

- If it throws an error, it will tell you the **real line/position** where the file is broken (which will confirm truncation).

## 4) Most common root cause: file got cut off during paste
This happens a lot when pasting into VS’s XML editor and it auto-formats or fails mid paste.

**Best practice:** paste into Notepad (or Notepad++) instead of Visual Studio, then save.

---

### One thing I need from you to pinpoint it precisely
Please paste the **last 25 lines** of the actual file that’s on disk right now (open in Notepad, scroll to bottom, copy last lines).  

If the file is truncated, your “last 25 lines” will reveal exactly which closing tag is missing and whether there’s garbage after it.

it looks like I had saved a version called amioffice recovered after I recovered from the blank screen issue. That was the working project name after, however I was inadvertently opening the original project file which throws that line 118 position 2 error no matter what. I've now loaded AMiOffice(recovered) however I'm getting an error that states: BC30113 'AMiOffice(Recovered)' is not a valid name and cannot be used as the root namespace name

## Copilot

That error is because **VB’s RootNamespace must be a valid VB identifier**, and `AMiOffice(Recovered)` is not (parentheses aren’t allowed).

You have two clean options:

## Option A (recommended): set RootNamespace to `AMiOffice`
1) In Visual Studio, right‑click the project **AMiOffice(Recovered)** → **Properties**
2) Go to the **Application** tab
3) Set **Root namespace** to:
   - `AMiOffice`
4) Save

That’s it.

## Option B: rename the project (also good)
If you don’t want “(Recovered)” anywhere:
1) In Solution Explorer, right‑click project → **Rename** → `AMiOffice`
2) Also rename the `.vbproj` file on disk to `AMiOffice.vbproj` (optional but keeps things tidy)
3) Update solution references if prompted

## If you prefer editing the `.vbproj` directly
Open the recovered `.vbproj` and add or change this inside your main `<PropertyGroup>`:

```xml
<RootNamespace>AMiOffice</RootNamespace>
```

Example:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  ...
  <RootNamespace>AMiOffice</RootNamespace>
</PropertyGroup>
```

After doing that, rebuild.

If you paste the top `<PropertyGroup>` from `AMiOffice(Recovered).vbproj`, I can show you exactly where to insert `<RootNamespace>AMiOffice</RootNamespace>` so it matches your current settings.

## @testsubject117

there is no place that says root namespace, only Startup Object which is set to AMiOffice(recovered).Form1

## Copilot

That means you’re looking at the **wrong project properties page** (or VS isn’t showing the VB “Application” page because the project is being treated oddly), but the underlying fix is the same:

1) Your **project’s root namespace / assembly name** likely became `AMiOffice(Recovered)`.
2) Your **StartupObject** is also pointing to `AMiOffice(recovered).Form1`, which is invalid for the same reason (parentheses).

### Fix it directly in the `.vbproj` (works even if the UI page is missing)

Open `AMiOffice(Recovered).vbproj` and in the first `<PropertyGroup>`:

#### A) Add/fix RootNamespace + AssemblyName
Set both to a valid identifier, e.g. `AMiOfficeRecovered` or just `AMiOffice`.

I recommend just `AMiOffice`:

```xml
<RootNamespace>AMiOffice</RootNamespace>
<AssemblyName>AMiOffice</AssemblyName>
```

#### B) Fix StartupObject
Change this:

```xml
<StartupObject>AMiOffice(recovered).Form1</StartupObject>
```

to (pick the one that matches your main form):

```xml
<StartupObject>AMiOffice.Form1</StartupObject>
```

or if you use the VB Application Framework like earlier:

```xml
<StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>
```

### Here is the exact corrected top section you can paste in
Replace your project’s **first `<PropertyGroup>...</PropertyGroup>`** with this (keeping your other properties below it intact):

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
  <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>

  <!-- FIX: must be valid identifiers (no parentheses) -->
  <RootNamespace>AMiOffice</RootNamespace>
  <AssemblyName>AMiOffice</AssemblyName>

  <!-- If you use the VB Application Framework, use this: -->
  <StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>

  <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
  <MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>

  <AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
  <FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
  <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>

  <GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
  <InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>

  <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>

  <Copyright>Copyright Kirk Saffell 2026</Copyright>
  <Title>AMiOfficeApplication</Title>
  <ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>
</PropertyGroup>
```

### After saving
- Close and reopen the solution (or at least unload/reload the project)
- Build

### One quick confirmation question
Do you actually have `My Project\Application.myapp` in this recovered project (VB Application Framework enabled)?
- If **yes**, keep `AMiOffice.My.MyApplication`
- If **no**, tell me what your main form class is (Form1? FormMainMenu?), and I’ll give you the correct `StartupObject` line.

## @testsubject117

46 errors

## Copilot

46 errors usually means the namespace/startup object change fixed the *first* problem, but now the project is compiling against the wrong “My” settings or the root namespace doesn’t match what your code expects.

I can get you to the exact fix quickly, but I need to see **what the errors are** (the first 5–10 are enough).

## Do this
In Visual Studio:
1) **Build → Rebuild Solution**
2) In the **Error List**, click the **Code** column to sort (or just look at the top)
3) Copy/paste here:
   - the **first 10 errors** (including error code + message)
   - and 1 line that shows the **project name** being built (AMiOffice, AMiOffice(Recovered), etc.)

## Meanwhile, the two most common causes after “(Recovered)” renames

### 1) RootNamespace doesn’t match your code’s namespaces
If your code files contain things like:
```vbnet
Namespace AMiOffice
```
then set:
```xml
<RootNamespace>AMiOffice</RootNamespace>
```
If your code **doesn’t** declare a namespace (typical VB WinForms), the root namespace *becomes* the implicit namespace for forms/modules. Changing it can break references like `My.Forms.FormMainMenu`.

### 2) StartupObject should be `My.MyApplication` only if VB Application Framework is enabled
If the recovered project lost the `Application.myapp` setup, then:
- `AMiOffice.My.MyApplication` won’t exist → cascades into many errors.

**Quick test:** do you have this file in the project?
`My Project\Application.myapp`

- If **yes** → StartupObject should be `AMiOffice.My.MyApplication`
- If **no** → StartupObject should be your main form, e.g. `AMiOffice.FormMainMenu` (or `AMiOffice.Form1`)

## One very likely “46 errors” scenario
Your project is named `AMiOffice(Recovered)` but your generated/default namespace is now something else, so designer partial classes don’t match.

### Best “stabilize everything” settings (usually)
In `.vbproj` set:

```xml
<RootNamespace>AMiOffice</RootNamespace>
<AssemblyName>AMiOffice</AssemblyName>
<MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>
<UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
<StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>
```

…but I don’t want to guess—paste the first errors and I’ll tell you exactly which of these is wrong.

If you can paste **just the first 10 error lines**, I’ll respond with the precise edits (and where) to get back to 0 errors.

## @testsubject117

'Public Sub New()' has multiple definitions with identical signatures.
'Protected Overrides Sub OnCreateMainForm()' has multiple definitions with identical signatures.
'Protected Overrides Function OnInitialize(commandLineArgs As ReadOnlyCollection(Of String)) As Boolean' has multiple definitions with identical signatures.
Attribute 'TargetFrameworkAttribute' cannot be applied multiple times.
module 'BuildInfo' and module 'BuildInfo' conflict in namespace 'AMiOffice'.
Attribute 'TargetFrameworkAttribute' cannot be applied multiple times.
Attribute 'AssemblyCompanyAttribute' cannot be applied multiple times.
Attribute 'AssemblyConfigurationAttribute' cannot be applied multiple times.
Attribute 'AssemblyCopyrightAttribute' cannot be applied multiple times.
Attribute 'AssemblyFileVersionAttribute' cannot be applied multiple times.

## Copilot

You’ve got **duplicate source files being compiled** (likely the “Recovered” project is including the same files twice, or you have two generated files / AssemblyInfo files). That’s why you see:

- multiple definitions of `Public Sub New()` (same class compiled twice)
- multiple definitions of `MyApplication` overrides (`OnCreateMainForm`, `OnInitialize`)
- `TargetFrameworkAttribute` and other assembly attributes applied multiple times
- `BuildInfo` module conflict (two `BuildInfo` modules compiled into the same namespace)

### Fix: remove the duplicate compile items (most common: you now have two BuildInfo files + an extra AssemblyInfo file)

Do these steps in this order.

## 1) Fix the BuildInfo module conflict
You currently have **two BuildInfo modules** being compiled. Common causes:
- a manually-created `BuildInfo.vb` or `BuildInfo.g.vb` in the project folder
- plus the generated one in `obj\...\BuildInfo.g.vb`

### What to do
In Solution Explorer, search for files named:
- `BuildInfo.vb`
- `BuildInfo.g.vb`
- anything similar in the project root

If you find a real file in the project (not under `obj\`), **exclude it from project** or rename it.

### In the `.vbproj`, add this to ensure you never compile any stray BuildInfo generated files outside obj
Put this near the bottom (before `</Project>`):

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <!-- Ensure we don't compile a copied/generated BuildInfo from anywhere else -->
  <Compile Remove="**\BuildInfo.g.vb" />
  <Compile Remove="**\BuildInfo.vb" Condition="Exists('BuildInfo.vb')" />
</ItemGroup>
```

(We’ll re-include the one we generate via `$(BuildInfoFile)` as you already do.)

## 2) Fix “Attribute … cannot be applied multiple times” (AssemblyInfo duplication)
This happens when **both** are true:
- SDK project auto-generates assembly info (`<GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>`)
- AND you also have an `AssemblyInfo.vb` (or similar) in `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb` with attributes.

### What to do (pick one)
**Option A (recommended): keep SDK generation, remove old AssemblyInfo file**
- If you have `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb`, remove it from the project (or delete it).

**Option B: keep the old AssemblyInfo.vb, disable auto-generation**
Add / set in `<PropertyGroup>`:

```xml
<GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
```

Most WinForms VB projects that came from older templates have `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb`, so Option B is often quickest.

## 3) Fix duplicate `MyApplication` / Form constructors
The `Public Sub New()` and `MyApplication` overrides errors strongly suggest your project is compiling the same `.vb` files twice.

The *most common reason in a recovered/renamed situation* is: you now have **both**:
- the original folder still included by wildcard `**\*.vb`
- and a second copy of the same files (e.g., in a “Recovered” folder or duplicate project folder) also included

### Quick check
Look in Solution Explorer for duplicate items like:
- `Form1.vb` appearing twice
- `FormMainMenu.vb` appearing twice
- `My Project\Application.Designer.vb` appearing twice

If you see duplicates, one of them is coming from a second path.

## What I need from you (to give the exact 2–3 line vbproj fix)
Please paste **just these things**:

1) The list of `.vb` files at the project root (or a screenshot of Solution Explorer showing duplicates)
2) Whether you have `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb` (yes/no)
3) Whether you have a real file named `BuildInfo.g.vb` or `BuildInfo.vb` outside `obj\` (yes/no)

### If you want a “likely fix” right now
Do this immediately (it often clears most/all of these):

1) Set in `.vbproj`:
```xml
<GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
```

2) Ensure you do **not** have a real `BuildInfo.g.vb` in your project folder. Delete/exclude it if present.

3) Delete build artifacts:
- close Visual Studio
- delete the project’s `bin` and `obj` folders
- reopen and rebuild

Reply with (2) and (3) above and I’ll tell you exactly which option to use and what to remove so you get back to **0 errors**.

## @testsubject117

Nothing found for BuildInfo.vb. there is 2 buildinfo.g.vb files found, one under amioffice\amioffice\obj\debug\net10.0-windows and the other under that same path but for release instead of debug

## Copilot

That part is normal: you’ll always have **two generated files** (one per configuration) under `obj\Debug\...` and `obj\Release\...`. Those **do not** conflict with each other because you only build one configuration at a time.

So the `module 'BuildInfo' ... conflict` error means you have **another `BuildInfo` module being compiled from somewhere else**, most commonly:

- the generated file is being included **twice** in the same build (once via your explicit `<Compile Include="...BuildInfo.g.vb" />`, and once via the SDK’s default glob include), or
- you have a second copy of the project’s source files being globbed in (causing *everything* to be duplicated), which would also explain the `Public Sub New()` duplicates and the `TargetFrameworkAttribute` duplicates.

### Fix 1 (very likely): stop explicitly including the generated file (let SDK default include it)
Right now you have both:
- default SDK behavior: includes `**\*.vb` (including anything under `obj` **if it gets picked up**, depending on settings)
- plus your explicit `<Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" ... />`

The safest approach: generate the file into `obj` but **do not explicitly include it**; instead generate it into a known “Generated” folder *outside* `obj`, or explicitly exclude `obj` from Compile includes and include only the generated file once.

#### Easiest reliable fix: generate into `Generated\BuildInfo.g.vb` (not obj)
1) Create folder: `Generated` under your project.
2) Change this property:

```xml
<BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\Generated\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
```

3) In your target, keep:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
  <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
</ItemGroup>
```

This guarantees it’s included exactly once and avoids obj weirdness.

### Fix 2 (also required): AssemblyInfo duplication
Your errors `TargetFrameworkAttribute` and `AssemblyCompanyAttribute` etc. “cannot be applied multiple times” almost always means you have an `AssemblyInfo.vb` file **and** `<GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>`.

#### Do this now
In the `.vbproj` set:

```xml
<GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
```

(Unless you want to delete `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb`, but disabling generation is quickest.)

### Fix 3: the `MyApplication` / `Sub New()` duplicates
Those happen when the same designer/codebehind is compiled twice. If after Fix 1 + Fix 2 you *still* get those, it means the project is including source files from two locations.

Common cause: in a recovered copy, the `.vbproj` lives in a folder that contains *another copy* of the project as a subfolder, and the SDK default glob pulls both in.

Example bad layout:
- `AMiOffice\`  
  - `Form1.vb`
  - `AMiOffice\`  ← second nested copy also containing `Form1.vb`

If that’s your case, we can add an exclusion like:
```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Remove="AMiOffice\**\*.vb" />
</ItemGroup>
```
…but I don’t want to guess the folder name.

---

## What to do next (minimal steps)
1) Open `.vbproj` and set:
```xml
<GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
```

2) Change BuildInfo output to `Generated\BuildInfo.g.vb` as described (and create the folder).

3) Close VS, delete `bin` and `obj`, reopen, Rebuild.

---

## One question to pinpoint the remaining duplicates
In File Explorer, is your project folder structured like this (nested duplicate folder)?

`...\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\...`

You mentioned: `amioffice\amioffice\obj\...` which looks nested. If you confirm what folders exist at:
`C:\Users\capnkirk\source\repos\AMiOFfice\AMiOffice\`
(list the immediate subfolders), I’ll give you the exact `<Compile Remove="...">` exclusion to stop the duplicate `Sub New()` / `MyApplication` errors.

## @testsubject117

I do not see this property at all in the xml: <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\Generated\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile> What I do see is <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>

## Copilot

Right — you won’t see the `Generated\...` version until you **change** it. You currently have it pointing into `obj\...`, and we want to move it out of `obj` to avoid “compiled twice” problems.

Make these **two edits** in your `.vbproj`:

## 1) Change the `BuildInfoFile` property (in the main `<PropertyGroup>`)
Replace this line:

```xml
<BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
```

with:

```xml
<BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\Generated\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
```

(Then create a folder named `Generated` next to the `.vbproj`.)

## 2) Disable auto-generated AssemblyInfo (fixes the duplicate attributes)
In the same `<PropertyGroup>`, change/add:

```xml
<GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
```

If you currently have it as `true`, change it to `false`.

---

### Then do the cleanup
1) Close Visual Studio  
2) Delete the `bin` and `obj` folders under the project  
3) Reopen VS → **Rebuild**

---

### Why this helps
- Putting `BuildInfo.g.vb` under `obj` can lead to it being picked up by SDK default compile includes **and** your explicit `<Compile Include=...>`, creating the `BuildInfo` conflict.
- Disabling `GenerateAssemblyInfo` prevents the SDK from generating attributes that you likely already have in `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb`, which is what causes the `TargetFrameworkAttribute` / `AssemblyCompanyAttribute` duplicates.

---

If after those two edits you *still* see “`Public Sub New()` has multiple definitions”, then we’ll address the “nested AMiOffice\AMiOffice” duplicate source inclusion next—but do these two first because they usually eliminate most of the 46 errors.

## @testsubject117

still have 16 errors

## Copilot

Those remaining 16 are almost certainly **duplicate source files being compiled** (same `.vb` included twice), typically because your recovered project sits in a folder layout like `...\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\...` and the SDK-style project’s default include (`**\*.vb`) is picking up both copies.

To fix this precisely, I need the *new* error list—but we can also confirm the duplicate-include cause quickly.

## 1) Paste the remaining errors (first 10 lines)
Please paste the first ~10 of the 16 errors (code + message). The key ones are the “multiple definitions” ones—those tell us which file/class is duplicated.

## 2) Quick check: do you have a nested copy of the project?
In File Explorer, go to:

`C:\Users\capnkirk\source\repos\AMiOFfice\AMiOffice\`

What are the immediate subfolders/files? Specifically, do you see another folder named `AMiOffice` inside it (or something like `AMiOffice (Recovered)`), e.g.:

- `C:\...\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\My Project\...`
- `C:\...\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\Forms\...`

If yes, that’s the culprit.

## 3) The standard fix (exclude the nested folder)
If your structure is `AMiOffice\AMiOffice\...` (nested), add this to your `.vbproj` (near the bottom, before `</Project>`):

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <!-- Exclude nested duplicate project folder from compile globs -->
  <Compile Remove="AMiOffice\**\*.vb" />
  <EmbeddedResource Remove="AMiOffice\**\*" />
  <None Remove="AMiOffice\**\*" />
</ItemGroup>
```

If the nested folder is named something else (like `AMiOffice (Recovered)`), change `AMiOffice\` in those lines to that exact folder name.

### Why we need this
SDK-style projects include `**\*.vb` by default. If your project folder contains *another copy* of the project under a subfolder, you’ll compile both sets → duplicate constructors, duplicate `MyApplication`, etc.

## 4) Also verify you didn’t accidentally add a second `.vbproj` worth of items
In Solution Explorer, do you see files listed twice (two Form1.vb, two Application.Designer.vb, etc.)? If so, tell me which ones.

---

If you paste the **exact remaining 16 errors** (or at least the first 10) and tell me the name of the nested folder (if any), I’ll give you the exact `<Compile Remove="...">` entries so it goes to **0 errors**.

## @testsubject117

Fatal error.
Internal CLR error. (0x80131506)
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.VisualBasic.Syntax.InternalSyntax.DeclarationContext.ProcessStatementTerminator(Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.VisualBasic.Syntax.InternalSyntax.BlockContext)
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.VisualBasic.Syntax.InternalSyntax.Parser.ParseCompilationUnitCore()
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.VisualBasic.Syntax.InternalSyntax.Parser.ParseWithStackGuard[[System.__Canon, System.Private.CoreLib, Version=10.0.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=7cec85d7bea7798e]](System.Func`1<System.__Canon>, System.Func`1<System.__Canon>)
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.VisualBasic.VisualBasicSyntaxTree.ParseText(Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Text.SourceText, Boolean, Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.VisualBasic.VisualBasicParseOptions, System.String, System.Collections.Immutable.ImmutableDictionary`2<System.String,Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.ReportDiagnostic>, System.Threading.CancellationToken)
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.VisualBasic.OptionsValidator.ParseImports(System.Collections.Generic.IEnumerable`1<System.String>, Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.DiagnosticBag)
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.VisualBasic.VisualBasicCommandLineParser.ParseGlobalImports(System.String, System.Collections.Generic.List`1<Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.VisualBasic.GlobalImport>, System.Collections.Generic.List`1<Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Diagnostic>)
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.VisualBasic.VisualBasicCommandLineParser.Parse(System.Collections.Generic.IEnumerable`1<System.String>, System.String, System.String, System.String)
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.CommonCompiler..ctor(Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.CommandLineParser, System.String, System.String[], Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.BuildPaths, System.String, Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.IAnalyzerAssemblyLoader, Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.GeneratorDriverCache, Roslyn.Utilities.ICommonCompilerFileSystem)

## Copilot

That stack trace is Roslyn (the VB compiler) crashing while parsing **global imports** / compilation unit. In practice this almost always comes from **a corrupted/invalid VB source file** (often a designer file), **bad encoding / control characters**, or a **broken `<Import Include="...">` / global imports setting** in the project after the recovery/rename.

### Immediate recovery (gets you building again)
1) Close Visual Studio.
2) Delete these folders (they’re safe to delete):
- `AMiOffice\bin`
- `AMiOffice\obj`
- also delete the hidden `.vs` folder next to your `.sln` (if present)

3) Reopen the solution and build again.

If it still crashes, do the next step (this is the real fix).

---

## Likely root cause in your `.vbproj`: these `<Import Include=...>` lines
You currently have:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Import Include="System.Data" />
  <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
  <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
</ItemGroup>
```

In SDK-style VB projects, global imports are usually controlled by `ImplicitUsings` / defaults; manually forcing these sometimes triggers weird behavior, and given your crash is in `ParseImports`, I’d remove them.

### Fix: remove the whole Import ItemGroup
Delete this entire block from the `.vbproj`:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Import Include="System.Data" />
  <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
  <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
</ItemGroup>
```

Then clean `bin/obj` again and rebuild.

---

## If it STILL crashes: find the bad VB file (fast binary search)
A compiler crash is often a single file with a bad character. Here’s the fastest way:

1) In Solution Explorer, **temporarily unload/exclude** half your `.vb` files (start with `My Project\*.Designer.vb`, `*.Designer.vb`, `*.resx`-paired files).
2) Build.
3) If it builds, the bad file is in the excluded half; if not, it’s in the included half.
4) Repeat (halve again) until you find the one file that triggers the crash.

**Most common culprits:**
- `My Project\Application.Designer.vb`
- `My Project\Resources.Designer.vb`
- any `Form*.Designer.vb`

When you find it, open it in Notepad++ and check:
- Encoding (save as UTF-8)
- Any weird characters at end of lines
- Truncated file / missing `End Class` / etc.

---

## Two questions so I can give the exact next step
1) After you remove that `<Import Include=...>` block, does it still crash?
2) Are you building with Visual Studio, or running `dotnet build` from a terminal? (If you can run `dotnet build`, it sometimes prints which file it was parsing when it died.)

If you paste your current `.vbproj` (or at least the `<ItemGroup>` sections and the top `<PropertyGroup>`), I can tell you exactly what to delete/keep to stop the crash.

## @testsubject117

response to question 2 - immediate sub folders and files are Generated, My Project, Properties folders, amioffice.vbproj, amioffice.vbproj.user, app.config, application events.vb

## Copilot

Good — that folder listing means you **do not** have a nested duplicate source tree (no inner `AMiOffice\` folder), so the “duplicate compile globs” theory is unlikely now.

Given the Roslyn crash stack points at `OptionsValidator.ParseImports` / global imports, and your project still has an `<ItemGroup><Import Include=... /></ItemGroup>`, the most likely cause is exactly that: **invalid Global Imports configuration** (or it’s being combined with something else into an invalid imports string).

## Do this now (safe, and very likely to stop the crash)

### 1) Remove the `<Import Include=...>` ItemGroup entirely
In `AMiOffice.vbproj`, delete this whole block:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Import Include="System.Data" />
  <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
  <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
</ItemGroup>
```

SDK-style VB projects don’t need it; and your `UseWindowsForms=true` already brings in what you need.

### 2) Clean caches
Close Visual Studio, then delete:
- the project’s `bin` folder
- the project’s `obj` folder
- the solution’s `.vs` folder (next to the `.sln`, if present)

Reopen VS and build.

---

## If it still crashes: it’s almost certainly a corrupted “My Project” generated file
Since you have a `My Project` folder, the top suspects are:

- `My Project\Application.Designer.vb`
- `My Project\Resources.Designer.vb`
- `My Project\Settings.Designer.vb`

### Quick test (no guessing)
Temporarily **exclude** `My Project\Application.Designer.vb` from compilation by adding this to the bottom of the `.vbproj`:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Remove="My Project\Application.Designer.vb" />
</ItemGroup>
```

Build again:
- If the crash stops, that file is corrupted / duplicated.
- If it still crashes, we’ll try the next designer file.

(You can do the same test for `Resources.Designer.vb` and `Settings.Designer.vb`.)

---

## One more thing: confirm you disabled AssemblyInfo generation
You previously changed `GenerateAssemblyInfo`. Make sure it is exactly:

```xml
<GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
```

If it’s still `true` *and* you have `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb`, you’ll keep getting attribute duplication (though that shouldn’t cause a compiler crash by itself).

---

### What I need from you to finish this in one shot
After you remove the `<Import Include=...>` block and clean, tell me:

1) Does the build still crash (same “Internal CLR error”)?
2) If it doesn’t crash, what are the remaining errors (if any)?

If it still crashes, paste your current `AMiOffice.vbproj` (entire file) and I’ll point out any remaining import/global-import related settings that could be poisoning the compiler.

## @testsubject117

I rebuilt and now have 14 errors

## Copilot

Great—compiler crash is gone. Now we’re back to normal VB compile errors, and the remaining 14 will be straightforward **once we see what they are**.

Please paste the **14 errors exactly** (copy from Error List: *Code + Description + File*). The “File” column is important because it tells us whether this is still duplication (same file twice) or just bad namespaces/startup object.

While you grab that, here are the two most likely fixes given what we changed:

## 1) If errors are still “attribute cannot be applied multiple times”
That means you still have both:
- SDK-generated attributes, and
- `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb`

Fix (choose one):
- keep `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb` → ensure `.vbproj` has:
  ```xml
  <GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
  ```
- or delete/exclude `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb` and set GenerateAssemblyInfo back to true.

## 2) If errors are “multiple definitions” (Sub New / OnCreateMainForm / OnInitialize)
That means `My Project\Application.Designer.vb` (or another designer file) is being compiled twice.

Fast check in Solution Explorer:
- do you see **two** `Application.Designer.vb` entries or two of the same form?

If yes, we’ll add one exclusion in the `.vbproj`.

---

### Quick command that helps (if you can run a terminal)
From the project folder, run:

```bat
dotnet build -v:minimal
```

and paste the first error block(s). It usually includes file paths that make duplication obvious.

Send the 14 error lines and I’ll respond with the exact `.vbproj` edits / file exclusions to get to **0 errors**.

## Copilot

The error list shows the exact problem: your project is compiling files from **outside the project folder**:

- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\My Project\Application.Designer.vb`  (correct)
- **and also** `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\My Project\Application.Designer.vb` (wrong, external)

Same for `obj\...`:
- it’s also picking up `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\obj\Debug\...` (wrong, external)

So your SDK-style project’s default include (`**\*.vb`) is accidentally reaching *up* to `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\...` and grabbing a stray `My Project` and `obj` directory that exist at the repo root.

## Fix: tell MSBuild to only compile files under the project directory
Add these two properties to your main `<PropertyGroup>` in `AMiOffice(recovered).vbproj`:

```xml
<EnableDefaultItems>false</EnableDefaultItems>
<EnableDefaultCompileItems>false</EnableDefaultCompileItems>
```

Then explicitly include your project’s VB files.

### Easiest explicit include (everything under the project folder, excluding bin/obj)
Add this **ItemGroup**:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Include="**\*.vb" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
  <EmbeddedResource Include="**\*.resx" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
  <None Include="**\*" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**;**\*.vb;**\*.resx" />
</ItemGroup>
```

That forces the scope to the project folder only, and will stop pulling in:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\My Project\...`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\obj\...`

## Also fix the duplicate assembly attributes
Once the external `obj` is no longer included, most of the attribute duplication should disappear. But also do this (recommended):

In the same `<PropertyGroup>` ensure:

```xml
<GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
```

(You likely have `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb` already.)

---

## Updated vbproj snippet to paste
In your first `<PropertyGroup>`, make sure you have these:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  ...
  <GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>

  <!-- CRITICAL: stop SDK default globs from pulling in files outside this project folder -->
  <EnableDefaultItems>false</EnableDefaultItems>
  <EnableDefaultCompileItems>false</EnableDefaultCompileItems>
  ...
</PropertyGroup>
```

And somewhere after that (after your existing ItemGroups is fine), add:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Include="**\*.vb" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
  <EmbeddedResource Include="**\*.resx" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
  <None Include="**\*" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**;**\*.vb;**\*.resx" />
</ItemGroup>
```

## Cleanup + rebuild
1) Close VS
2) Delete:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\bin`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\obj`
3) Reopen and rebuild

---

### One important question
Why do you have `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\My Project\...` at the repo root? That’s not standard. If that folder was created accidentally, you can also delete/rename it—but the project-file fix above will make your build robust even if it exists.

If you want, paste your current `AMiOffice(recovered).vbproj` and I’ll return a complete corrected file with these changes merged in (so you can paste it once).

## Copilot

Those same 14 errors mean the project is **still compiling** the stray files at:

- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\My Project\Application.Designer.vb`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\obj\Debug\...`

So the “disable default items + explicitly include only project-local files” change either **didn’t get applied to the right .vbproj**, or VS is still building the other/original project file.

Let’s lock this down.

## 1) Confirm you’re editing the right file
In Solution Explorer, right‑click the project **AMiOffice(recovered)** → **Unload Project**  
Then right‑click it again → **Edit AMiOffice(recovered).vbproj**

Make the edits *there* (not in the old/original one), save, then **Reload Project**.

## 2) Paste this exact block into the FIRST `<PropertyGroup>`
Add these three lines (or ensure they exist exactly like this):

```xml
<GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
<EnableDefaultItems>false</EnableDefaultItems>
<EnableDefaultCompileItems>false</EnableDefaultCompileItems>
```

## 3) Add this ItemGroup near the bottom (before `</Project>`)
```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <!-- Only include files under THIS project folder -->
  <Compile Include="**\*.vb" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
  <EmbeddedResource Include="**\*.resx" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
  <None Include="**\*" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**;**\*.vb;**\*.resx" />
</ItemGroup>
```

## 4) Clean and rebuild
Close VS and delete **only these** (the ones under the project):
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\bin`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\obj`

Reopen VS → Rebuild.

---

## If you still get the same 14 errors after that
Then the build is *definitely* still including sources outside the project directory, which means one of these is true:

- Your `.vbproj` actually lives at a higher folder than you think (so `**\*.vb` includes `..\My Project\...`), or
- There is a `Directory.Build.props` or `Directory.Build.targets` in `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\` adding includes, or
- The project is being built from a different path than the one you edited.

### One-step diagnosis
Please paste:
1) the **full path** of the `.vbproj` you edited (shown at the top of the “Edit .vbproj” tab in VS), and
2) the **top `<PropertyGroup>`** from that `.vbproj` after your edits.

With that, I can tell you exactly why those external paths are still getting included and give you the exact `Remove` patterns to stop it.

### Emergency workaround (guaranteed)
Rename the stray folders so they can’t be picked up:
- Rename `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\My Project` → `My Project - stray`
- Rename `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\obj` → `obj-stray`

If the 14 errors disappear immediately, it confirms the diagnosis 100%.
