## Copilot

Your `.vbproj` is **cut off** in what you pasted (it ends right after `<!-- Write back -->`). If the file on disk is actually truncated like that, it will cause all kinds of weirdness—including Visual Studio falling back to other includes / loading stale state. First make sure the file on disk ends with proper closing tags.

But even before that: I can already see the *main reason you still get the same duplicate-definition errors*:

### You are explicitly including **all** `**\*.vb`…
```xml
<Compile Include="**\*.vb" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
```

…and you also still have this **global imports** block back in the project:
```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Import Include="System.Data" />
  <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
  <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
</ItemGroup>
```

You previously removed it to stop the compiler crash—putting it back can reintroduce problems. Also, the `**\*.vb` include will include any VB file under the project folder (good), but your error list shows it is still pulling in files outside the folder. That typically happens when the project directory is not what you think *or* when MSBuild is still using default items (or Directory.Build.* is injecting items).

Let’s fix this in a way that **guarantees** those external paths can’t be compiled, even if some higher-level props/targets add them.

## Fix plan (do exactly this)

### A) Remove the `Import Include=...` ItemGroup again
Delete this block completely:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Import Include="System.Data" />
  <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
  <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
</ItemGroup>
```

### B) Add hard “Remove” rules to prevent compiling the rogue folders
Add this near the top (after your explicit `<Compile Include="**\*.vb"...>` ItemGroup is fine):

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <!-- Hard-stop: never compile anything from repo-root stray folders -->
  <Compile Remove="..\My Project\**\*.vb" />
  <Compile Remove="..\obj\**\*.vb" />
</ItemGroup>
```

This works even if something else tries to include them later.

### C) Ensure your project file is not truncated
At the very end of your `.vbproj`, you must have:

- the missing `<WriteLinesToFile .../>` “write back” task
- `</Target>` for IncrementBuildCounter
- the GenerateBuildInfo target
- final `</Project>`

Since your paste got cut off, I’m going to give you a complete, known-good `.vbproj` that includes:
- your explicit compile includes
- the hard “Remove” rules
- NO `<Import Include=...>` block
- Build counter + BuildInfo generation
- proper closing tags

## Paste this entire file as `AMiOffice.vbproj`
(Replace everything in the file with this.)

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <!-- CRITICAL: stop SDK default globs -->
    <EnableDefaultItems>false</EnableDefaultItems>
    <EnableDefaultCompileItems>false</EnableDefaultCompileItems>

    <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>
    <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
    <MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>

    <AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
    <FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>

    <!-- IMPORTANT: avoids duplicate assembly attributes if you have My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb -->
    <GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
    <InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>

    <!-- Put generated file outside obj to avoid double-includes -->
    <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\Generated\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>

    <Copyright>Copyright Kirk Saffell 2026</Copyright>
    <Title>AMiOfficeApplication</Title>
    <ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Explicitly include only project-folder files -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Include="**\*.vb" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
    <EmbeddedResource Include="**\*.resx" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
    <None Include="**\*" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**;**\*.vb;**\*.resx" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <!-- Hard-stop: NEVER compile stray repo-root folders (these are causing your duplicates) -->
    <Compile Remove="..\My Project\**\*.vb" />
    <Compile Remove="..\obj\**\*.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Remove="ESC Logo.jpg" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Include="ESC Logo.jpg">
      <CopyToOutputDirectory>Never</CopyToOutputDirectory>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Update="My Project\Application.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Application.myapp</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Resources.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Resources.resx</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Settings.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTimeSharedInput>True</DesignTimeSharedInput>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Settings.settings</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Update="My Project\Resources.resx">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My.Resources</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>VbMyResourcesResXFileCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Resources.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Update="My Project\Application.myapp">
      <Generator>MyApplicationCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Application.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
    <None Update="My Project\Settings.settings">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>SettingsSingleFileGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Settings.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Prevent compiling stray BuildInfo files -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
    <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Increment build number ONLY when publishing -->
  <Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <ReadLinesFromFile File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="$(BuildNumber)" />
  </Target>

  <!-- Generate BuildInfo.g.vb for builds and publishes -->
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <ReadLinesFromFile Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''" File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines2" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''">
      <BuildNumber>%(BuildCounterLines2.Identity)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <MakeDir Directories="$([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName('$(BuildInfoFile)'))" />

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="Option Strict On&#xD;&#xA;Option Explicit On&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;Friend Module BuildInfo&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const ProductVersion As String = &quot;$(ProductVersion)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const BuildNumber As String = &quot;$(BuildNumber)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend ReadOnly Property DisplayVersion As String&#xD;&#xA;        Get&#xD;&#xA;            Return &quot;AMiOffice v&quot; &amp; ProductVersion &amp; &quot; (Build &quot; &amp; BuildNumber &amp; &quot;)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;        End Get&#xD;&#xA;    End Property&#xD;&#xA;End Module&#xD;&#xA;" />

    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>
```

## After pasting
1) Close VS
2) Delete `bin` and `obj` under `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\`
3) Reopen VS → Rebuild

### If you STILL see those same paths in errors
Then something outside the project is injecting compile items (usually `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\Directory.Build.props`).

If that happens, tell me whether you have either of these files:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\Directory.Build.props`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\Directory.Build.targets`

and paste their contents (they’re usually short).

```xml name=AMiOffice.vbproj
    <None Update="My Project\Application.myapp">
      <Generator>MyApplicationCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Application.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>

    <None Update="My Project\Settings.settings">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>SettingsSingleFileGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Settings.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Prevent compiling any stray BuildInfo file from the project root -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
    <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Increment build number ONLY when publishing -->
  <Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Ensure the file exists -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <!-- Read current number -->
    <ReadLinesFromFile File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <!-- Increment -->
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Write back -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="$(BuildNumber)" />
  </Target>

  <!-- Generate BuildInfo.g.vb for BOTH normal builds and publishes -->
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Ensure the file exists -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <!-- If BuildNumber wasn't set by Publish, just read the current counter -->
    <ReadLinesFromFile Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''" File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines2" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''">
      <BuildNumber>%(BuildCounterLines2.Identity)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <MakeDir Directories="$([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName('$(BuildInfoFile)'))" />

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="Option Strict On&#xD;&#xA;Option Explicit On&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;Friend Module BuildInfo&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const ProductVersion As String = &quot;$(ProductVersion)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const BuildNumber As String = &quot;$(BuildNumber)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend ReadOnly Property DisplayVersion As String&#xD;&#xA;        Get&#xD;&#xA;            Return &quot;AMiOffice v&quot; &amp; ProductVersion &amp; &quot; (Build &quot; &amp; BuildNumber &amp; &quot;)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;        End Get&#xD;&#xA;    End Property&#xD;&#xA;End Module&#xD;&#xA;" />

    <ItemGroup>
      <!-- Include generated file exactly once -->
      <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>
```

## Copilot

Those error paths prove MSBuild is **still compiling VB files from the repo root**:

- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\My Project\Application.Designer.vb`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\obj\Debug\...`

That means something *above your project* is adding compile items (most commonly `Directory.Build.props` / `Directory.Build.targets` in `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\`), and it’s overriding your project settings.

### Fastest guaranteed fix (no MSBuild spelunking)
Rename or delete the stray folders at the repo root so they can’t be picked up:

1) Close Visual Studio
2) In File Explorer:
- Rename `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\My Project` → `My Project - stray` (or delete it)
- Rename `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\obj` → `obj - stray` (or delete it)

3) Reopen VS → Rebuild

Those two folders **should not exist at repo root** (only under your project). Removing them will immediately eliminate:
- the duplicate `Application.Designer.vb` compile
- the duplicate generated assembly attribute files

### The “proper” MSBuild fix (if you want it robust even if those folders exist)
Add these two properties to your `<PropertyGroup>` (in addition to what you already have):

```xml
<BaseIntermediateOutputPath>obj\</BaseIntermediateOutputPath>
<IntermediateOutputPath>obj\$(Configuration)\</IntermediateOutputPath>
```

And add a hard exclusion that also catches items introduced later:

```xml
<Target Name="RemoveRogueRepoRootCompiles" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="..\My Project\**\*.vb" />
    <Compile Remove="..\obj\**\*.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Target>
```

Why a Target? Because if `Directory.Build.targets` (higher up) adds `Compile Include=...` after your ItemGroups, this will still remove them right before compile.

### What I recommend
Do the rename/delete first (2 minutes). It’s the cleanest because those folders are wrong to have there anyway.

### One question
Do you have either of these files at `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\`?
- `Directory.Build.props`
- `Directory.Build.targets`

If yes, paste them here—because that’s almost certainly where those repo-root `Compile Include` items are being injected.

## Copilot

You effectively have **two different projects**:

1) `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice.vbproj`  (repo root)
2) `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj` (the real project folder)

And your errors are coming from the *root* project pulling in:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\My Project\...`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\obj\...`

That’s why the same 14 errors persist no matter what you do inside the inner project—Visual Studio is still building/loading the **wrong** `.vbproj` (or both).

## The clean fix
### 1) Stop using the repo-root project
Do this:

1. Close Visual Studio.
2. In File Explorer, rename the repo-root project file so VS can’t pick it up:
   - Rename `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice.vbproj`
   - to `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice.vbproj.bak`

(Or delete it if you’re sure you don’t need it.)

### 2) Open the correct project/solution
- If you have a `.sln`, open the `.sln` that lives alongside the **inner** project (`...\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\`).
- If you don’t have a `.sln`, create/open one that references only:
  - `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj`

### 3) Delete wrong repo-root build artifacts (optional but recommended)
Delete these if they exist:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\obj`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\bin`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\My Project`

### 4) Rebuild
Open VS and rebuild. The duplicate `Application.Designer.vb` should be gone because the correct project will only see:
`...\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\My Project\...`

## Why this works
Your duplicate-definition errors show **two Application.Designer.vb paths**, one inside the real project and one at repo root. That only happens if the repo-root project is being built (or being included) and it’s globbing everything under `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\`.

---

## If you want: I can also give you the “inner” vbproj to use
Before I do: confirm which one you want to keep as the real project:
- the inner one: `...\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj` (recommended)
- or the repo-root one: `...\AMiOffice.vbproj`

If you reply “keep inner”, I’ll tell you the exact steps to update the `.sln` (remove the wrong project, add the right one) and what to delete so VS stops ever referencing the root project again.

## Copilot

These errors mean your forms are no longer being compiled as **WinForms types** (so `Label`, `Panel`, `Timer`, and events like `SizeChanged`/`Tick` aren’t in scope). This happens when you disabled the SDK’s default items and *didn’t bring back the VB global imports / WinForms references the way VB expects*.

### Fix: turn VB implicit/global imports back on the *right* way (not via `<Import Include=...>`)
In SDK-style **VB** projects, WinForms designer code typically relies on default VB imports (like `System.Windows.Forms`) being available globally.

Do this in your **inner** `AMiOffice.vbproj`:

## 1) Remove the explicit `EnableDefaultItems/EnableDefaultCompileItems` workaround (now that you’re using the correct project)
Set these back to default by deleting them (or setting them to true):

- Remove:
```xml
<EnableDefaultItems>false</EnableDefaultItems>
<EnableDefaultCompileItems>false</EnableDefaultCompileItems>
```

Also remove the manual “include everything” ItemGroup:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Include="**\*.vb" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
  <EmbeddedResource Include="**\*.resx" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
  <None Include="**\*" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**;**\*.vb;**\*.resx" />
</ItemGroup>
```

Because the SDK already does this correctly and also sets up the right behavior for WinForms designer.

## 2) Ensure these WinForms properties exist (you already have them, but confirm)
```xml
<UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
<MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>
```

## 3) Add VB’s implicit imports explicitly (safe + fixes your current errors)
Add this to your main `<PropertyGroup>`:

```xml
<VbMyType>WindowsForms</VbMyType>
<ImplicitUsings>true</ImplicitUsings>
```

(If `VbMyType` isn’t recognized in your SDK version, it won’t hurt; `MyType` is the main one.)

## 4) If you still need protection from the repo-root stray folders
Instead of disabling default items, keep default items ON and just exclude those rogue paths explicitly:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Remove="..\My Project\**\*.vb" />
  <EmbeddedResource Remove="..\My Project\**\*" />
  <None Remove="..\My Project\**\*" />

  <Compile Remove="..\obj\**\*.vb" />
  <EmbeddedResource Remove="..\obj\**\*" />
  <None Remove="..\obj\**\*" />
</ItemGroup>
```

But if you deleted/renamed those stray folders already, you can skip this.

---

## Minimal “do this now” steps
1) Edit inner `AMiOffice.vbproj`
2) **Delete** the two properties that disable default items and delete the big manual include ItemGroup.
3) Keep `<UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>` and `<MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>`
4) Clean + rebuild:
   - delete `AMiOffice\AMiOffice\bin` and `AMiOffice\AMiOffice\obj`
   - rebuild

That should make `Label`, `Panel`, `Timer`, `SizeChanged`, and `Tick` resolve again.

---

### If you want, paste your current inner `AMiOffice.vbproj` (entire file)
…and I’ll return a single corrected final version that:
- uses default SDK items (WinForms designer-friendly)
- keeps your BuildInfo/build counter targets
- avoids the repo-root stray inclusion problem without breaking WinForms types.

I think that after the blank page issue I somehow got created a 2nd project that we were working on, but now the 2 are getting mixed up

## Copilot

Yes—what you’re seeing is exactly what happens when VS has *two* similar projects/solutions and/or the repo-root “stray” folders (`My Project`, `obj`) are being picked up by the wrong `.vbproj`. The good news: your latest 17 errors are **not mixed-up code** anymore; they’re from the *inner* project being forced into a “manual include” mode that broke VB’s normal WinForms imports.

Here’s the clean “unmix” procedure.

## 1) Pick the one true project and solution
You said we’re keeping the inner project:

- **Keep:** `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj`
- **Ignore:** anything at `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice.vbproj.bak` (already renamed—good)

Now ensure you open the correct thing:
- Close VS
- Open **ONLY** the solution that lives under `...\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\` (if it exists), or open the inner `.vbproj` directly.

## 2) Remove the “manual include / disable defaults” changes (they break WinForms types)
Those `Label/Panel/Timer not defined` errors are because the project is no longer using VB’s normal WinForms compile defaults.

Edit **inner** `AMiOffice.vbproj` and do this:

### Delete these lines entirely (or set them back to true)
```xml
<EnableDefaultItems>false</EnableDefaultItems>
<EnableDefaultCompileItems>false</EnableDefaultCompileItems>
```

### Delete this whole ItemGroup (the manual include)
```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Include="**\*.vb" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
  <EmbeddedResource Include="**\*.resx" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
  <None Include="**\*" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**;**\*.vb;**\*.resx" />
</ItemGroup>
```

Leave these in place (they’re correct):
```xml
<UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
<MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>
```

## 3) Delete the stray repo-root folders (so nothing can “mix” again)
In File Explorer, at **repo root** (`C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\`), delete/rename if present:

- `My Project`  (this should NOT be here)
- `obj` (this should NOT be here)
- `bin` (this should NOT be here)

It’s fine if `obj/bin` exist under the inner project folder—only the repo-root ones are bad.

## 4) Clean inner project build outputs + VS cache
Delete these folders:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\obj`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\bin`
- the `.vs` folder next to the `.sln` you’re opening (if present)

Then reopen VS and **Rebuild**.

## 5) If you still see *any* “duplicate Application.Designer.vb” errors
That means VS is still building something from repo-root. At that point:
- check **Solution Explorer** for a second project
- check **Build → Configuration Manager** to ensure only the inner project builds

---

### One quick question to finish this cleanly
Do you have a solution file at:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.sln` (or any `.sln` under that folder)?

If yes, tell me its exact name and I’ll give you the exact “remove wrong project / add right project” clicks so the solution can’t ever point at the stray project again.

## Copilot

You’ve got multiple solutions pointing at different projects—so yes, they’re getting mixed. The fastest way to stop this permanently is to **choose one solution to keep**, make it reference **only the inner project**, and archive the rest.

## Do this (recommended, minimal confusion)

### 1) Decide the “one true” solution
Keep **one** of these as the only one you open from now on (I recommend `AMiOffice.sln` under the inner folder if it exists, otherwise create a new one):

- Preferred: `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.sln` (if it exists)
- Otherwise: use the repo-root `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice(recovered).sln` but we must fix it to point to the inner `.vbproj`.

### 2) Archive the extras (so you can’t accidentally open them)
Create a folder:
`C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\_solution-archive\`

Move these into it (don’t delete, just move):
- `AMiOffice.slnx` (this is solution filter / alternate solution metadata—easy to open by accident)
- `AMiOffice(recovered).sln` (after we fix or if you decide not to keep it)
- `AMiOffie BU of Solution 04-16-2026` (backup folder—keep archived)
- any other old `.sln` files you don’t intend to open

The goal: when you double-click, there’s only one obvious choice.

## Now fix the solution you are keeping

### Option A (best): create a fresh solution that references only the inner project
1) Close Visual Studio
2) Open a Developer Command Prompt (or regular terminal) and run:

```bat
cd C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice
dotnet new sln -n AMiOffice
dotnet sln AMiOffice.sln add AMiOffice.vbproj
```

3) Double-click `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.sln`

`...\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj`

`dotnet new sln` is refusing because **`AMiOffice.slnx` already exists** and the template wants to create a solution file but (in your SDK) it’s trying to use/overwrite that same name. So you never actually created a `.sln`, hence “Could not find solution”.


### 3) Add the inner project to the solution

```bat
dotnet sln AMiOffice.sln add AMiOffice.vbproj
```

```bat
dir *.sln *.slnx
```

### 5) Open the solution (not the filter)

`C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.sln`

## Copilot

Visual Basic/Visual Studio 2026 does **not** replace `.sln` with `.slnx`. What’s happening is specifically your **.NET SDK / `dotnet new sln` template**: in your environment it’s producing a **solution filter** (`.slnx`) instead of a classic solution (`.sln`). That’s why VS opens it as “no project” unless it contains project entries.

So we’ll avoid `dotnet new sln` entirely and create a classic `.sln` in a way that always works.

## Guaranteed fix: create a classic `.sln` using Visual Studio

1) Open Visual Studio.
2) **File → New → Project**
3) Search for **“Blank Solution”**
4) Name it: `AMiOffice`
5) Location: `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice`
6) Create.

Now you will have:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.sln`

7) In Solution Explorer:
   - Right-click the Solution → **Add → Existing Project…**
   - Choose: `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj`
8) Save, close, reopen the `.sln`, build.

## If you only see `.slnx` after doing this
Then you didn’t create a Blank Solution; you created a filter or opened the wrong file. A **Blank Solution** in VS creates a `.sln`.

- `<EnableDefaultCompileItems>false</EnableDefaultCompileItems>`
- the manual ItemGroup that includes `**\*.vb`

C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.slnx

## Copilot

You’re still opening a **solution filter** (`AMiOffice.slnx`). That’s the root of the “projects getting mixed / blank / missing projects” behavior.

A `.slnx` is *not* the authoritative solution; it just points at a solution and selects a subset of projects. If its target solution or project paths changed (you renamed the root `.vbproj` to `.bak`, moved folders, etc.), the `.slnx` can end up empty or pointing at the wrong project.

## Do this now

### 1) Find the real `.sln` that the `.slnx` is filtering
Open the `.slnx` in Notepad (or any text editor). It’s tiny. Copy/paste its contents here.

It will contain a reference to a `.sln` (or some path info). Once we see that, we’ll open the **actual `.sln`** and fix it.

### 2) After we locate the `.sln`, fix it to reference only the inner project
In that `.sln`:
- Remove any project that points to the old repo-root `.vbproj` (now `.bak`)
- Add only:
  `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj`

### 3) Stop using the `.slnx` until the `.sln` is correct
Once the `.sln` builds clean, we can regenerate a fresh `.slnx` filter if you still want one.

---

## Why this is important
Your earlier errors literally referenced files under repo root (`C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\My Project\...` and `...\repos\obj\...`). That only happens when the loaded solution/project is rooted too high or includes the wrong project. The `.slnx` is the easiest way for that to keep happening.

## @testsubject117

you keep saying that it's a solution filter, it's not. Read what google says about .slnx: In Visual Studio 2026, .slnx is the new, XML-based default solution file format (.slnx replaces .sln) designed for modern .NET projects, offering a human-readable, compact structure that simplifies manual editing, reduces Git merge conflicts, and removes the need for cryptic GUIDs. It acts as a lightweight container for your project structure.

## Copilot

You’re right to push back: in **Visual Studio 2026 (18.5)** Microsoft introduced **`.slnx` as an XML-based solution format**. So in *your* environment, `.slnx` can be the *primary* solution file, not a “filter”.

What still matters for your problem is this: **your `.slnx` is currently not consistently pointing at just the inner project**, so VS ends up building/including the wrong things (that’s how you got errors referencing `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\My Project\...` and `...\repos\obj\...`).

### What we need to do next
We should inspect the actual `.slnx` contents and make sure it references **only**:
`C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj`

## Step 1: Paste your `.slnx` contents
Open this file in Notepad and paste it here:

`C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.slnx`

It’s usually small XML.

## Step 2: What I’ll do with it
Once I see it, I’ll tell you exactly what to edit so that:
- the solution contains only the inner project
- no reference remains to the old repo-root project (the `.vbproj.bak`)
- build stops pulling in `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\My Project\...` or `...\repos\obj\...`

## Step 3 (immediately after): revert the inner `.vbproj` to normal WinForms defaults
Your current “Label/Panel/Timer not defined” errors are a separate (but related) issue: they’re caused by the inner `.vbproj` being in the “manual include / disabled default items” mode we used earlier to work around the wrong project. Once the solution is correct, we can safely undo that and WinForms types/events will resolve again.

## @testsubject117

<Solution>
  <Project Path="AMiOffice/AMiOffice.vbproj" />
</Solution>

## Copilot

That `.slnx` is clean and it points to the **inner** project:

```xml
<Project Path="AMiOffice/AMiOffice.vbproj" />
```

So the “mixing” is no longer coming from the solution file. Your remaining issue is the **project file settings** we changed earlier: you currently have the inner `.vbproj` in a state that breaks WinForms compilation, which is why you got:

- `Type 'Label' is not defined`
- `Type 'Panel' is not defined`
- events like `SizeChanged` / `Tick` not found

That happens when VB WinForms defaults/imports aren’t being applied (often due to disabling default items and manually including files, plus other project metadata getting out of sync).

## Fix (do this in the inner project): revert to normal SDK defaults

Edit:
`C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj`

### 1) Remove these properties entirely
```xml
<EnableDefaultItems>false</EnableDefaultItems>
<EnableDefaultCompileItems>false</EnableDefaultCompileItems>
```

### 2) Remove the “manual include everything” ItemGroup entirely
Remove this whole block:
```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Include="**\*.vb" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
  <EmbeddedResource Include="**\*.resx" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
  <None Include="**\*" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**;**\*.vb;**\*.resx" />
</ItemGroup>
```

### 3) Ensure these are present (and true)
In your main `<PropertyGroup>`:
```xml
<UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
<MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>
```

### 4) Ensure you are NOT using the old VB `<Import Include=...>` ItemGroup
If you have something like this, delete it:
```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
  ...
</ItemGroup>
```

## Clean rebuild (important)
1) Close Visual Studio.
2) Delete:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\obj`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\bin`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\obj`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\bin`

3) Reopen VS by opening `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.slnx`
4) Build → Rebuild

## If duplicates come back after reverting defaults
Then you still have stray repo-root folders:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\My Project`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\obj`

Those must be deleted/renamed, because with default globs enabled, MSBuild will happily compile them if they are under the project root being globbed. (This usually only happens if the project is rooted too high, but after your `.slnx` it shouldn’t—still, check they’re gone.)

Error (active)	BC30590	Event 'Tick' cannot be found.	AMiOffice	C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\FormMainMenu.vb	50		


## Copilot

Those 17 errors mean the compiler is treating your forms as if **Windows Forms isn’t referenced at all**. When WinForms is correctly enabled, `Label`, `Panel`, `Timer`, `SizeChanged`, `Tick`, etc. are all known.

So we need to confirm/fix **one of these** (in order of likelihood):

1) The project is not actually targeting **Windows** (or WinForms isn’t enabled in the *effective* project properties)
2) `UseWindowsForms` is not being applied (wrong `<PropertyGroup>` condition, overridden by `Directory.Build.props`, etc.)
3) Your designer files lost their `Inherits System.Windows.Forms.Form` or the project’s **RootNamespace** / imports are broken

Let’s do the shortest path to certainty.

## 1) Verify the project file has the required properties (exactly)
Open `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj` and make sure this exact minimal set exists in an **unconditional** `<PropertyGroup>` (no `Condition=` on the group):

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
  <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
  <MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>
</PropertyGroup>
```

Also add this (explicitly) because VB can be picky about default imports in some SDK-style setups:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
</PropertyGroup>
```

Save.

## 2) Force a hard WinForms reference (diagnostic + usually fixes it)
Add this **temporarily** to the `.vbproj`:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Reference Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
</ItemGroup>
```

If that makes the 17 errors disappear, it proves the WinForms framework reference wasn’t being applied.

(We can remove this later once the underlying property issue is fixed.)

## 3) Check your Form designer file inheritance (30 seconds)
Open `FormAbout.Designer.vb` and find the class declaration line. It should look like:

```vb
Partial Class FormAbout
    Inherits System.Windows.Forms.Form
```

If it does **not** inherit `Form`, then `SizeChanged` won’t exist and controls can get weird. If it’s missing, tell me what it says.

## 4) If steps 1–2 don’t fix it: you have an override file at repo root
This is common in “recovered” projects: a higher-level props file overrides settings.

Check if either exists and paste contents:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\Directory.Build.props`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\Directory.Build.targets`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\Directory.Build.props`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\Directory.Build.targets`

These can silently disable WinForms or change target framework behavior.

---

## Do this now (minimal)
1) Add the temporary reference:
```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Reference Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
</ItemGroup>
```
2) Rebuild.
3) Tell me: did the 17 errors go away?

If yes: we’ll then remove that reference and fix the real cause (usually a `Directory.Build.props` override or missing `-windows` target).

    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
    <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />

  <!-- Increment build number ONLY when publishing -->
  <Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Ensure the file exists -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <!-- Read current number -->
    <ReadLinesFromFile File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <!-- Increment -->
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Write back -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="$(BuildNumber)" />
  </Target>

  <!-- Generate BuildInfo.g.vb for BOTH normal builds and publishes -->
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Ensure the file exists -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <!-- If BuildNumber wasn't set by Publish, just read the current counter -->
    <ReadLinesFromFile Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''" File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines2" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''">
      <BuildNumber>%(BuildCounterLines2.Identity)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <MakeDir Directories="$([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName('$(BuildInfoFile)'))" />

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="Option Strict On&#xD;&#xA;Option Explicit On&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;Friend Module BuildInfo&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const ProductVersion As String = &quot;$(ProductVersion)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const BuildNumber As String = &quot;$(BuildNumber)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend ReadOnly Property DisplayVersion As String&#xD;&#xA;        Get&#xD;&#xA;            Return &quot;AMiOffice v&quot; &amp; ProductVersion &amp; &quot; (Build &quot; &amp; BuildNumber &amp; &quot;)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;        End Get&#xD;&#xA;    End Property&#xD;&#xA;End Module&#xD;&#xA;" />

    <ItemGroup>
      <!-- Include generated file exactly once -->
      <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>

## Copilot

Your `.vbproj` is missing the *single most important thing* for VB WinForms here: it’s **not using the VB SDK**. You currently have:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
```

For **VB** Windows Forms projects, this should be:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk.WindowsDesktop">
```

Without the WindowsDesktop SDK, you can still sometimes compile, but the WinForms surface area and references (and designer expectations) often won’t be wired up correctly—leading exactly to `Label` / `Panel` / `Timer` “not defined”.

## Fix: change the SDK line
Change the first line to:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk.WindowsDesktop">
```

Nothing else needs to change to prove the point.

### Then do a clean rebuild
1) Close Visual Studio
2) Delete:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\bin`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\obj`
3) Reopen VS (your `.slnx`)
4) Rebuild

## If you still see any “Label not defined” after that
Add this property (usually not required, but helps VB import behavior in SDK-style projects):

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
</PropertyGroup>
```

## Here is the corrected `.vbproj` (only the top line changed + ImplicitUsings added)
```xml name=AMiOffice.vbproj

      <CustomToolNamespace>My.Resources</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>VbMyResourcesResXFileCodeGenerator</Generator>

  </ItemGroup>

  </ItemGroup>

  <Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish">

    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"

      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <ReadLinesFromFile File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="$(BuildNumber)" />
  </Target>

  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
```

### After you rebuild

## @testsubject117

Error NETSDK1137 It is no longer necessary to use the Microsoft.NET.Sdk.WindowsDesktop SDK. Consider changing the Sdk attribute of the root Project element to 'Microsoft.NET.Sdk'.

## Copilot

Got it—on **.NET 10 / VS 2026**, WinForms is supported with plain `Microsoft.NET.Sdk`, and the `WindowsDesktop` SDK is deprecated, hence `NETSDK1137`. So revert that SDK line back to `Microsoft.NET.Sdk`.

Your WinForms types still not resolving means: **the WinForms reference is not being added at build time**, despite `<UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>`. The most common reason in a “recovered” repo is a higher-level MSBuild file overriding things (e.g., `Directory.Build.props/targets`) or the project is being evaluated with a non-Windows target somehow.

Let’s do two deterministic fixes:

## 1) Add an explicit framework reference to Windows Forms (this is the real missing piece)
In your `.vbproj`, keep:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
```

Add this **ItemGroup** (near the top, after the main PropertyGroup is fine):

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <FrameworkReference Include="Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.WindowsForms" />
</ItemGroup>
```

This forces the WinForms framework reference even if something is overriding the normal behavior of `UseWindowsForms`.

## 2) Add explicit Imports for designer compilation (belt-and-suspenders)
Also add:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Import Include="System" />
  <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
  <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
</ItemGroup>
```

(These are VB “global imports” controlled by MSBuild; they’re different from `Imports` statements in code.)

> You previously mentioned a “blank page issue” tied to Imports; that crash was likely from the earlier duplicate-compilation mess. With the solution now clean, these global imports are safe and commonly used to stabilize recovered VB projects.

## Here is your “completely fixed” `.vbproj` (full file)
Replace your current file with this:

```xml name=AMiOffice.vbproj
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>

    <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
    <MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>

    <AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
    <FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>

    <GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
    <InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>

    <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\Generated\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>

    <Copyright>Copyright Kirk Saffell 2026</Copyright>
    <Title>AMiOfficeApplication</Title>
    <ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Force WinForms framework reference (fixes 'Label/Panel/Timer not defined') -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <FrameworkReference Include="Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.WindowsForms" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- VB global imports to make designer code compile reliably -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Import Include="System" />
    <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
    <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Hard-stop: never compile stray repo-root folders -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="..\My Project\**\*.vb" />
    <Compile Remove="..\obj\**\*.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Remove="ESC Logo.jpg" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Include="ESC Logo.jpg">
      <CopyToOutputDirectory>Never</CopyToOutputDirectory>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Update="My Project\Application.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Application.myapp</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Resources.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Resources.resx</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Settings.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTimeSharedInput>True</DesignTimeSharedInput>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Settings.settings</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Update="My Project\Resources.resx">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My.Resources</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>VbMyResourcesResXFileCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Resources.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Update="My Project\Application.myapp">
      <Generator>MyApplicationCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Application.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>

    <None Update="My Project\Settings.settings">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>SettingsSingleFileGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Settings.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Prevent compiling any stray BuildInfo file from the project root -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
    <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <ReadLinesFromFile File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="$(BuildNumber)" />
  </Target>

  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <ReadLinesFromFile Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''" File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines2" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''">
      <BuildNumber>%(BuildCounterLines2.Identity)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <MakeDir Directories="$([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName('$(BuildInfoFile)'))" />

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="Option Strict On&#xD;&#xA;Option Explicit On&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;Friend Module BuildInfo&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const ProductVersion As String = &quot;$(ProductVersion)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend Const BuildNumber As String = &quot;$(BuildNumber)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;&#xD;&#xA;    Friend ReadOnly Property DisplayVersion As String&#xD;&#xA;        Get&#xD;&#xA;            Return &quot;AMiOffice v&quot; &amp; ProductVersion &amp; &quot; (Build &quot; &amp; BuildNumber &amp; &quot;)&quot;&#xD;&#xA;        End Get&#xD;&#xA;    End Property&#xD;&#xA;End Module&#xD;&#xA;" />

    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>
```

## Then do a real clean rebuild
1) Close VS
2) Delete `...\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\bin` and `...\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\obj`
3) Reopen VS and rebuild

Severity	Code	Description	Project	File	Line	Suppression State	Details
Error (active)	BC30456	'BuildTimestamp' is not a member of 'BuildInfo'.	AMiOffice	C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\FormMainMenu.vb	16

## Copilot

You’re down to **1 real compile error** now; the rest are warnings.

## Fix the error: `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp` doesn’t exist
Your generated `BuildInfo.g.vb` module currently defines only:

- `ProductVersion`
- `BuildNumber`
- `DisplayVersion`

…but your code in `FormMainMenu.vb` line 16 references `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp`.

### Option A (recommended): add `BuildTimestamp` to the generated BuildInfo module
Edit the `GenerateBuildInfo` target in your `.vbproj` where it writes `Lines="...` and add one more constant, e.g.:

```vb
Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = "2026-04-20T12:34:56Z"
```

Because this is generated at build time, use MSBuild’s `System.DateTime`:

**Change your `WriteLinesToFile` “Lines” string to include:**
- `Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = "$(BuildTimestamp)"`

…and define `BuildTimestamp` in the target:

Add this inside `<Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" ...>` before `WriteLinesToFile`:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ"))</BuildTimestamp>
</PropertyGroup>
```

That will satisfy `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp`.

### Option B: remove the reference from code
In `FormMainMenu.vb` line 16, change it to use `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion` (which you already generate) and stop referencing `BuildTimestamp`.

---

## About the WinForms framework warning (NETSDK1086)
You can remove this now:

- `FrameworkReference Include="Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.WindowsForms"`

Because with `TargetFramework=net10.0-windows` and `<UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>`, the SDK already adds it.

So: **delete that FrameworkReference ItemGroup** to silence NETSDK1086.

## About all the CA1416 warnings
These are analyzers complaining “reachable on all platforms”. But your project is **Windows-targeted** (`net10.0-windows`). Two easy ways:

### If you just want them gone (recommended for WinForms apps)
Add to your main `<PropertyGroup>`:

```xml
<EnableNETAnalyzers>false</EnableNETAnalyzers>
```

Or, more targeted:

```xml
<NoWarn>$(NoWarn);CA1416</NoWarn>
```

(Only do this if you’re comfortable ignoring platform-compat warnings.)

---

## Minimal patch summary
1) Add `BuildTimestamp` generation in `GenerateBuildInfo` target (or remove reference in code).
2) Remove the explicit WinForms `FrameworkReference` (optional, to silence warning).
3) Optionally suppress CA1416.

If you paste the current `WriteLinesToFile ... Lines="..."` block, I can show you the exact edited version with `BuildTimestamp` added without breaking your XML encoding.

## @testsubject117

weren't we using buildtimestamp to add a build number that appeared as a date or time stamp previously and I asked to remove that?

## Copilot

Yes. Earlier you had a timestamp-style build identifier, and you asked to remove it in favor of the **publish-only build counter** + `DisplayVersion`. What’s happening now is simply: **the code still references the old field** (`BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp`) even though the generator no longer emits it.

So the correct fix is to **remove the usage** of `BuildTimestamp` (don’t bring it back).

## Do this fix (recommended)

Open `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\FormMainMenu.vb` and at/around **line 16** replace whatever uses `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp` with `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion` (or just `BuildInfo.BuildNumber` if you only want the number).

Examples:

### If you were building a window title like “AMiOffice …”
Change from something like:
```vb
Me.Text = "AMiOffice " & BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp
```

to:
```vb
Me.Text = BuildInfo.DisplayVersion
```

(or)
```vb
Me.Text = $"AMiOffice v{BuildInfo.ProductVersion} (Build {BuildInfo.BuildNumber})"
```

That will clear the **only error**.

## Clean up the warnings you showed

### 1) NETSDK1086 warning
Remove this from the `.vbproj` if you added it:
```xml
<FrameworkReference Include="Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.WindowsForms" />
```
It’s redundant in `net10.0-windows` + `UseWindowsForms=true`.

### 2) CA1416 warnings (optional)
These are analyzers being noisy even though you’re Windows-targeted. Easiest suppress:

In the `.vbproj` `<PropertyGroup>` add:
```xml
<NoWarn>$(NoWarn);CA1416</NoWarn>
```

(Or leave them—they’re warnings, not blockers.)

---

### If you paste the few lines around FormMainMenu.vb line 16
(about 10 lines), I’ll tell you the exact one-line edit to make, matching your intent (no timestamps).

## Copilot

What’s the “however…”—what’s the remaining symptom?

1) Is it a **runtime** problem (app crashes / blank form / closes immediately) or a **build/publish** problem?
2) What exact action triggers it (start debugging / click a menu / open About box / publish)?
3) If there’s an error message or exception, paste the full text (and the Call Stack if you have it).

If it’s the “blank page / blank form” issue again, also tell me which form is supposed to show first and what you actually see.

## @testsubject117

both the main menu window title and the about box now display AMiOffice v1.0 (Build 0)

## Copilot

That means your `BuildInfo.g.vb` is being generated, but `build-counter.txt` is still `0` (or you’re always reading `0`). Since you changed it so the counter only increments on **Publish**, normal builds will keep showing whatever number is currently in `build-counter.txt`.

## Fix it (choose what you want)

### Option A (recommended): keep “increment only on Publish”, but set the initial number
Open (or create) this file:

`C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\build-counter.txt`

Put a number in it, e.g.:

```
1
```

Save, rebuild. Now the UI will show Build 1, and it will only change when you Publish.

### Option B: verify Publish actually increments it
Do a publish once:
- **Build → Publish AMiOffice**
Then check that `build-counter.txt` changed (e.g., to 2).

If it did not change, your `IncrementBuildCounter` target isn’t firing (usually because you’re using a publish method that doesn’t run the `Publish` target you think it does).

### Option C: increment on every build (not just Publish)
Change:
```xml
<Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish">
```
to:
```xml
<Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
```

That will bump the build number every time you build/debug.

---

## Quick confirmation question
Open `build-counter.txt` and tell me what it currently contains (exact contents). If it’s `0`, Option A will immediately fix your display. If it’s not `0`, then `GenerateBuildInfo` is reading the wrong file or the property isn’t being set correctly and I’ll walk you through that next.

## @testsubject117

also, how can I save this entire Visual Basic Project so that all files, .vbproj, .slnx etc. all get saved and backed up so I can have an external version to fall back on if something happens again?

## Copilot

To “save everything” for a Visual Basic project, you don’t rely on Visual Studio’s Save (that only saves edited files). You want a **full-folder backup** plus (ideally) **version control**.

## Best practice (recommended): put the project under Git and push it somewhere
This gives you unlimited rollback and protects you from exactly what happened.

1) In Visual Studio: **Git → Create Git Repository**
2) Choose **Local only** first (or GitHub if you want)
3) Add a `.gitignore` (VS can generate one)
4) Commit.
5) Push to GitHub (private repo is fine) or another remote.

**Important:** do *not* commit build outputs:
- `bin/`
- `obj/`
- `.vs/`

But **do** commit:
- `AMiOffice.vbproj`
- `AMiOffice.slnx`
- all `*.vb`, `*.resx`, images, `build-counter.txt`, etc.

If you want, tell me whether you want GitHub or just local Git and I’ll give the exact click-by-click steps in VS 2026.

## Simple “external backup” (no Git): copy the right folders/files
1) Close Visual Studio (prevents locked files and partial writes).
2) Copy the **project root folder**:
   - `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\`
   to an external drive or a backup folder like:
   - `E:\Backups\AMiOffice\AMiOffice_backup_2026-04-20\`

3) Before copying (or after), delete these to reduce size and avoid stale build junk:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\.vs` (if present)
- `**\bin`
- `**\obj`

This backup method is dumb-but-reliable: if something breaks, you restore the folder.

## “One file” backup: zip it
After closing VS and deleting `bin/obj/.vs`:
- Right-click the `AMiOffice` folder → **Send to → Compressed (zipped) folder**
- Store the zip on an external drive/cloud.

## What NOT to back up
Avoid relying on:
- only `.slnx`
- only `.vbproj`
because the app also needs all the forms/resources/images/settings files.

---

### Quick question
Do you want the fallback to be:
1) **GitHub** (best rollback, easiest long-term), or
2) **a zip copy to an external drive** (simplest)?

Tell me which, and I’ll give you the exact steps for your preferred approach in VS 2026.

## @testsubject117

for the command line publish batch file we created I want to change the output folder to "c:\users\capnkirk\downloads\AM Conversion\VisStudBuilds\Build 14 after recovered from 46 errors" the current contents of the batch file are: dotnet publish "C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj" -c Release /p:PublishProfile=Folder-win-x64

## Copilot

Change your command to pass an explicit publish directory.

Use this (quotes are important because your path has spaces):

```bat
dotnet publish "C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj" ^
  -c Release ^
  /p:PublishProfile=Folder-win-x64 ^
  /p:PublishDir="C:\Users\CapnKirk\Downloads\AM Conversion\VisStudBuilds\Build 14 after recovered from 46 errors\"
```

Notes:
- Keep the trailing `\` on `PublishDir` (MSBuild publish paths behave best with it).
- If you don’t want the `^` line continuations, you can put it all on one line:

```bat
dotnet publish "C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj" -c Release /p:PublishProfile=Folder-win-x64 /p:PublishDir="C:\Users\CapnKirk\Downloads\AM Conversion\VisStudBuilds\Build 14 after recovered from 46 errors\"
```

If your publish profile is also setting an output folder, `PublishDir` on the command line will override it.

## @testsubject117

    C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj(114,7): error MSB4184: The expression "[System.Int32]::Parse('1') + 1" cannot be evaluated.

## Copilot

That error is coming from this line in your `.vbproj`:

```xml
<BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
```

In MSBuild property functions you must use the **fully-qualified** `System.Int32`, not `[System.Int32]`.

## Fix
Change it to:

```xml
<BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
```

Wait—yours already *looks* like that, but your error message shows:

```
"[System.Int32]::Parse('1') + 1"
```

So somewhere it is actually using `[System.Int32]` (square brackets), likely:

```xml
<BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
```

No—MSBuild uses `$([Type]::Method())` syntax; the *problem* is your expression is missing the `$()` wrapper around the addition, or you’re doing `Parse('1') + 1` outside the property function evaluation.

### The safest working form
Replace your increment block with this (this always evaluates correctly):

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)'))</BuildNumber>
  <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Add($(BuildNumber), 1))</BuildNumber>
</PropertyGroup>
```

But MSBuild doesn’t have `Int32.Add` as a static method in that way; better:

**Use one expression with fully qualified type:**
```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
</PropertyGroup>
```

This *should* work. Since you’re getting MSB4184, the actual issue is usually that `%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)` is being treated as an item and the quoting is collapsing weirdly, or the line is not inside a `$()` properly in your real file.

## Deterministic fix: use MSBuild’s `Add` property function instead of `+`
MSBuild supports arithmetic via property functions using `System.Int32` but `+` can be finicky in some contexts. Do this:

Replace the increment with:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)'))</BuildNumber>
  <BuildNumber>$([MSBuild]::Add($(BuildNumber), 1))</BuildNumber>
</PropertyGroup>
```

That avoids `Parse(...) + 1` entirely.

## What to change (exactly where)
In your `IncrementBuildCounter` target, replace:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
</PropertyGroup>
```

with:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)'))</BuildNumber>
  <BuildNumber>$([MSBuild]::Add($(BuildNumber), 1))</BuildNumber>
</PropertyGroup>
```

Then publish again.

---

### One quick check
Open `build-counter.txt` and confirm it contains only a plain number like:

```
1
```

(no extra spaces, no blank line). If there’s an empty line, the parse can also blow up.

## @testsubject117

the main menu window is showing the correct build number however the about dialogue does not, it says (not Set) also, the about dialogue says My at the top, why does it say that? it also says version 0.0.0.0 and file version is 0.0.0.0 those all need to be fixed as well

## Copilot

You have two separate things going on in the About dialog:

1) the fields it’s showing (“(not Set)”, “My”, 0.0.0.0) are coming from the **assembly metadata** / **Application Framework** info, not from your `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion`.
2) your assembly metadata is currently not being populated the way VB’s built-in About dialog expects, because you set `<GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>` and you likely don’t have a proper `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb` providing the attributes.

### Why it says **“My”** at the top
That’s the VB “Application Framework” name/title coming from the `My` application info (often `My.Application.Info.Title` / `AssemblyTitle`). When it can’t find a proper title/product, it falls back to odd defaults like “My”.

### Why it says **(not Set)** and **0.0.0.0**
Those are classic symptoms of missing assembly attributes such as:
- `AssemblyTitle`
- `AssemblyProduct`
- `AssemblyCompany`
- `AssemblyVersion` / `AssemblyFileVersion`

Right now, you set `<AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>` etc. in the project, but because `GenerateAssemblyInfo` is off, those may **not** get emitted into the assembly unless you also provide attributes in `AssemblyInfo.vb`.

---

## Fix approach (recommended): turn assembly info generation back on, and delete/disable old AssemblyInfo.vb
This is the cleanest way in SDK-style .NET projects.

### Step 1: Check if you have this file
`C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb`

- If it exists, it likely contains old attributes and/or duplicates.
- We want **one source of truth**.

### Step 2: In the `.vbproj`, set:
```xml
<GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
```

### Step 3: Remove assembly attributes from `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb` (or exclude the file)
Easiest: rename it to `AssemblyInfo.vb.bak` (so it won’t compile), OR remove the attribute blocks inside it.

### Step 4: Ensure these properties are set in the `.vbproj` (these feed the generated attributes)
Add/verify in the main `<PropertyGroup>`:

```xml
<AssemblyTitle>AMiOffice</AssemblyTitle>
<Product>AMiOffice</Product>
<Company>Kirk Saffell</Company>
<Description>AMiOffice Application</Description>
<AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
<FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
<Version>1.0.0.0</Version>
```

Then clean/rebuild.

That should fix:
- About dialog “Title” (no more “My”)
- “(not Set)” fields
- Version and File Version showing 1.0.0.0 instead of 0.0.0.0

---

## Also: make the About dialog show your **BuildInfo.DisplayVersion**
The built-in VB About box typically shows assembly version/file version, not your custom build counter. If your About form is custom (FormAbout.vb), just set the label text to `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion`.

If your About dialog is the **VB Application Framework About box**, then you don’t directly control the format much; you usually show build numbers via assembly informational version or product version.

### Best option: set InformationalVersion to include build number
Since you already generate a build number, you can set:

- `InformationalVersion` to something like `1.0 (Build 14)`

In your `GenerateBuildInfo` target, after you compute `BuildNumber`, add:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <InformationalVersion>$(ProductVersion) (Build $(BuildNumber))</InformationalVersion>
</PropertyGroup>
```

(Only do this if you want the assembly metadata to reflect the build counter.)

---

## Two quick questions so I can tell you the exact minimal edits
1) Is your About dialog a **custom form** (`FormAbout`) that you open, or the **built-in** VB “About” dialog from Application Framework?
2) Do you currently have `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb`? If yes, paste its contents (or at least the attribute lines at the top).

## @testsubject117

1 is formabout.vb and 2 the answer is no

## Copilot

Since your About dialog is **your own `FormAbout.vb`** and you **do not** have `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb`, the fixes are straightforward:

## A) Fix “My” and the 0.0.0.0 Version/FileVersion
Right now you have `<GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>`. With no `AssemblyInfo.vb`, that leaves you with **no assembly attributes**, which is why About shows defaults like **My** and **0.0.0.0**.

### Change in `AMiOffice.vbproj`
Set this back to **true**:

```xml
<GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
```

Keep your existing:
```xml
<AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
<FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
<Version>1.0.0.0</Version>
<Title>AMiOfficeApplication</Title>
<Copyright>...</Copyright>
```

Also add these (these help About info look right):

```xml
<AssemblyTitle>AMiOffice</AssemblyTitle>
<Product>AMiOffice</Product>
```

(You can keep your `<Title>` too, but `AssemblyTitle` is the one the UI typically uses.)

Then **Clean/Rebuild**.

That should eliminate:
- “My” at the top
- Version 0.0.0.0 / File version 0.0.0.0

## B) Fix “(not Set)” in FormAbout
That text is coming from your **FormAbout code**, not MSBuild. You’re probably doing something like:

- `My.Application.Info.Description` (which is empty) or
- `My.Application.Info.ProductName` (empty), etc.

### Quick, reliable approach: display your BuildInfo + assembly versions explicitly
In `FormAbout_Load`, set your labels like this:

```vb
lblTitle.Text = "AMiOffice"
lblDisplayVersion.Text = BuildInfo.DisplayVersion
lblAssemblyVersion.Text = My.Application.Info.Version.ToString()
```

If you want file version too, use:

```vb
Dim fvi = FileVersionInfo.GetVersionInfo(Application.ExecutablePath)
lblFileVersion.Text = fvi.FileVersion
lblProductVersion.Text = fvi.ProductVersion
```

And if you currently show “(not Set)” for something, change it to fall back to a sane default, e.g.:

```vb
lblDescription.Text =
    If(String.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(My.Application.Info.Description),
       "AMiOffice Application",
       My.Application.Info.Description)
```

## Tell me exactly what to change in FormAbout

## Copilot

Use this as a complete replacement for `FormAbout.vb`. It forces the title to **AMiOffice**, shows your **BuildInfo.DisplayVersion**, and pulls **AssemblyVersion / FileVersion / ProductVersion** from the built executable so you won’t get `(not Set)` or `0.0.0.0` once assembly info generation is enabled.

Also: this assumes you have labels named (at least) `lblTitle`, `lblDisplayVersion`, `lblAssemblyVersion`. For the rest, I’ve used common names—if yours differ, either rename your labels to match or tell me your actual label names and I’ll adjust.

```vbnet name=FormAbout.vb
Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Imports System.Diagnostics
Imports System.Reflection
Imports System.Windows.Forms

Public Class FormAbout

    Private Sub FormAbout_Load(sender As Object, e As EventArgs) Handles MyBase.Load
        ' Always show a stable app title (avoid "My")
        lblTitle.Text = "AMiOffice"

        ' Your custom build display (e.g. "AMiOffice v1.0 (Build 14)")
        lblDisplayVersion.Text = SafeText(BuildInfo.DisplayVersion, "AMiOffice")

        ' Assembly version (from VB Application Framework)
        lblAssemblyVersion.Text = My.Application.Info.Version.ToString()

        ' File/Product version come from the built EXE metadata
        Dim exePath As String = Application.ExecutablePath
        Dim fvi As FileVersionInfo = FileVersionInfo.GetVersionInfo(exePath)

        ' If you have these labels, populate them; otherwise remove these lines.
        If ControlExists("lblFileVersion") Then
            DirectCast(Controls.Find("lblFileVersion", True)(0), Label).Text =
                SafeText(fvi.FileVersion, My.Application.Info.Version.ToString())
        End If

        If ControlExists("lblProductVersion") Then
            DirectCast(Controls.Find("lblProductVersion", True)(0), Label).Text =
                SafeText(fvi.ProductVersion, BuildInfo.ProductVersion)
        End If

        If ControlExists("lblProductName") Then
            DirectCast(Controls.Find("lblProductName", True)(0), Label).Text =
                SafeText(fvi.ProductName, "AMiOffice")
        End If

        If ControlExists("lblCompanyName") Then
            DirectCast(Controls.Find("lblCompanyName", True)(0), Label).Text =
                SafeText(fvi.CompanyName, "")
        End If

        If ControlExists("lblCopyright") Then
            DirectCast(Controls.Find("lblCopyright", True)(0), Label).Text =
                SafeText(fvi.LegalCopyright, "")
        End If
    End Sub

    Private Shared Function SafeText(value As String, fallback As String) As String
        If String.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(value) Then
            Return fallback
        End If
        Return value
    End Function

    Private Function ControlExists(controlName As String) As Boolean
        Dim matches = Me.Controls.Find(controlName, True)
        Return matches IsNot Nothing AndAlso matches.Length > 0
    End Function

End Class
```

### Two required project settings (otherwise versions may still show 0.0.0.0)
In `AMiOffice.vbproj`, make sure you have:

```xml
<GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
<AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
<FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
<Version>1.0.0.0</Version>
<Product>AMiOffice</Product>
<AssemblyTitle>AMiOffice</AssemblyTitle>
```

### I need one detail to make this “perfect”
What are the **actual label control names** on your About form designer? If you paste `FormAbout.Designer.vb` (or just the `Friend WithEvents lbl...` lines), I’ll tailor the replacement so it sets exactly the labels you have, with no `ControlExists` fallback code.

## Copilot

Replace your entire `FormAbout.vb` with the following. It uses your actual control names (`lblAppName`, `rtbInfo`, etc.), shows the same build display as the main menu (`BuildInfo.DisplayVersion`), and fills in assembly/file/product versions from the built EXE metadata. It also avoids ever showing “(not Set)”.

```vbnet name=FormAbout.vb
Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Imports System.Diagnostics
Imports System.Reflection
Imports System.Text

Public Class FormAbout

    Private Sub FormAbout_Load(sender As Object, e As EventArgs) Handles MyBase.Load
        ' Title bar text
        Me.Text = "About AMiOffice"

        ' Big red app name line (your designer label)
        lblAppName.Text = "AMiOffice"

        ' Optional: make the "About:" header include the build display
        lblAbout.Text = "About: " & BuildInfo.DisplayVersion

        ' Show detailed info in the rich text box
        rtbInfo.Clear()
        rtbInfo.SelectionAlignment = HorizontalAlignment.Left
        rtbInfo.Font = New Font("Consolas", 9.75F, FontStyle.Regular)

        Dim exePath As String = Application.ExecutablePath
        Dim fvi As FileVersionInfo = FileVersionInfo.GetVersionInfo(exePath)

        Dim sb As New StringBuilder()

        sb.AppendLine(BuildInfo.DisplayVersion)
        sb.AppendLine()

        sb.AppendLine($"Assembly Version : {My.Application.Info.Version}")
        sb.AppendLine($"File Version     : {Safe(fvi.FileVersion)}")
        sb.AppendLine($"Product Version  : {Safe(fvi.ProductVersion)}")
        sb.AppendLine()

        sb.AppendLine($"Product Name     : {Safe(fvi.ProductName, "AMiOffice")}")
        sb.AppendLine($"Company          : {Safe(fvi.CompanyName)}")
        sb.AppendLine($"Copyright        : {Safe(fvi.LegalCopyright)}")
        sb.AppendLine()

        sb.AppendLine($"Executable Path  : {exePath}")

        rtbInfo.Text = sb.ToString()

        ' If you want a nicer "Developed By" line, set it here:
        ' (Change the name to whatever you want displayed.)
        lblDevelopedBy.Text = "Developed By: Kirk Saffell"

        ' If you want to show an embedded image in picDeveloper, do it here.
        ' (Only if you have one available. Otherwise leave it blank.)
        ' picDeveloper.Image = My.Resources.YourImageName
    End Sub

    Private Shared Function Safe(value As String, Optional fallback As String = "") As String
        If String.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(value) Then
            If String.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(fallback) Then
                Return "(not set)"
            End If
            Return fallback
        End If
        Return value.Trim()
    End Function

End Class
```

### Important: fix the “0.0.0.0” at the source (project file)
For the About box to show real File/Product versions, make sure your project is generating assembly info. In `AMiOffice.vbproj` set:

```xml
<GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
```

…and keep your existing version properties:

```xml
<AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
<FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
<Version>1.0.0.0</Version>
```

Optionally add:

```xml
<Product>AMiOffice</Product>
<AssemblyTitle>AMiOffice</AssemblyTitle>
```

### Why you previously saw “My”
That was coming from using `My.Application.Info.*` fields (title/product/description) when assembly metadata wasn’t being generated (because `GenerateAssemblyInfo` was false and you had no `AssemblyInfo.vb`). This replacement avoids relying on those fields for the visible app name.

If you paste your current `AMiOffice.vbproj` version/GenerateAssemblyInfo section (just that part), I can tell you exactly what to set so FileVersion/ProductVersion display exactly what you want.

<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <InformationalVersion>$(ProductVersion) (Build $(BuildNumber))</InformationalVersion>
    <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>
    <EnableNETAnalyzers>false</EnableNETAnalyzers>

    <AssemblyTitle>AMiOffice</AssemblyTitle>
    <Product>AMiOffice</Product>

    <FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>

    <GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
    <InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>

    <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\Generated\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>

    <Copyright>Copyright Kirk Saffell 2026</Copyright>
    <Title>AMiOfficeApplication</Title>
    <ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Force WinForms framework reference (fixes 'Label/Panel/Timer not defined') -->

  <ItemGroup>
    <Import Include="System" />
    <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
    <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Hard-stop: never compile stray repo-root folders -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="..\My Project\**\*.vb" />
    <Compile Remove="..\obj\**\*.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Remove="ESC Logo.jpg" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
